q:
The development team at a social media company wants to handle some complicated queries such as "What are the number of likes on the videos that have been posted by friends of a user A?".
As a solutions architect, which of the following AWS database services would you suggest as the BEST fit to handle such use cases?
options: Amazon OpenSearch Service
Amazon Neptune
Amazon Redshift
Amazon Aurora
explain: Amazon Neptune is the correct choice for social graph queries like 'likes on videos posted by friends of user A' because it is a purpose-built, fully managed graph database engine optimized for storing billions of relationships and querying highly connected datasets with milliseconds latency. Neptune supports graph query languages like Gremlin and SPARQL, making traversals across friend networks natural and efficient. Amazon Redshift is an analytics data warehouse optimized for column-oriented SQL queries on large datasets, not relationship traversals. Aurora and OpenSearch also lack native graph traversal capabilities.

q:
A financial services company is looking to move its on-premises IT infrastructure to AWS Cloud. The company has multiple long-term server bound licenses across the application stack and the CTO wants to continue to utilize those licenses while moving to AWS.
As a solutions architect, which of the following would you recommend as the MOST cost-effective solution?
options: Use Amazon EC2 dedicated instances
Use Amazon EC2 dedicated hosts
Use Amazon EC2 reserved instances (RI)
Use Amazon EC2 on-demand instances
explain: Amazon EC2 Dedicated Hosts are the correct solution when you need to use existing per-socket, per-core, or per-VM software licenses (BYOL) because Dedicated Hosts provide visibility into the physical socket and core count required by many enterprise license agreements (Microsoft, Oracle). Unlike Dedicated Instances, which simply isolate your instance on dedicated hardware without licensing visibility, Dedicated Hosts provide the specific host details (CPU sockets, cores) required to comply with software vendor licensing terms. Reserved Instances and On-Demand instances run on shared physical hardware with no license compliance controls, making them unsuitable for strict BYOL scenarios.

q:
You have been hired as a Solutions Architect to advise a company on the various authentication/authorization mechanisms that AWS offers to authorize an API call within the Amazon API Gateway. The company would prefer a solution that offers built-in user management.
Which of the following solutions would you suggest as the best fit for the given use-case?
options: Use AWS Lambda authorizer for Amazon API Gateway
Use Amazon Cognito Identity Pools
Use AWS_IAM authorization
Use Amazon Cognito User Pools
explain: An AWS Lambda authorizer (formerly Custom Authorizer) is the correct solution when you need to use a custom authorization mechanism or a third-party OAuth/OIDC identity provider with Amazon API Gateway. The Lambda function receives the caller's identity in the incoming request and returns an IAM policy, giving you full control over custom authentication logic. Amazon Cognito User Pools is better suited for built-in user directory management, while Cognito Identity Pools handle federating identities to AWS credentials. Lambda authorizers are the preferred option when integrating with existing enterprise identity systems or custom token-based auth.

q:
An e-commerce application uses an Amazon Aurora Multi-AZ deployment for its database. While analyzing the performance metrics, the engineering team has found that the database reads are causing high input/output (I/O) and adding latency to the write requests against the database.
As an AWS Certified Solutions Architect Associate, what would you recommend to separate the read requests from the write requests?
options: Activate read-through caching on the Amazon Aurora database
Set up a read replica and modify the application to use the appropriate endpoint
Configure the application to read from the Multi-AZ standby instance
Provision another Amazon Aurora database and link it to the primary database as a read replica
explain: Setting up an Aurora Read Replica and directing read traffic to the reader endpoint is the correct approach to offload analytics queries from the primary writer instance. Aurora Read Replicas share the same underlying storage as the primary, so replication lag is minimal (typically under 100ms). Configuring the application to read from the Multi-AZ standby is incorrect—the standby in RDS/Aurora Multi-AZ is not accessible for reads and exists solely for automatic failover. Read-through caching does not exist as a configurable Aurora feature.

q:
An Electronic Design Automation (EDA) application produces massive volumes of data that can be divided into two categories. The 'hot data' needs to be both processed and stored quickly in a parallel and distributed fashion. The 'cold data' needs to be kept for reference with quick access for reads and updates at a low cost.
Which of the following AWS services is BEST suited to accelerate the aforementioned chip design process?
options: Amazon FSx for Lustre
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
AWS Glue
Amazon EMR
explain: Amazon FSx for Lustre is the optimal choice for the 'hot data' tier of an EDA application because it provides a high-performance POSIX-compliant parallel file system with sub-millisecond latency and hundreds of GB/s of throughput, natively integrated with Amazon S3 for cold data. FSx for Lustre is purpose-built for compute-intensive workloads including EDA, genomics, and financial simulation. AWS Glue is an ETL service for data cataloging and transformation, not a high-performance storage layer. Amazon EMR is a managed big data processing platform, not a storage tier.

q:
A development team has deployed a microservice to the Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS). The application layer is in a Docker container that provides both static and dynamic content through an Application Load Balancer. With increasing load, the Amazon ECS cluster is experiencing higher network usage. The development team has looked into the network usage and found that 90% of it is due to distributing static content of the application.
As a Solutions Architect, what do you recommend
options: Distribute the dynamic content through Amazon S3
Distribute the static content through Amazon EFS
Distribute the dynamic content through Amazon EFS
Distribute the static content through Amazon S3
explain: Distributing static content through Amazon S3 is correct because S3 is purpose-built for storing and serving static assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images) at scale with high durability and low cost, often paired with CloudFront for global edge caching. Dynamic content generated by the ECS microservice should be distributed through Amazon EFS, which provides a shared, POSIX-compliant file system accessible by multiple containers simultaneously. Serving dynamic content through S3 is incorrect because S3 stores objects rather than hosting dynamic application logic. Serving static content through EFS would add unnecessary complexity and cost compared to S3.

q:
A Big Data processing company has created a distributed data processing framework that performs best if the network performance between the processing machines is high. The application has to be deployed on AWS, and the company is only looking at performance as the key measure.
As a Solutions Architect, which deployment do you recommend?
options: Use Spot Instances
Use a Spread placement group
Use a Cluster placement group
Optimize the Amazon EC2 kernel using EC2 User Data
explain: A Cluster placement group is the correct placement strategy for distributed data processing workloads that require high inter-node network performance because it packs instances closely together within a single Availability Zone, enabling the low-latency, high-bandwidth network connections (up to 100 Gbps enhanced networking) that HPC and distributed processing frameworks require. A Spread placement group distributes instances across separate physical hardware to maximize fault tolerance—the opposite of what's needed here. Spot Instances address cost optimization, not network performance. EC2 User Data scripts run at launch time and cannot tune the underlying network kernel for placement-level performance.

q:
The infrastructure team at a company maintains 5 different VPCs (let's call these VPCs A, B, C, D, E) for resource isolation. Due to the changed organizational structure, the team wants to interconnect all VPCs together. To facilitate this, the team has set up VPC peering connection between VPC A and all other VPCs in a hub and spoke model with VPC A at the center. However, the team has still failed to establish connectivity between all VPCs.
As a solutions architect, which of the following woul
options: Establish VPC peering connections between all VPCs
Use AWS transit gateway to interconnect the VPCs
Use an internet gateway to interconnect the VPCs
Use a VPC endpoint to interconnect the VPCs
explain: AWS Transit Gateway is the correct solution for interconnecting multiple VPCs in a hub-and-spoke (star) topology because it acts as a central network transit hub, allowing any-to-any connectivity without the O(n²) complexity of VPC peering. Transit Gateway supports up to 5,000 VPC attachments and simplifies routing management with a single gateway. VPC Peering requires a separate peering connection between each pair of VPCs—with 5 VPCs, that means 10 peering connections, each requiring individual route table updates and no transitive routing support. Internet Gateways and VPC endpoints are not designed for VPC-to-VPC connectivity.

q:
A retail company has developed a REST API which is deployed in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer. The REST API stores the user data in Amazon DynamoDB and any static content, such as images, are served via Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3). On analyzing the usage trends, it is found that 90% of the read requests are for commonly accessed data across all users.
As a Solutions Architect, which of the following would you suggest as the MOST efficient solution to impr
options: Enable ElastiCache Redis for DynamoDB and Amazon CloudFront for Amazon S3
Enable ElastiCache Redis for DynamoDB and ElastiCache Memcached for Amazon S3
Enable Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for Amazon DynamoDB and ElastiCache Memcached for Amazon S3
Enable Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for Amazon DynamoDB and Amazon CloudFront for Amazon S3
explain: Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is the correct caching layer for DynamoDB because it is a fully managed, in-memory cache purpose-built for DynamoDB that delivers up to 10x read performance improvement with microsecond latency, without requiring application code changes to the DynamoDB API. Amazon CloudFront is correct for S3 because it caches static content at 450+ global edge locations, dramatically reducing latency for end users. ElastiCache Redis is a general-purpose cache and does not provide the native DynamoDB API compatibility that DAX offers. ElastiCache Memcached lacks CloudFront's global edge network for S3 content.

q:
The engineering team at an e-commerce company is working on cost optimizations for Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instances. The team wants to manage the workload using a mix of on-demand and spot instances across multiple instance types. They would like to create an Auto Scaling group with a mix of these instances.
Which of the following options would allow the engineering team to provision the instances for this use-case?
options: You can use a launch configuration or a launch template to provision capacity across multiple instance types using both On-Demand Instances and Spot Instances to achieve the desired scale, performance, and cost
You can only use a launch configuration to provision capacity across multiple instance types using both On-Demand Instances and Spot Instances to achieve the desired scale, performance, and cost

You can neither use a launch configuration nor a launch template to provision capacity across multiple instance types using both On-Demand Instances and Spot Instances to achieve the desired scale, performance, and cost
You can only use a launch template to provision capacity across multiple instance types using both On-Demand Instances and Spot Instances to achieve the desired scale, performance, and cost
explain: AWS Auto Scaling launch templates (not launch configurations) support the mixed instances policy feature, which enables provisioning capacity across multiple EC2 instance types using a combination of On-Demand and Spot Instances in a single Auto Scaling group. This is critical for cost optimization at scale—Spot Instances can reduce compute costs by up to 90% when combined with On-Demand as a base. Launch configurations are a legacy feature that does not support mixed instance type policies or the newer EC2 instance purchasing options. This is a key distinction on the SAA-C03 exam: any question about multiple instance types + Spot in the same ASG requires a launch template.

q:
A medium-sized business has a taxi dispatch application deployed on an Amazon EC2 instance. Because of an unknown bug, the application causes the instance to freeze regularly. Then, the instance has to be manually restarted via the AWS management console.
Which of the following is the MOST cost-optimal and resource-efficient way to implement an automated solution until a permanent fix is delivered by the development team?
options: Setup an Amazon CloudWatch alarm to monitor the health status of the instance. In case of an Instance Health Check failure, Amazon CloudWatch Alarm can publish to an Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) event which can then trigger an AWS lambda function. The AWS lambda function can use Amazon EC2 API to reboot the instance

Use Amazon EventBridge events to trigger an AWS Lambda function to check the instance status every 5 minutes. In the case of Instance Health Check failure, the AWS lambda function can use Amazon EC2 API to reboot the instance
Setup an Amazon CloudWatch alarm to monitor the health status of the instance. In case of an Instance Health Check failure, an EC2 Reboot CloudWatch Alarm Action can be used to reboot the instance
Use Amazon EventBridge events to trigger an AWS Lambda function to reboot the instance status every 5 minutes
explain: Setting up a CloudWatch alarm with an EC2 Reboot Alarm Action is the simplest and most operationally efficient approach because it uses a built-in CloudWatch action that directly reboots the instance when the Instance Status Check fails, without requiring custom Lambda code. The Amazon EC2 Reboot CloudWatch Alarm Action is specifically designed for this use case and requires no additional infrastructure. Using an SNS-triggered Lambda function (option A) adds unnecessary complexity when a native alarm action exists. EventBridge polling every 5 minutes would introduce delays of up to 5 minutes before detecting the failure.

q:
A retail company uses Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instances, Amazon API Gateway, Amazon RDS, Elastic Load Balancer and Amazon CloudFront services. To improve the security of these services, the Risk Advisory group has suggested a feasibility check for using the Amazon GuardDuty service.
Which of the following would you identify as data sources supported by Amazon GuardDuty?
options: Amazon CloudFront logs, Amazon API Gateway logs, AWS CloudTrail events
VPC Flow Logs, Amazon API Gateway logs, Amazon S3 access logs
Elastic Load Balancing logs, Domain Name System (DNS) logs, AWS CloudTrail events
VPC Flow Logs, Domain Name System (DNS) logs, AWS CloudTrail events
explain: Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes three primary data sources: VPC Flow Logs (to detect unusual network traffic patterns), DNS query logs (to detect communication with known malicious domains), and AWS CloudTrail events (to detect unauthorized API activity or configuration changes). CloudFront logs and API Gateway logs are not native GuardDuty data sources. S3 access logs, ELB logs, and API Gateway logs provide operational insights but are not used by GuardDuty's ML-based threat detection engine. Enabling GuardDuty in all regions is a best practice to maintain threat coverage across the organization's entire AWS footprint.

q:
A retail company wants to rollout and test a blue-green deployment for its global application in the next 48 hours. Most of the customers use mobile phones which are prone to Domain Name System (DNS) caching. The company has only two days left for the annual Thanksgiving sale to commence.
As a Solutions Architect, which of the following options would you recommend to test the deployment on as many users as possible in the given time frame?
options: Use AWS CodeDeploy deployment options to choose the right deployment
Use AWS Global Accelerator to distribute a portion of traffic to a particular deployment
Use Amazon Route 53 weighted routing to spread traffic across different deployments
Use Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) to distribute traffic across deployments
explain: Blue/green deployment is a technique for releasing applications by shifting traffic between two identical environments running different versions of the application: "Blue" is the currently running version and "green" the new version. This type of deployment allows you to test features in the green environment without impacting the currently running version of your application. When you’re satisfied that the green version is working properly, you can gradually reroute the traffic from the old blue environment to the new green environment.

q:
A media company wants a low-latency way to distribute live sports results which are delivered via a proprietary application using UDP protocol.
As a solutions architect, which of the following solutions would you recommend such that it offers the BEST performance for this use case?
options: Use AWS Global Accelerator to provide a low latency way to distribute live sports results
Use Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) to provide a low latency way to distribute live sports results
Use Auto Scaling group to provide a low latency way to distribute live sports results
Use Amazon CloudFront to provide a low latency way to distribute live sports results
explain: AWS Global Accelerator is the correct solution for low-latency delivery of dynamic live sports results because it routes user traffic through AWS's global network backbone using anycast IP addresses, reducing latency by bypassing congested public internet segments and directing traffic to the optimal AWS edge location. Unlike Amazon CloudFront, which is optimized for static and cacheable content at the edge, Global Accelerator is designed for dynamic, non-cacheable content requiring consistent low latency. Elastic Load Balancing operates within a single region and cannot reduce cross-regional latency. Auto Scaling groups address compute capacity, not network latency.

q:
A company has hired you as an AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Associate to help with redesigning a real-time data processor. The company wants to build custom applications that process and analyze the streaming data for its specialized needs.
Which solution will you recommend to address this use-case?
options: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose to process the data streams as well as decouple the producers and consumers for the real-time data processor
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to process the data streams as well as decouple the producers and consumers for the real-time data processor
Use Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) to process the data streams as well as decouple the producers and consumers for the real-time data processor
Use Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) to process the data streams as well as decouple the producers and consumers for the real-time data processor
explain: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is the correct solution for real-time data processing with producer-consumer decoupling because it supports high-throughput data ingestion (thousands of records per second), persistent storage of data records for up to 365 days enabling replay, and multiple independent consumers reading from the same stream at different positions. Kinesis Data Firehose is a delivery service for loading data into destinations like S3 and Redshift—it does not support the consumer group pattern needed for decoupled real-time processing. Amazon SQS and SNS support message queuing and fan-out but lack the ordered, replayable stream semantics needed for real-time analytics.

q:
A cyber security company is running a mission critical application using a single Spread placement group of Amazon EC2 instances. The company needs 15 Amazon EC2 instances for optimal performance.
How many Availability Zones (AZs) will the company need to deploy these Amazon EC2 instances per the given use-case?
explain: A Spread placement group can have a maximum of 7 running instances per Availability Zone per group. Since the mission-critical application is running in a single spread placement group, the maximum number of EC2 instances supported is 7. This limit exists because each instance in a spread placement group is placed on distinct underlying hardware (different racks), and there are only a limited number of distinct physical racks available per AZ. Cluster placement groups have no such instance count limit but sacrifice fault isolation for network performance.

q:
A financial services company recently launched an initiative to improve the security of its AWS resources and it had enabled AWS Shield Advanced across multiple AWS accounts owned by the company. Upon analysis, the company has found that the costs incurred are much higher than expected.
Which of the following would you attribute as the underlying reason for the unexpectedly high costs for AWS Shield Advanced service?
options: Savings Plans has not been enabled for the AWS Shield Advanced service across all the AWS accounts
Consolidated billing has not been enabled. All the AWS accounts should fall under a single consolidated billing for the monthly fee to be charged only once
AWS Shield Advanced is being used for custom servers, that are not part of AWS Cloud, thereby resulting in increased costs
AWS Shield Advanced also covers AWS Shield Standard plan, thereby resulting in increased costs
explain: The root cause is that consolidated billing has not been enabled—AWS Shield Advanced requires all protected AWS accounts to be part of a single AWS Organizations consolidated billing family to receive cost protection benefits and coverage across accounts. Without consolidated billing, DDoS-related cost protection (the data transfer out cost protection feature) does not apply across accounts. Savings Plans are irrelevant to AWS Shield Advanced. Shield Advanced does not cover custom on-premises servers outside AWS infrastructure. Shield Advanced does not extend Shield Standard pricing—they are distinct service tiers.

q:
A developer needs to implement an AWS Lambda function in AWS account A that accesses an Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) bucket in AWS account B.
As a Solutions Architect, which of the following will you recommend to meet this requirement?
options: AWS Lambda cannot access resources across AWS accounts. Use Identity federation to work around this limitation of Lambda

Create an IAM role for the AWS Lambda function that grants access to the Amazon S3 bucket. Set the IAM role as the AWS Lambda function's execution role. Make sure that the bucket policy also grants access to the AWS Lambda function's execution role
The Amazon S3 bucket owner should make the bucket public so that it can be accessed by the AWS Lambda function in the other AWS account

Create an IAM role for the AWS Lambda function that grants access to the Amazon S3 bucket. Set the IAM role as the Lambda function's execution role and that would give the AWS Lambda function cross-account access to the Amazon S3 bucket
explain: For cross-account Amazon S3 access, you must create an IAM execution role for the Lambda function in account A that grants the necessary S3 permissions, then add a bucket policy in account B explicitly granting access to that Lambda execution role. This two-step approach—IAM role on the Lambda side plus bucket policy on the S3 side—is required for cross-account resource access. Making the S3 bucket public is a severe security anti-pattern that exposes the bucket to the entire internet. Lambda cannot access cross-account resources without explicit cross-account IAM policies.

q:
A financial services firm uses a high-frequency trading system and wants to write the log files into Amazon S3. The system will also read these log files in parallel on a near real-time basis. The engineering team wants to address any data discrepancies that might arise when the trading system overwrites an existing log file and then tries to read that specific log file.
Which of the following options BEST describes the capabilities of Amazon S3 relevant to this scenario?
options: A process replaces an existing object and immediately tries to read it. Amazon S3 always returns the latest version of the object
A process replaces an existing object and immediately tries to read it. Until the change is fully propagated, Amazon S3 does not return any data
A process replaces an existing object and immediately tries to read it. Until the change is fully propagated, Amazon S3 might return the previous data
A process replaces an existing object and immediately tries to read it. Until the change is fully propagated, Amazon S3 might return the new data
explain:
A process replaces an existing object and immediately tries to read it. Amazon S3 always returns the latest version of the object
Amazon S3 delivers strong read-after-write consistency automatically, without changes to performance or availability, without sacrificing regional isolation for applications, and at no additional cost.
After a successful write of a new object or an overwrite of an existing object, any subsequent read request immediately receives the latest version of the object.

q:
A financial services company wants to identify any sensitive data stored on its Amazon S3 buckets. The company also wants to monitor and protect all data stored on Amazon S3 against any malicious activity.
As a solutions architect, which of the following solutions would you recommend to help address the given requirements?
options: Use Amazon GuardDuty to monitor any malicious activity on data stored in Amazon S3 as well as to identify any sensitive data stored on Amazon S3
Use Amazon Macie to monitor any malicious activity on data stored in Amazon S3. Use Amazon GuardDuty to identify any sensitive data stored on Amazon S3
Use Amazon Macie to monitor any malicious activity on data stored in Amazon S3 as well as to identify any sensitive data stored on Amazon S3
Use Amazon GuardDuty to monitor any malicious activity on data stored in Amazon S3. Use Amazon Macie to identify any sensitive data stored on Amazon S3
explain:
Use Amazon GuardDuty to monitor any malicious activity on data stored in Amazon S3. Use Amazon Macie to identify any sensitive data stored on Amazon S3
Amazon GuardDuty offers threat detection that enables you to continuously monitor and protect your AWS accounts, workloads, and data stored in Amazon S3. GuardDuty analyzes continuous streams of meta-data generated from your account and network activity found in AWS CloudTrail Events, Amazon VPC Flow Logs, and DNS Logs.

q:
An organization wants to delegate access to a set of users from the development environment so that they can access some resources in the production environment which is managed under another AWS account.
As a solutions architect, which of the following steps would you recommend?
options: Create a new IAM role with the required permissions to access the resources in the production environment. The users can then assume this IAM role while accessing the resources from the production environment
It is not possible to access cross-account resources
Create new IAM user credentials for the production environment and share these credentials with the set of users from the development environment
Both IAM roles and IAM users can be used interchangeably for cross-account access
explain: Cross-account access in AWS is achieved by creating an IAM role in the production account with the required permissions, then granting the development users permission to assume (sts:AssumeRole) that role. This is the standard AWS approach for delegating access across accounts without sharing long-term credentials. Stating that cross-account access is impossible is incorrect—AWS IAM roles are specifically designed to enable this pattern. Creating separate IAM user credentials for the production environment and sharing them violates least-privilege and key rotation best practices. IAM roles and IAM users are NOT interchangeable for cross-account access—only roles support trust policies for cross-account assumption.

q:
An IT company wants to review its security best-practices after an incident was reported where a new developer on the team was assigned full access to Amazon DynamoDB. The developer accidentally deleted a couple of tables from the production environment while building out a new feature.
Which is the MOST effective way to address this issue so that such incidents do not recur?
options: Remove full database access for all IAM users in the organization
Only root user should have full database access in the organization
The CTO should review the permissions for each new developer's IAM user so that such incidents don't recur
Use permissions boundary to control the maximum permissions employees can grant to the IAM principals
explain: AWS IAM permissions boundaries are the correct control mechanism to prevent developers from granting themselves or others more permissions than intended. A permissions boundary sets the maximum permissions that an IAM entity can have, regardless of identity-based policies attached to it—so even if a developer attaches a broad policy, the boundary limits the effective permissions. Removing all database access would prevent legitimate developers from working. Having only the root user with database access is a security anti-pattern. Manual CTO review is not scalable and is a reactive rather than preventive control.

q:
A retail company maintains an AWS Direct Connect connection to AWS and has recently migrated its data warehouse to AWS. The data analysts at the company query the data warehouse using a visualization tool. The average size of a query returned by the data warehouse is 60 megabytes and the query responses returned by the data warehouse are not cached in the visualization tool. Each webpage returned by the visualization tool is approximately 600 kilobytes.
Which of the following options offers the
options: Deploy the visualization tool on-premises. Query the data warehouse directly over an AWS Direct Connect connection at a location in the same AWS region
Deploy the visualization tool in the same AWS region as the data warehouse. Access the visualization tool over the internet at a location in the same region
Deploy the visualization tool in the same AWS region as the data warehouse. Access the visualization tool over a Direct Connect connection at a location in the same region
Deploy the visualization tool on-premises. Query the data warehouse over the internet at a location in the same AWS region
explain: Deploying the visualization tool in the same AWS region as the data warehouse and accessing it over a Direct Connect connection eliminates internet latency and bandwidth costs while leveraging the company's existing Direct Connect investment. Since the company already has a Direct Connect connection, placing the visualization tool in AWS and routing traffic through Direct Connect avoids the variable latency of internet connections, which is critical for a data warehouse visualization use case. Accessing the data warehouse over the internet introduces unpredictable latency and egress costs. Deploying on-premises and querying AWS over Direct Connect would require moving large result sets across the connection.

q:
An Internet of Things (IoT) company would like to have a streaming system that performs real-time analytics on the ingested IoT data. Once the analytics is done, the company would like to send notifications back to the mobile applications of the IoT device owners.
As a solutions architect, which of the following AWS technologies would you recommend to send these notifications to the mobile applications?
options: Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) with Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS)
Amazon Kinesis with Amazon Simple Email Service (Amazon SES)
Amazon Kinesis with Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS)
Amazon Kinesis with Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS)
explain: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is the correct choice for real-time IoT data ingestion and analytics because it is designed for high-throughput, low-latency streaming with the ability to replay data and support multiple consumers. Amazon SNS enables fan-out delivery of processed results to multiple downstream subscribers (email, SMS, Lambda, SQS) simultaneously, making the Kinesis + SNS combination ideal for real-time analytics with multiple output channels. SQS with SNS is a messaging pattern but lacks the ordered, replayable stream capabilities needed for time-series IoT analytics. Amazon SES is an email service and is inappropriate for real-time stream processing outputs.

q:
A mobile gaming company is experiencing heavy read traffic to its Amazon Relational Database Service (Amazon RDS) database that retrieves player’s scores and stats. The company is using an Amazon RDS database instance type that is not cost-effective for their budget. The company would like to implement a strategy to deal with the high volume of read traffic, reduce latency, and also downsize the instance size to cut costs.
Which of the following solutions do you recommend?
options: Switch application code to AWS Lambda for better performance
Setup Amazon ElastiCache in front of Amazon RDS
Move to Amazon Redshift
Setup Amazon RDS Read Replicas
explain: Amazon ElastiCache is the optimal caching layer in front of Amazon RDS for heavy read traffic because it serves frequently accessed data from in-memory storage at microsecond latency, dramatically reducing the number of database reads that hit RDS and lowering both latency and database load. ElastiCache supports Redis and Memcached, both capable of caching complex database query results and session data. Amazon RDS Read Replicas can also reduce read traffic but still require disk I/O and connection management—they are better for analytical workloads, not high-frequency cache hits. Migrating to Redshift is inappropriate as it's an OLAP data warehouse, not a caching or OLTP solution.

q:
A company has noticed that its application performance has deteriorated after a new Auto Scaling group was deployed a few days back. Upon investigation, the team found out that the Launch Configuration selected for the Auto Scaling group is using the incorrect instance type that is not optimized to handle the application workflow.
As a solutions architect, what would you recommend to provide a long term resolution for this issue?
options: No need to modify the launch configuration. Just modify the Auto Scaling group to use more number of existing instance types. More instances may offset the loss of performance
Create a new launch configuration to use the correct instance type. Modify the Auto Scaling group to use this new launch configuration. Delete the old launch configuration as it is no longer needed
No need to modify the launch configuration. Just modify the Auto Scaling group to use the correct instance type
Modify the launch configuration to use the correct instance type and continue to use the existing Auto Scaling group
explain: When you need to change the EC2 instance type in an Auto Scaling group, you must create a new launch configuration (or launch template) with the correct instance type and then update the Auto Scaling group to use it—you cannot modify an existing launch configuration in place once it has been created. After updating the group, new instances launched will use the correct type. Existing instances must be terminated and replaced. Modifying the Auto Scaling group directly without creating a new launch configuration is not possible because instance type is a launch configuration property. The current instances with the wrong type will continue running until they are replaced.

q:
A company wants to store business-critical data on Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes which provide persistent storage independent of Amazon EC2 instances. During a test run, the development team found that on terminating an Amazon EC2 instance, the attached Amazon EBS volume was also lost, which was contrary to their assumptions.
As a solutions architect, could you explain this issue?
options: On termination of an Amazon EC2 instance, all the attached Amazon EBS volumes are always terminated
The Amazon EBS volume was configured as the root volume of Amazon EC2 instance. On termination of the instance, the default behavior is to also terminate the attached root volume
The Amazon EBS volumes were not backed up on Amazon EFS file system storage, resulting in the loss of volume
The Amazon EBS volumes were not backed up on Amazon S3 storage, resulting in the loss of volume
explain: The most likely cause is that the EBS volume was the root volume of the EC2 instance, and by default, root EBS volumes are set to delete on instance termination (DeleteOnTermination=true). When the instance was terminated, the root volume was automatically deleted following this default behavior. This is the critical architectural oversight—to prevent data loss, you must change the DeleteOnTermination attribute to false for root volumes containing critical data, or use a non-root EBS volume for critical data with a separate data lifecycle management strategy. EBS volumes are not stored on S3 or EFS—the backup statements in options C and D are incorrect.

q:
Your e-commerce application is using an Amazon RDS PostgreSQL database and an analytics workload also runs on the same database. When the analytics workload is run, your e-commerce application slows down which further affects your sales.
Which of the following is the MOST cost-optimal solution to fix this issue?
options: Enable Multi-AZ for the Amazon RDS database and run the analytics workload on the standby database
Migrate the analytics application to AWS Lambda
Create a Read Replica in another Region as the Master database and point the analytics workload there
Create a Read Replica in the same Region as the Master database and point the analytics workload there
explain: Creating an RDS Read Replica in the same region as the primary and directing analytics workloads to it is the correct approach because Read Replicas are specifically designed for read scaling, allowing the analytics queries to run without impacting the primary database's write performance. Enabling Multi-AZ does not help with read scaling—the standby instance in Multi-AZ is not accessible for reads and only exists for high availability failover. Creating a Read Replica in another region adds unnecessary complexity and cross-region replication costs when the analytics team is in the same region. Migrating analytics to Lambda is impractical for complex reporting queries.

q:
A developer has configured inbound traffic for the relevant ports in both the Security Group of the Amazon EC2 instance as well as the network access control list (network ACL) of the subnet for the Amazon EC2 instance. The developer is, however, unable to connect to the service running on the Amazon EC2 instance.
As a solutions architect, how will you fix this issue?
options: Security Groups are stateful, so allowing inbound traffic to the necessary ports enables the connection. Network access control list (network ACL) are stateless, so you must allow both inbound and outbound traffic

Rules associated with network access control list (network ACL) should never be modified from command line. An attempt to modify rules from command line blocks the rule and results in an erratic behavior

Network access control list (network ACL) are stateful, so allowing inbound traffic to the necessary ports enables the connection. Security Groups are stateless, so you must allow both inbound and outbound traffic
IAM Role defined in the Security Group is different from the IAM Role that is given access in the network access control list (network ACL)
explain: Security Groups are stateful, meaning that if you allow inbound traffic on a port, the return traffic is automatically allowed regardless of outbound rules. Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) are stateless, meaning you must explicitly configure both inbound AND outbound rules to allow traffic to flow in both directions. The developer's issue is that the NACL is blocking the return traffic because only inbound rules were configured, not the corresponding outbound rules for the ephemeral port range (1024-65535). Option C incorrectly states that NACLs are stateful, which is the opposite of their actual behavior.

q:
A tech enterprise operates several workloads using Amazon EC2, AWS Fargate, and AWS Lambda across various teams. To optimize compute costs, the company has purchased Compute Savings Plans. The cloud operations team needs to implement a solution that not only monitors utilization but also sends automated alerts when coverage levels of the Compute Savings Plans fall below a defined threshold.
What is the MOST operationally efficient way to achieve this?
options: Use AWS Budgets to create a daily coverage budget specifically for Compute Savings Plans. Define a coverage threshold and configure notifications to alert relevant stakeholders
Enable Compute Optimizer recommendations for EC2 and Fargate. Configure automatic notifications for cost optimization opportunities and Savings Plans coverage drops
Create a standalone dashboard in Amazon CloudWatch to track EC2 and Fargate usage. Use metric math to estimate coverage and trigger alarms
Configure a custom script that queries the Savings Plans utilization API and pushes results to an Amazon S3 bucket. Use Amazon QuickSight to visualize coverage and email reports weekly
explain: AWS Budgets with a Compute Savings Plans coverage budget is the most operationally efficient approach for tracking whether EC2, Fargate, and Lambda usage is covered by existing Savings Plans commitments. A coverage budget alerts you when the percentage of your compute spend covered by Savings Plans drops below a defined threshold (e.g., 80%), enabling you to proactively purchase additional commitments. AWS Cost Explorer provides Savings Plans utilization reports but lacks automated proactive alerting. Custom CloudWatch dashboards and Lambda scripts require building and maintaining infrastructure that AWS Budgets provides natively. AWS Compute Optimizer focuses on right-sizing recommendations, not Savings Plans coverage.

q: A global photography startup hosts a static image-sharing site on an Amazon S3 bucket. The website allows users from different parts of the world to upload, view, and download photos through their mobile devices. As the platform has gained popularity, users have started experiencing latency issues, especially when uploading and downloading images. The team needs a solution to enhance global performance but wants to implement it with minimal development effort and without redesigning the applicat
options: Deploy an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as the origin to improve download speeds. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration to reduce upload latency for global users
Migrate the website from S3 to Amazon EC2 instances in multiple Regions. Use an Application Load Balancer with AWS Global Accelerator to distribute global traffic and reduce latency
Enable AWS Global Accelerator on the S3 bucket to accelerate both uploads and downloads. Reconfigure the website to route requests through the accelerator
Create multiple S3 buckets in different Regions and replicate image data based on user location. Configure CloudFront to upload and download from the nearest bucket
explain: Amazon CloudFront is the correct solution for accelerating static content delivery from an S3 bucket because it caches content at 450+ edge locations globally, dramatically reducing download latency for international users. CloudFront also supports S3 Transfer Acceleration equivalents for uploads through its edge network. AWS Global Accelerator is designed for dynamic, non-cacheable content and uses anycast routing to AWS endpoints—it does not natively integrate with S3 buckets as an origin. Creating multiple S3 buckets in different regions with replication adds significant management complexity and S3 replication costs. EC2 instances would introduce infrastructure management overhead and higher costs.

q:
The development team at a company manages a Python based nightly process with a runtime of 30 minutes. The process can withstand any interruptions in its execution and start over again. The process currently runs on the on-premises infrastructure and it needs to be migrated to AWS.
Which of the following options do you recommend as the MOST cost-effective solution?
options: Run on an Application Load Balancer
Run on Amazon EMR
Run on AWS Lambda
Run on a Spot Instance with a persistent request type
explain: A Spot Instance with a persistent request type is the correct choice for a 30-minute Python batch process because it can leverage unused EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount, and a persistent request ensures the Spot Instance is relaunched if interrupted before completion. AWS Lambda has a maximum execution timeout of 15 minutes, making it unsuitable for a 30-minute process. Amazon EMR is designed for distributed big data frameworks (Spark, Hadoop) and would be over-engineered for a single Python script. An Application Load Balancer is a network component, not a compute service, and cannot execute code. The persistent Spot request type continues to resubmit the request if the instance is reclaimed.

q:
An e-commerce company uses Amazon RDS MySQL DB to store the data. The analytics department at the company runs its reports on the same database. The engineering team has noticed sluggish performance on the database when the analytics reporting process is in progress.
As an AWS Certified Solutions Architect - Associate, which of the following would you suggest as the MOST cost-optimal solution to improve the performance?
options: Create a read-replica with the same compute capacity and the same storage capacity as the primary. Point the reporting queries to run against the read replica
Create a standby instance in a multi-AZ configuration with the same compute capacity and the same storage capacity as the primary. Point the reporting queries to run against the standby instance
Create a standby instance in a multi-AZ configuration with half compute capacity and half storage capacity as the primary. Point the reporting queries to run against the standby instance
Create a read-replica with half compute capacity and half storage capacity as the primary. Point the reporting queries to run against the read replica
explain: Creating a Read Replica with the same compute and storage capacity as the primary and pointing analytics queries to it is the correct approach because Read Replicas in Amazon RDS MySQL accept read-only connections, allowing analytics reports to run without competing with production write traffic. Using the same capacity ensures analytics queries do not become a bottleneck. The Multi-AZ standby instance is not accessible for reads—it exists solely as a hot standby for automatic failover and does not reduce read load on the primary. Using half the capacity for a replica risks the analytics queries timing out on large datasets.

q: A startup uses a fleet of Amazon EC2 servers to manage its CRM application. These Amazon EC2 servers are behind Elastic Load Balancing (ELB). Which of the following configurations are NOT allowed for Elastic Load Balancing?
options: Use the Elastic Load Balancing to distribute traffic for four Amazon EC2 instances. All the four instances are deployed in Availability Zone A of `us-east-1` region
Use the Elastic Load Balancing to distribute traffic for four Amazon EC2 instances. All the four instances are deployed across two Availability Zones of `us-east-1` region

Use the Elastic Load Balancing to distribute traffic for four Amazon EC2 instances. Two of these instances are deployed in Availability Zone A of us-east-1 region and the other two instances are deployed in Availability Zone B of `us-west-1` region
Use the Elastic Load Balancing to distribute traffic for four Amazon EC2 instances. All the four instances are deployed in Availability Zone B of `us-west-1` region
explain: Distributing two EC2 instances across Availability Zone A and two across Availability Zone B within the same region provides the optimal balance of high availability and cost efficiency for the CRM application. If one Availability Zone experiences an outage, the Elastic Load Balancer automatically routes traffic to the instances in the remaining AZ, maintaining application availability. Deploying all four instances in a single AZ eliminates redundancy—a single AZ failure would take down the entire application. Spanning multiple regions is not required for this scenario and would significantly increase cost and architectural complexity.

q:
A digital media company wants to track user engagement across its streaming platform by capturing events such as video starts, pauses, and search queries. These events must be ingested and analyzed in real time to improve user experience and optimize recommendations. The platform experiences unpredictable spikes in traffic during popular content releases. The company needs a highly scalable and serverless solution that can seamlessly adjust to changing workloads without manual provisioning.
Whic
options: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose to ingest user events. Set the destination as Amazon S3. Use Amazon Athena with scheduled queries to analyze the data periodically

Use Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) to publish clickstream events. Subscribe an Amazon SQS standard queue to receive the events. Process the events in batches with AWS Glue jobs scheduled at fixed intervals
Use an Amazon Kinesis Data Streams stream in on-demand capacity mode to ingest user engagement data. Configure an AWS Lambda function as a consumer to process the events in real time

Deploy a fleet of Amazon EC2 instances running Apache Kafka to ingest clickstream data. Set up custom scripts to manually scale the Kafka cluster based on CPU usage. Use Amazon Athena to run periodic queries on stored clickstream logs
explain: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams in on-demand capacity mode is the correct solution for variable-volume clickstream ingestion because it automatically scales shard capacity based on incoming data throughput without requiring manual shard management, eliminating under-provisioning during traffic spikes or over-provisioning during quiet periods. AWS Lambda consumers can process stream records in real time with sub-second latency, enabling immediate analytics on user engagement events. Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose is a near-real-time delivery service (60-second minimum buffer) and does not support the Lambda consumer pattern for immediate processing. SNS is a notification service without stream retention or replay capability. Self-managed Kafka on EC2 increases operational overhead significantly.

q:
A retail company needs a secure connection between its on-premises data center and AWS Cloud. This connection does not need high bandwidth and will handle a small amount of traffic. The company wants a quick turnaround time to set up the connection.
What is the MOST cost-effective way to establish such a connection?
options: Set up AWS Direct Connect
Set up an AWS Site-to-Site VPN connection
Set up an Internet Gateway between the on-premises data center and AWS cloud
Set up a bastion host on Amazon EC2
explain: AWS Site-to-Site VPN is the correct solution for establishing a secure encrypted connection between an on-premises data center and AWS without requiring dedicated physical infrastructure. Site-to-Site VPN uses IPSec to encrypt traffic over the public internet, providing confidentiality without the lead time and cost of AWS Direct Connect. Direct Connect provides a dedicated private network connection with consistent bandwidth and lower latency but requires weeks to provision and higher recurring costs—not appropriate when the requirement does not specify performance SLAs. An Internet Gateway provides outbound internet access for VPC resources but provides no encryption or secure private connectivity. Bastion hosts provide SSH access to instances, not network-level secure connectivity.

q:
A company needs an Active Directory service to run directory-aware workloads in the AWS Cloud and it should also support configuring a trust relationship with any existing on-premises Microsoft Active Directory.
Which AWS Directory Service is the best fit for this requirement?
options: AWS Directory Service for Microsoft Active Directory (AWS Managed Microsoft AD)
Simple Active Directory (Simple AD)
Active Directory Connector
AWS Transit Gateway
explain: AWS Directory Service for Microsoft Active Directory (AWS Managed Microsoft AD) is the correct choice when you need an Active Directory service that runs directory-aware workloads in AWS Cloud AND supports trust relationships with on-premises Active Directory. AWS Managed Microsoft AD is an actual Microsoft Active Directory built on Windows Server, managed by AWS, and supports AD features like Group Policy, schema extensions, and transitive trust with existing on-premises AD domains. AWS Transit Gateway is a network routing service for VPC connectivity, not a directory service. Simple AD is based on Samba and does not support trust relationships with Microsoft AD.

q:
A global financial services provider operates data analytics workloads across multiple AWS Regions. The company stores regulated datasets in Amazon S3 buckets and requires visibility into security and compliance configurations. As part of a new audit initiative, the compliance team must identify all S3 buckets across the environment that do not have versioning enabled. The solution must scale across all Regions and accounts with minimal manual intervention.
Which solution will meet these require
options: Enable IAM Access Analyzer for all Regions. Review the analyzer reports to identify S3 buckets without versioning enabled and configure IAM policies to restrict access to such buckets

Enable Amazon S3 Storage Lens with advanced metrics and recommendations. Use the per-bucket dashboard to filter and view versioning status across Regions and identify all buckets that do not have versioning enabled
Create a centralized Amazon S3 Multi-Region Access Point for all buckets. Use this access point to perform versioning checks programmatically by inspecting objects' metadata from each bucket

Configure an AWS CloudTrail trail across all Regions. Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that filters for `PutBucketVersioning` and `DeleteBucketVersioning` API calls. Trigger an AWS Lambda function to analyze the bucket configurations and generate a report of unversioned buckets
explain: Amazon S3 Storage Lens with advanced metrics is the correct tool for monitoring S3 bucket configurations—including versioning status—across multiple AWS Regions from a centralized dashboard. Storage Lens provides organization-wide visibility into S3 usage with per-bucket breakdowns, allowing administrators to filter and identify all buckets that do not have versioning enabled. IAM Access Analyzer identifies resource-based policies that allow public or cross-account access, not versioning configuration. Creating a Multi-Region Access Point does not assess or enforce versioning policies—it only provides a unified endpoint for accessing buckets. CloudTrail + EventBridge would detect future events but cannot audit the current state of existing buckets.

q: A healthcare startup is deploying an AWS-based analytics platform that processes sensitive patient records. The application backend uses Amazon RDS for structured data and Amazon S3 for storing medical files. S3 Event Notifications trigger AWS Lambda for real-time data classification and alerting. The startup uses AWS IAM Identity Center to manage federated access from their enterprise directory. Development, operations, and compliance teams require granular and secure access to RDS and S3 resou
options: Create an IAM identity provider that integrates with the company's IdP (e.g., Azure AD or Okta). Use SAML federation to grant access to IAM roles that are manually assigned to each user. Create and maintain inline IAM policies for each role to access RDS and S3

Use AWS IAM Identity Center integrated with the organization’s directory. Define permission sets with least-privilege policies for Amazon RDS and Amazon S3. Assign users to groups based on their team roles and map those groups to the appropriate permission sets

Create individual IAM users for each team member. Attach role-based IAM policies granting permissions to RDS and S3 based on team roles. Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to monitor for unused permissions and rotate access keys periodically

Use AWS Organizations to group team accounts under a single organizational unit (OU). Attach Service Control Policies (SCPs) to the OU that define access boundaries for Amazon RDS and Amazon S3 based on each team’s responsibilities. Assign users to accounts and let SCPs enforce the required access
explain: AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) integrated with the organization's directory is the recommended approach for centralized workforce access management because it enables single sign-on, centrally managed permission sets, and integration with corporate identity providers (Azure AD, Okta). It follows least-privilege principles at scale without creating and managing individual IAM users. Creating individual IAM users for each team member is an anti-pattern for organizations—it creates management overhead, lacks centralized SSO, and makes onboarding/offboarding error-prone. AWS Organizations with SCPs restricts maximum permissions but does not provide SSO or fine-grained per-service access management.

q:
Your application is deployed on Amazon EC2 instances fronted by an Application Load Balancer. Recently, your infrastructure has come under attack. Attackers perform over 100 requests per second, while your normal users only make about 5 requests per second.
How can you efficiently prevent attackers from overwhelming your application?
options: Use an AWS Web Application Firewall (AWS WAF) and setup a rate-based rule
Define a network access control list (network ACL) on your Application Load Balancer
Configure Sticky Sessions on the Application Load Balancer
Use AWS Shield Advanced and setup a rate-based rule
explain: AWS WAF with a rate-based rule is the correct solution for protecting against HTTP floods and bot attacks because WAF rate-based rules can automatically block or throttle IP addresses that exceed a specified request rate threshold, which is exactly the mechanism needed to protect against request floods. AWS Shield Advanced provides DDoS protection at the network and transport layers and includes WAF integration, but Shield Advanced does not itself offer rate-based rules—those rules are a WAF feature. NACLs cannot be applied to Application Load Balancers. Sticky sessions are a load distribution mechanism, not a security control.

q: A media company is modernizing its legacy image processing application by migrating it from an on-premises environment to AWS. The application handles a high volume of image transformation jobs, generating large output files. To support rapid growth, the company wants a cloud-native solution that automatically scales, minimizes manual intervention, and avoids managing servers or infrastructure. The team also wants to improve workflow automation to handle task sequencing and job state transitions
options: Use a combination of AWS Lambda functions and EC2 Spot Instances for processing. Store processed images in Amazon FSx
Use Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling groups with a static fleet of instances for image processing. Trigger each job through Step Functions and store results on attached EBS volumes
Deploy Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS) with self-managed EC2 worker nodes for image processing. Use Amazon SQS to queue jobs and store processed outputs in Amazon EBS volumes
Use AWS Batch to process image jobs. Orchestrate the workflow using AWS Step Functions and store output files in Amazon S3
explain: AWS Batch is the correct service for batch image processing workloads because it dynamically provisions EC2 or Fargate compute capacity based on job queue volume, eliminating the need to manage compute infrastructure. AWS Step Functions orchestrates the multi-step workflow—job submission, dependency management, retry logic, and error handling—providing a visual, auditable workflow. Storing output in S3 provides durable, cost-effective storage for processed images. A static EC2 Auto Scaling fleet wastes money when the queue is empty. Lambda functions cannot run batch jobs longer than 15 minutes. EKS with self-managed nodes requires substantial operational effort compared to the fully managed AWS Batch solution.

q: A research firm archives experimental datasets generated by automated laboratory equipment. Each dataset is about 10 MB in size and is initially accessed frequently for analysis within the first month. After this period, the access rate drops significantly, but the data must remain immediately retrievable if needed. Due to compliance policies, each dataset must be retained in AWS storage for exactly 4 years before deletion. The firm currently stores the data in Amazon S3 Standard storage and wan
options: Define an S3 Lifecycle policy that transitions datasets to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval 30 days after creation and schedules the deletion of each object exactly 4 years after its creation
Configure an S3 Lifecycle policy to migrate datasets to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 30 days and delete them automatically 4 years after creation

Define an S3 Lifecycle policy that transitions datasets to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) 30 days after creation and schedules the deletion of each object exactly 4 years after its creation
Set up an S3 Lifecycle configuration to transfer all datasets to S3 One Zone-Infrequent Access (S3 One Zone-IA) after 30 days, and permanently delete them 4 years after creation
explain: Amazon S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval is the correct storage class for research datasets that are randomly accessed infrequently (e.g., annually) because it provides long-term archival storage at significantly lower cost than S3 Standard-IA, with retrieval times of 1-12 hours acceptable for non-urgent research access patterns. S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval is more expensive than Flexible Retrieval and is designed for quarterly-access patterns requiring millisecond retrieval—appropriate only when instant access is needed. S3 Standard-IA is designed for data accessed at least monthly and charges a minimum storage duration of 30 days. S3 One Zone-IA stores data in only one Availability Zone, creating risk of data loss inappropriate for archival research datasets.

q:
A company is experiencing stability issues with their cluster of self-managed RabbitMQ message brokers and the company now wants to explore an alternate solution on AWS.
As a solutions architect, which of the following AWS services would you recommend that can provide support for quick and easy migration from RabbitMQ?
options: Amazon MQ
Amazon SQS FIFO (First-In-First-Out)
Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) Standard
Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS)
explain: Amazon MQ is the correct solution because it is a managed message broker service that supports Apache ActiveMQ and RabbitMQ, allowing teams to migrate their existing RabbitMQ workloads without rewriting application code—the standard AMQP, MQTT, and STOMP protocols continue to work. Amazon SNS is a pub/sub notification service that does not support AMQP or the RabbitMQ broker API. Amazon SQS requires code changes since it uses a different API and lacks RabbitMQ-specific features like exchanges and binding patterns. Amazon MQ eliminates the operational burden of managing broker clusters, patching, and scaling while maintaining compatibility with the existing application.

q:
A company is deploying a publicly accessible web application. To accomplish this, the engineering team has designed the VPC with a public subnet and a private subnet. The application will be hosted on several Amazon EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group. The team also wants Transport Layer Security (TLS) termination to be offloaded from the Amazon EC2 instances.
Which solution should a solutions architect implement to address these requirements in the most secure manner?
options: Set up a Network Load Balancer in the private subnet. Create an Auto Scaling group in the public subnet and associate it with the Network Load Balancer
Set up a Network Load Balancer in the public subnet. Create an Auto Scaling group in the public subnet and associate it with the Network Load Balancer
Set up a Network Load Balancer in the private subnet. Create an Auto Scaling group in the private subnet and associate it with the Network Load Balancer
Set up a Network Load Balancer in the public subnet. Create an Auto Scaling group in the private subnet and associate it with the Network Load Balancer
explain: For a publicly accessible web application, the Network Load Balancer must be placed in a public subnet (with an internet-facing scheme) to receive traffic from the internet, while the EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group should reside in private subnets for security—they receive traffic only from the NLB and do not need direct internet exposure. Placing the NLB in a private subnet means it cannot receive inbound internet traffic, breaking public accessibility. Placing the EC2 instances in a public subnet exposes them directly to the internet, violating the defense-in-depth principle. The correct architecture separates the public-facing load balancer from the private application tier.

q:
A company has many Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (Amazon VPC) in various accounts, that need to be connected in a star network with one another and connected with on-premises networks through AWS Direct Connect.
What do you recommend?
options: AWS PrivateLink
Virtual private gateway (VGW)
AWS Transit Gateway
VPC Peering Connection
explain: AWS Transit Gateway is the correct solution for connecting multiple VPCs in a star (hub-and-spoke) network topology because it acts as a central hub that any number of VPCs and on-premises networks can attach to, enabling transitive routing between all connected networks through a single gateway. VPC Peering connections do not support transitive routing—traffic from VPC A cannot route through VPC B to reach VPC C—so connecting many VPCs would require an O(n²) number of peering connections. A Virtual Private Gateway (VGW) is used for connecting VPCs to on-premises networks via VPN or Direct Connect, not for VPC-to-VPC connectivity. AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity to services, not general VPC interconnection.

q:
A car dealership website hosted in Amazon EC2 stores car listings in an Amazon Aurora database managed by Amazon RDS. Once a vehicle has been sold, its data must be removed from the current listings and forwarded to a distributed processing system.
Which of the following options can satisfy the given requirement?
options: Create an RDS event subscription and send the notifications to Amazon SQS. Configure the SQS queues to fan out the event notifications to multiple Amazon SNS topics. Process the data using AWS Lambda functions.

Use an Aurora MySQL native function to invoke an AWS Lambda function whenever a vehicle listing is deleted. Configure the Lambda function to send the data to an Amazon SQS queue for the distributed processing system to consume.

Create an RDS event subscription and send the notifications to Amazon SNS. Configure the SNS topic to fan out the event notifications to multiple Amazon SQS queues. Process the data using AWS Lambda functions.
Create an RDS event subscription and send the notifications to AWS Lambda. Configure the Lambda function to fanout the event notifications to multiple Amazon SQS queues to update the target groups.
explain: Amazon Aurora MySQL supports natively invoking AWS Lambda functions directly from the database using the lambda_sync() and lambda_async() stored functions, triggered by database events such as INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. This native integration allows the Aurora database to push vehicle listing change notifications to Lambda without requiring application-layer polling or external event bridge configurations. Using RDS event subscriptions (options A and C) captures instance-level events like failovers and restores, not row-level data change events. Sending notifications directly from RDS to Lambda via event subscription (option D) does not capture row-level changes.

q:
An online learning company hosts its Microsoft .NET e-Learning application on a Windows Server in its on-premises data center. The application uses an Oracle Database Standard Edition as its backend database.
The company wants a high-performing solution to migrate this workload to the AWS cloud to take advantage of the cloud’s high availability. The migration process should minimize development changes, and the environment should be easier to manage.
Which of the following options should be impl
options: Use AWS Application Migration Service (AWS MGN) to migrate the on-premises Oracle database server to a new Amazon EC2 instance.
Perform a homogeneous migration by moving the Oracle database to Amazon RDS for Oracle in a Multi-AZ deployment using AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS).

Provision and replatform the application to Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) with Amazon EC2 worker nodes. Use the Windows Server Amazon Machine Image (AMI) and deploy the .NET application using to the ECS cluster via the ECS Anywhere service.
Rehost the on-premises .NET application to an AWS Elastic Beanstalk Multi-AZ environment which runs in multiple Availability Zones.
explain: AWS Application Migration Service (AWS MGN) is the primary recommended service for lift-and-shift migrations because it continuously replicates source servers (including the .NET application and Oracle database) to AWS and allows non-disruptive cutover testing, minimizing downtime. Unlike VM Import/Export, which requires exporting VMs and uploading images manually, AWS MGN uses agent-based continuous replication, significantly reducing migration risk and RPO. AWS Database Migration Service handles data replication but is designed for database engine changes, not full-server lift-and-shift migrations. DataSync is optimized for file and object storage transfers, not server migration.

q:
A Solutions Architect needs to make sure that the On-Demand Amazon EC2 instance can only be accessed from this IP address (110.238.98.71) via an SSH connection.
Which configuration below will satisfy this requirement?
options: Security Group Inbound Rule: Protocol – UDP, Port Range – 22, Source 110.238.98.71/32
Security Group Inbound Rule: Protocol – TCP, Port Range – 22, Source 110.238.98.71/32
Security Group Outbound Rule: Protocol – TCP, Port Range – 22, Destination 110.238.98.71/32
Security Group Outbound Rule: Protocol – UDP, Port Range – 22, Destination 0.0.0.0/0
explain: SSH requires TCP protocol on port 22—not UDP—because SSH uses TCP's reliable, ordered delivery to establish secure interactive sessions. A Security Group inbound rule must specify TCP, port 22, and the source IP as 110.238.98.71/32 (/32 is the CIDR notation for a single specific IP address). Security Groups are stateful, so an inbound rule allowing TCP port 22 automatically allows the return traffic without a corresponding outbound rule. An outbound rule would control traffic leaving the instance, not access to it. UDP does not support SSH protocol.

q:
An online cryptocurrency exchange platform is hosted in AWS, utilizing an Amazon ECS Cluster and Amazon RDS in a Multi-AZ Deployments configuration. The application heavily uses the RDS instance to process complex read and write database operations. To maintain reliability, availability, and performance, it is necessary to closely monitor how the different processes or threads on a DB instance use the CPU, including the percentage of CPU bandwidth and total memory consumed by each process.
Which
options: Enable Enhanced Monitoring in RDS.

Check the `CPU%` and `MEM%` metrics which are readily available in the RDS console that shows the percentage of the CPU bandwidth and total memory consumed by each database process of your RDS instance.

Create a script that collects and publishes custom metrics to Amazon CloudWatch, which tracks the real-time CPU Utilization of the RDS instance, and then set up a custom CloudWatch dashboard to view the metrics.
Use Amazon CloudWatch to monitor the CPU Utilization of your database.
explain: Amazon RDS Enhanced Monitoring provides OS-level metrics (CPU, memory, file system, disk I/O) with up to 1-second granularity by running an agent directly on the DB instance. Standard CloudWatch metrics for RDS are collected from the hypervisor, which cannot accurately report memory utilization inside the database instance—Enhanced Monitoring captures this from within the OS. The RDS console does not natively display CPU% and MEM% metrics from inside the instance without Enhanced Monitoring enabled. Amazon CloudWatch basic CPU utilization for RDS is a hypervisor metric and does not reflect the actual OS-level memory consumption needed for capacity planning.

q: A Forex trading platform, which frequently processes and stores global financial data every minute, is hosted in an on-premises data center and uses an Oracle database. Due to a recent cooling problem in its data center, the company urgently needs to migrate its infrastructure to AWS to improve the performance of its applications. As the Solutions Architect, the responsibility is to ensure that the database is properly migrated and remains available in case of database server failure in the futu
options: Launch an Oracle database instance in Amazon RDS with Recovery Manager (RMAN) enabled.
Migrate the Oracle database to a non-cluster Amazon Aurora with a single instance.
Migrate the Oracle database to AWS using the AWS Database Migration Service
Convert the database schema using the AWS Schema Conversion Tool.
explain: Amazon RDS Multi-AZ deployments provide enhanced availability by maintaining a synchronous standby replica in a different Availability Zone, with automatic failover in 60-120 seconds if the primary instance fails. For a Forex trading platform requiring high availability and durability for financial data, Amazon Aurora (with built-in Multi-AZ storage replication across 3 AZs) is more appropriate than a single Aurora instance which has no automatic failover. Oracle RMAN is a backup and recovery tool that does not provide the HA capabilities needed. AWS DMS is a migration service, not a deployment architecture. Schema Conversion Tool assists with database schema migration from Oracle to another engine, not HA design.

q:
An e-commerce company utilizes a regional Amazon API Gateway to host its public REST APIs. The API Gateway endpoint is accessed through a custom domain name set up with an Amazon Route 53 alias record. To support continuous improvement, the company intends to launch a new version of its APIs with enhanced features and performance optimizations.
How can the company reduce customer disruption and ensure MINIMAL data loss during the update process in the MOST cost-effective way?
options: Create a new API Gateway with the updated version of the APIs in OpenAPI JSON or YAML file format, but keep the same custom domain name for the new API Gateway.

Modify the existing API Gateway with the updated version of the APIs, but keep the same custom domain name for the new API Gateway by using the import-to-update operation in either overwrite or merge mode.

Implement a blue-green deployment strategy for the API Gateway, deploying the latest version of the APIs to the green environment. Route some user traffic to it, validate the new APIs, and once thoroughly validated, promote the green environment to production.

Implement a canary release deployment strategy for the API Gateway. Deploy the latest version of the APIs to a canary stage and direct a portion of the user traffic to this stage. Verify the new APIs. Gradually increase the traffic percentage, monitor for any issues, and, if successful, promote the
explain: Amazon API Gateway supports canary release deployments, which route a configurable percentage of API traffic to a new 'canary' stage deployment while the majority continues to serve the existing production version. This enables safe, incremental validation of API changes in production with real traffic before promoting the canary to full production. Blue-green deployment replaces traffic all-at-once (not gradually), increasing rollout risk. Creating a new API Gateway in a separate OpenAPI file requires updating all client integrations. Modifying the existing API directly risks breaking production if the update has defects. Canary releases allow instant rollback by reverting the traffic split percentage.

q:
A government entity is conducting a population and housing census in the city. Each household information uploaded on their online portal is stored in encrypted files in Amazon S3. The government assigned its Solutions Architect to set compliance policies that verify data containing personally identifiable information (PII) in a manner that meets their compliance standards. They should also be alerted if there are potential policy violations with the privacy of their S3 buckets.
Which of the fol
options: Set up and configure Amazon Macie to monitor their Amazon S3 data.
Set up and configure Amazon Fraud Detector to send out alert notifications whenever a security violation is detected on their Amazon S3 data.
Set up and configure Amazon Polly to scan for usage patterns on Amazon S3 data
Set up and configure Amazon Kendra to monitor malicious activity on their Amazon S3 data
explain: Amazon Macie is the correct service for protecting sensitive data in Amazon S3 because it uses machine learning to automatically discover, classify, and protect sensitive data such as personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, and health records.
