An object is only cached by CloudFront once a successful request has been made hence, the objects were not requested before, which is why the request is still directed to the origin server.
There are two primary origins configured in your Amazon CloudFront Origin Group.
explain:
You can control how long your objects stay in a CloudFront cache before CloudFront forwards another request to your origin. Reducing the duration allows you to serve dynamic content. Increasing the duration means your users get better performance because your objects are more likely to be served directly from the edge cache. A longer duration also reduces the load on your origin.
Typically, CloudFront serves an object from an edge location until the cache duration that you specified passes — that is, until the object expires.

q:
A company has a VPC for its Human Resource department and another VPC located in different AWS regions for its Finance department. The Solutions Architect must redesign the architecture to allow the finance department to access all resources that are in the human resource department, and vice versa. An Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) must also be integrated for active traffic flow inspection and to block any vulnerability exploits.
Which network
options: Establish a secure connection between the two VPCs using a NAT Gateway. Manage user sessions via the AWS Systems Manager Session Manager service.
Create a Traffic Policy in Amazon Route 53 to connect the two VPCs. Configure the Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall to do active traffic flow inspection and block any vulnerability exploits.
Create a Direct Connect Gateway and add VPC attachments to connect all departments. Configure AWS Security Hub to secure the application traffic travelling between the VPCs.
Launch an AWS Transit Gateway and add VPC attachments to connect all departments. Set up AWS Network Firewall to secure the application traffic travelling between the VPCs.
explain:
A transit gateway is a network transit hub that you can use to interconnect your virtual private clouds (VPCs) and on-premises networks. As your cloud infrastructure expands globally, inter-Region peering connects transit gateways together using the AWS Global Infrastructure. Your data is automatically encrypted and never travels over the public internet.

A transit gateway attachment is both a source and a destination of packets. You can attach the following resources to your transit gateway:
– One or more VPCs.

options: The `Resources` section is missing.
An invalid section named `Parameters` is present. This will cause the CloudFormation stack to fail.
The value of the `AWSTemplateFormatVersion` is incorrect. It should be 2017-06-06.
The `Conditions` section is missing.
explain: In CloudFormation, a template is a JSON or a YAML-formatted text file that describes your AWS infrastructure. Templates include several major sections. The Resources section is the only required section. Some sections in a template can be in any order. However, as you build your template, it might be helpful to use the logical ordering of the following list, as values in one section might refer to values from a previous section. Take note that all of the sections here are optional, except for Resources, which is the only one required.

q:
A company has several unencrypted Amazon EBS snapshots in its Amazon VPC. The Solutions Architect must ensure that all of the new EBS volumes restored from the unencrypted snapshots are automatically encrypted.
What should be done to accomplish this requirement?
options: Enable the EBS Encryption By Default feature for specific EBS volumes.
Launch new EBS volumes and encrypt them using asymmetric AWS KMS keys.
Enable the EBS Encryption By Default feature for the AWS Region.
Launch new EBS volumes and specify the symmetric encryption AWS KMS keys for encryption.
explain:
You can configure your AWS account to enforce the encryption of the new EBS volumes and snapshot copies that you create. For example, Amazon EBS encrypts the EBS volumes created when you launch an instance and the snapshots that you copy from an unencrypted snapshot.

Encryption by default has no effect on existing EBS volumes or snapshots. The following are important considerations in EBS encryption:
– Encryption by default is a Region-specific setting. If you enable it for a Region, you cannot disable it for individual volumes or snapshots in that Region.

q:
A company is planning to deploy a High Performance Computing (HPC) cluster in its VPC that requires a scalable, high-performance file system. The storage service must be optimized for efficient workload processing, and the data must be accessible via a fast and scalable file system interface. It should also work natively with Amazon S3 that enables you to easily process your S3 data with a high-performance POSIX interface.
Which of the follow
options: Amazon FSx for Lustre
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
Amazon Elastic File System (EFS)
Amazon Elastic Block Storage (EBS)
explain: Amazon FSx for Lustre is the optimal storage solution for high-performance computing (HPC) and machine learning workloads that require extremely fast throughput because it provides a POSIX-compliant parallel file system with sub-millisecond latency and throughput scalable to hundreds of GB/s, natively integrated with Amazon S3 for data lake access. FSx for Lustre is purpose-built for compute-intensive workloads including machine learning, HPC, video processing, and financial simulation. Amazon EBS provides block storage for individual instances but cannot match FSx for Lustre's parallel throughput or be shared across an HPC cluster without complex NFS configuration. Amazon EFS provides shared NFS storage but with significantly lower throughput than FSx for Lustre.

q:
A startup is building a microservices architecture in which the software is composed of small independent services that communicate over well-defined APIs. In building large-scale systems, fine-grained decoupling of microservices is a recommended practice to implement. The decoupled services should scale horizontally from each other to improve scalability.
What is the difference between Horizontal scaling and Vertical scaling?
options: Horizontal scaling means running the same software on smaller containers such as Docker and Kubernetes using ECS or EKS. Vertical scaling is adding more servers to the existing pool and doesn’t run into limitations of individual servers.

Vertical scaling means running the same software on a fully serverless architecture using Lambda. Horizontal scaling means adding more servers to the existing pool and it doesn’t run into limitations of individual servers.

Horizontal scaling means running the same software on bigger machines which is limited by the capacity of individual servers. Vertical scaling is adding more servers to the existing pool and doesn’t run into limitations of individual servers.

Vertical scaling means running the same software on bigger machines which is limited by the capacity of the individual server. Horizontal scaling is adding more servers to the existing pool and doesn’t run into limitations of individual servers.
explain: Vertical scaling (scaling up) means running the same application on increasingly powerful and larger machines with more CPU, RAM, or storage, which is bounded by the maximum available instance size—a hard ceiling that cannot exceed the largest single server available. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) means adding more servers to the pool, distributing workload across many instances with no theoretical upper limit, and providing better fault tolerance as individual server failures do not take down the entire service. Microservices architectures inherently favor horizontal scaling since each service can be independently scaled based on its own demand. Lambda and serverless computing represent the extreme of horizontal scaling abstracted from the user.

q:
A global medical research company has a molecular imaging system that provides each client with frequently updated images of what is happening inside the human body at the molecular and cellular levels. The system is hosted in AWS and the images are hosted in an S3 bucket behind a CloudFront web distribution. When a fresh batch of images is uploaded to S3, it is required to keep the previous ones in order to prevent them from being overwritten.
Whi
options: Invalidate the files in your CloudFront web distribution
Add a separate cache behavior path for the content and configure a custom object caching with a Minimum TTL of 0
Add Cache-Control no-cache, no-store, or private directives in the S3 bucket
Use versioned objects
explain:
To control the versions of files that are served from your distribution, you can either invalidate files or give them versioned file names. If you want to update your files frequently, AWS recommends that you primarily use file versioning for the following reasons:
   * Versioning enables you to control which file a request returns even when the user has a version cached either locally or behind a corporate caching proxy. If you invalidate the file, the user might continue to see the old version until it expires from those caches.

q:
A web application, which is hosted in the on-premises data center and uses a MySQL database, must be migrated to AWS Cloud. You need to ensure that the network traffic to and from your RDS database instance is encrypted using SSL. For improved security, you have to use the profile credentials specific to your EC2 instance to access your database, instead of a password.
Which of the following should you do to meet the above requirement?
options: Set up an RDS database and enable the IAM DB Authentication.
Launch a new RDS database instance using Aurora with the Backtrack feature enabled.
Launch the mysql client using the `--ssl-ca` parameter when connecting to the database.
Configure your RDS database to enable encryption.
explain:
You can authenticate to your DB instance using AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) database authentication. IAM database authentication works with MySQL and PostgreSQL. With this authentication method, you don’t need to use a password when you connect to a DB instance. Instead, you use an authentication token.
An authentication token is a unique string of characters that Amazon RDS generates on request. Authentication tokens are generated using AWS Signature Version 4. Each token has a lifetime of 15 minutes.

q:
A Solutions Architect is managing a three-tier web application that processes credit card payments and online transactions. Static web pages are used on the front-end tier while the application tier contains a single Amazon EC2 instance that handles long-running processes. The data is stored in a MySQL database. The Solutions Architect is instructed to decouple the tiers to create a highly available application.
Which of the following options can s
options: Move all the static assets to Amazon S3. Set concurrency limit in AWS Lambda to move the application to a serverless architecture. Migrate the database to Amazon DynamoDB.
Move all the static assets and web pages to Amazon CloudFront. Use Auto Scaling in Amazon EC2 instance. Migrate the database to Amazon RDS with Multi-AZ deployments configuration.

Move all the static assets and web pages to Amazon S3. Re-host the application to Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS) containers and enable Service Auto Scaling. Migrate the database to Amazon RDS with Multi-AZ deployments configuration.
Move all the static assets, web pages, and the backend application to a larger instance. Use Auto Scaling in Amazon EC2 instance. Migrate the database to Amazon Aurora.
explain: Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) is a highly scalable, high performance container management service that supports Docker containers and allows you to easily run applications on a managed cluster of Amazon EC2 instances. Amazon ECS makes it easy to use containers as a building block for your applications by eliminating the need for you to install, operate, and scale your own cluster management infrastructure. Amazon ECS lets you schedule long-running applications, services, and batch processes using Docker containers.

q:
A company has an application architecture that stores both the access key ID and the secret access key in a plain text file on a custom Amazon Machine Image (AMI). The EC2 instances, which are created by using this AMI, are using the stored access keys to connect to a DynamoDB table.
What should the Solutions Architect do to make the current architecture more secure?
options: Remove the stored access keys in the AMI. Create a new IAM role with permissions to access the DynamoDB table and assign it to the EC2 instances.
Put the access keys in Amazon Glacier instead.
Do nothing. The architecture is already secure because the access keys are already in the Amazon Machine Image.
Put the access keys in an Amazon S3 bucket instead.
explain:
You should use an IAM role to manage temporary credentials for applications that run on an EC2 instance. When you use an IAM role, you don’t have to distribute long-term credentials (such as a user name and password or access keys) to an EC2 instance.
Instead, the role supplies temporary permissions that applications can use when they make calls to other AWS resources. When you launch an EC2 instance, you specify an IAM role to associate with the instance. Applications that run on the instance can then use the role-supplied temporary credentials to sign API requests.

q:
A computer animation film studio runs a web application on an Amazon EC2 instance. It uploads 5 GB video objects to an Amazon S3 bucket. Video uploads take longer than expected, impacting the application’s performance.
Which method will help improve the performance of the application?
options: Enable Enhanced Networking with the Elastic Network Adapter (ENA) on your EC2 Instances.
Use Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) Provisioned IOPS volumes in a Logical Volume Management (LVM) stripe configuration on an EBS-optimized instance to maximize throughput.
Leverage on Amazon CloudFront and use HTTP POST method to reduce latency.
Use S3 Multipart Upload API.
explain:
The main issue is the slow upload time of the video objects to Amazon S3. To address this issue, you can use Multipart upload in S3 to improve the throughput. It allows you to upload parts of your object in parallel thus, decreasing the time it takes to upload big objects. Each part is a contiguous portion of the object’s data.
You can upload these object parts independently and in any order. If transmission of any part fails, you can retransmit that part without affecting other parts. After all parts of your object are uploaded, Amazon S3 assembles these parts and creates the object.

q:
A company developed a financial analytics web application hosted in a Docker container using MEAN (MongoDB, Express.js, AngularJS, and Node.js) stack. You want to easily port that web application to AWS Cloud which can automatically handle all the tasks such as balancing load, auto-scaling, monitoring, and placing your containers across your cluster.
Which of the following services can be used to fulfill this requirement?
options: AWS CloudFormation
AWS Compute Optimizer
AWS Elastic Beanstalk
Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS)
explain: AWS Elastic Beanstalk is the correct service for deploying Docker-containerized web applications because it provides a fully managed platform that handles the deployment, capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring automatically, while still allowing developers to retain control over the underlying resources. Elastic Beanstalk's Multicontainer Docker environments use ECS under the hood but abstract the cluster management. Amazon ECS provides the same underlying capabilities but requires explicit cluster configuration, task definitions, and service management, resulting in higher operational overhead. The key differentiator is Elastic Beanstalk's opinionated, convention-over-configuration approach that minimizes infrastructure management.

q:
An e-commerce company plans to optimize its disaster recovery configuration using AWS Cloud to minimize operational disruptions during outages or major system maintenance for its on-premises Microsoft SQL Server-based application. The objective is to achieve a recovery point objective (RPO) of 60 seconds or less and a recovery time objective (RTO) of 1 hour.
Which of the following is the MOST cost-effective solution for this scenario?
options: On AWS, implement a warm standby using Amazon RDS for SQL Server database and configure AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) with change data capture (CDC) to sync the data from the on-premises application.
Back up SQL Server to AWS Storage Gateway for hybrid storage and fast disaster recovery. Enable fast snapshot restore in Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS).
Set up a pilot light strategy using AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (AWS DRS) to replicate the changes of the on-premises application to AWS
Use Microsoft SQL Server Enterprise with Always On availability groups and set up a multi-site active/active setup between the corporate on-premises application and AWS.
explain:
AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (AWS DRS) provides continuous block-level replication, recovery orchestration, and automated server conversion capabilities. These allow customers to achieve a crash-consistent recovery point objective (RPO) of seconds, and a recovery time objective (RTO) typically ranging between 5–20 minutes. 
In the event of a disaster, AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (AWS DRS) assists in performing a failover to AWS for immediate recovery. After mitigating the disaster, a failback to the original source infrastructure is necessary.

q:
A Solutions Architect designed a real-time data analytics system based on Kinesis Data Stream and Lambda. A week after the system has been deployed, the users noticed that it performed slowly as the data rate increases. The Architect identified that the performance of the Kinesis Data Streams is causing this problem.
Which of the following should the Architect do to improve performance?
options: Implement Step Scaling to the Kinesis Data Stream.
Improve the performance of the stream by decreasing the number of its shards using the `MergeShard` command.
Increase the number of shards of the Kinesis stream by using the `UpdateShardCount` command.
Replace the data stream with Amazon Data Firehose instead.
explain:
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams supports resharding, which lets you adjust the number of shards in your stream to adapt to changes in the rate of data flow through the stream. Resharding is considered an advanced operation.
There are two types of resharding operations: shard split and shard merge. In a shard split, you divide a single shard into two shards. In a shard merge, you combine two shards into a single shard. Resharding is always pairwise in the sense that you cannot split into more than two shards in a single operation, and you cannot merge more than two shards in a single operation.

q:
A Solutions Architect needs to launch a web application that will be served globally using Amazon CloudFront. The application is hosted in an Amazon EC2 instance which will be configured as the origin server to process and serve dynamic content to its customers.
Which of the following options provides high availability for the application?
options: Provision two EC2 instances deployed in different Availability Zones and configure them to be part of an origin group.
Use Amazon S3 to serve the dynamic content of your web application and configure the S3 bucket to be part of an origin group.
Use Lambda@Edge to improve the performance of your web application and ensure high availability. Set the Lambda@Edge functions to be part of an origin group.
Launch an Auto Scaling group of EC2 instances and configure it to be part of an origin group.
explain: An origin is a location where content is stored and from which CloudFront gets content to serve to viewers. Amazon CloudFront is a service that speeds up the distribution of your static and dynamic web content, such as .html, .css, .js, and image files, to your users. CloudFront delivers your content through a worldwide network of data centers called edge locations. When a user requests content that you’re serving with CloudFront, the user is routed to the edge location that provides the lowest latency (time delay) so that content is delivered with the best possible performance.

q:
A company is running an on-premises application backed by a 1TB MySQL 8.0 database. A couple of times each month, the production data is fully copied to a staging database at the request of the analytics team. The team can’t work on the staging database until the copy is finished, which takes hours.
Throughout this period, the application experiences intermittent downtimes as well. To expedite the process for the analytics team, a solutions archite
options: Use an Amazon RDS database in a Multi-AZ Deployments configuration
Take a manual snapshot and restore it to a database in the staging environment.
Replicate the production database to a staging database using the `mysqldump` client utility
Use an Amazon Aurora database with Multi-AZ Replicas.
explain: The resiliency of an application pertains to its ability to recover from infrastructure or service disruptions. Both Amazon Aurora and Amazon RDS can give you a highly resilient infrastructure by deploying replicas in multiple availability zones. Both database services can perform an automatic failover to a standby instance in the event of failure. However, only Amazon Aurora has the ability to replicate a database in a fast and efficient manner without impacting performance, thanks to its underlying storage system.

q:
A game company has a requirement of load balancing the incoming TCP traffic at the transport level (Layer 4) to their containerized gaming servers hosted in AWS Fargate. To maintain performance, it should handle millions of requests per second sent by gamers around the globe while maintaining ultra-low latencies.
Which of the following must be implemented in the current architecture to satisfy the new requirement?
options: Launch a new Network Load Balancer.
Launch a new microservice in AWS Fargate that acts as a load balancer since using an ALB or NLB with Fargate is not possible.
Launch a new Application Load Balancer.
Create a new record in Amazon Route 53 with Weighted Routing policy to load balance the incoming traffic.
explain: Elastic Load Balancing automatically distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets, such as Amazon EC2 instances, containers, IP addresses, and Lambda functions. It can handle the varying load of your application traffic in a single Availability Zone or across multiple Availability Zones. Elastic Load Balancing offers three types of load balancers that all feature the high availability, automatic scaling, and robust security necessary to make your applications fault-tolerant.

q:
A Solutions Architect is trying to enable Cross-Region Replication to an Amazon S3 bucket but this option is disabled. The bucket has been configured with a Multi-Region Access Point to manage incoming requests.
Which of the following options is a valid reason for this?
options: In order to use the Cross-Region Replication feature in S3, you need to first enable versioning on the bucket.
The Cross-Region Replication feature is only available for S3 – One Zone-IA.
The Cross-Region Replication feature is only available for S3 – Infrequent Access.
This is a premium feature which is only for AWS Enterprise accounts.
explain:
To enable the cross-region replication feature in S3, the following items should be met:
   1. The source and destination buckets must have versioning enabled.
   2. The source and destination buckets must be in different AWS Regions.
   3. Amazon S3 must have permission to replicate objects from that source bucket to the destination bucket on your behalf.

Hence, the correct answer is: In order to use the Cross-Region Replication feature in S3, you need to first enable versioning on the bucket.

q: A Solutions Architect is managing a company's AWS account of approximately 300 IAM users. They have a new company policy that requires changing the associated permissions of all 100 IAM users that control the access to Amazon S3 buckets. What will the Solutions Architect do to avoid the time-consuming task of applying the policy to each user?
options: Create a new S3 bucket access policy with unlimited access for each IAM user.
Create a new IAM group and then add the users that require access to the S3 bucket. Afterwards, apply the policy to IAM group.
Create a new IAM role and add each user to the IAM role.
Create a new policy and apply it to multiple IAM users using a shell script.
explain: Create a new IAM Group and add users that require S3 access. Apply the policy to the IAM group. This enables easy management — add/remove users from the group instead of updating each user individually.

q: An insurance company utilizes SAP HANA for its day-to-day ERP operations. Since they can't migrate this database due to customer preferences, they need to integrate it with the current AWS workload in the VPC in which they are required to establish a site-to-site VPN connection. What needs to be configured outside of the VPC for them to have a successful site-to-site VPN connection?
options: An Internet-routable IP address (static) of the customer gateway's external interface for the on-premises network
A dedicated NAT instance in a public subnet
An EIP to the Virtual Private Gateway
The main route table in your VPC to route traffic through a NAT instance
explain: To create a VPN connection, you must create a customer gateway resource in AWS. You need to set up an Internet-routable IP address (static) of the customer gateway's external interface for the on-premises network.

q: A company runs a read-heavy e-commerce application backed by Amazon RDS for MySQL. During flash sales, the database receives thousands of read queries per second, causing latency spikes above 2 seconds. The development team wants to reduce read latency to under 100 milliseconds with minimal application code changes. Which solution should a solutions architect recommend?
options: Add Amazon ElastiCache for Redis in front of RDS and cache frequently read product and inventory data with a TTL of 5 minutes
Increase the RDS instance size to a memory-optimized db.r6g.16xlarge to handle the peak read throughput
Enable RDS Multi-AZ and direct all read traffic to the standby instance to distribute the read load
Partition the MySQL database into multiple RDS instances by product category and shard reads across them
explain: ElastiCache for Redis sits in front of RDS and serves cached reads in sub-millisecond latency, dramatically reducing pressure on the database during flash sales. Multi-AZ standby instances do not serve read traffic — they are purely for failover. Upsizing the instance is expensive and still bound by single-instance throughput limits. Manual sharding adds significant application complexity and operational overhead.
hint: Cache hot data outside the database

q: A gaming company uses Amazon DynamoDB to store player session data. As the player base grows, the team notices that certain popular game items are causing hot partition issues, resulting in ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors. The items are accessed millions of times per hour. Which approach should a solutions architect recommend to eliminate hot partitions with the least application redesign?
options: Add Amazon DAX as a caching layer in front of DynamoDB to absorb the high read traffic for hot items
Create a Global Secondary Index on the item attribute to distribute reads across multiple partitions
Switch the DynamoDB table to on-demand capacity mode so it automatically scales to handle any request rate
Increase the provisioned read capacity units on the DynamoDB table to 100,000 RCUs
explain: DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) is an in-memory cache purpose-built for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond read latency and absorbs hot-key traffic before it reaches DynamoDB, eliminating throttling without changing application logic beyond pointing to the DAX endpoint. On-demand mode helps with unpredictable scaling but does not eliminate hot partitions for extremely popular keys. A GSI does not fix hot partition issues for the same key. Simply adding RCUs does not solve the fundamental hot-partition problem when a single key receives disproportionate traffic.
hint: Purpose-built DynamoDB cache

q: A media streaming company stores video metadata in Amazon DynamoDB. Analysts run complex ad-hoc queries filtering on multiple non-key attributes such as genre, release year, and director. These queries are slow and consume many read capacity units. The table has a partition key of video_id. Which solution provides the best query performance for the analysts' access patterns?
options: Export the DynamoDB table to Amazon S3 using DynamoDB export and query it with Amazon Athena
Use DynamoDB PartiQL to run SQL-compatible queries directly on the table without creating additional indexes
Enable DynamoDB Streams and use AWS Lambda to replicate data to Amazon RDS for MySQL where analysts can run SQL queries
Create Global Secondary Indexes on the genre, release_year, and director attributes to support efficient filtered queries
explain: Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs) allow DynamoDB to efficiently serve queries on non-key attributes. By creating GSIs on genre, release_year, and director, analysts can query these attributes directly without full table scans, dramatically reducing latency and RCU consumption. Replicating to RDS adds operational complexity and latency. Exporting to S3 and querying with Athena introduces batch delay and is not suitable for real-time lookups. PartiQL without indexes still performs full table scans, offering no performance improvement.
hint: Index non-key attributes for efficient queries

q: A SaaS company's API handles 50,000 requests per second during business hours. The backend is Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL, and the application uses short-lived Lambda functions to process each request. The team observes that RDS frequently exhausts its connection limit because each Lambda invocation opens a new database connection. Which solution resolves this with minimal operational overhead?
options: Implement connection pooling within each Lambda function using PgBouncer running as a Lambda layer
Replace RDS with Amazon DynamoDB so that connection management is no longer a concern
Increase the max_connections parameter on the RDS instance by switching to a larger instance class
Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy between the Lambda functions and RDS to pool and reuse database connections
explain: RDS Proxy is designed specifically for serverless and high-concurrency workloads — it pools database connections and multiplexes thousands of Lambda connections into a much smaller pool of persistent RDS connections, staying well within the connection limit. Increasing the instance size raises the limit but does not fundamentally solve connection exhaustion with Lambda's ephemeral nature. Running PgBouncer as a Lambda layer is technically complex and does not provide centralized pooling. Replacing RDS with DynamoDB requires significant application redesign.
hint: Managed connection pooler for serverless-to-RDS

q: A financial services company runs an Aurora MySQL cluster with one writer and two reader instances. During market hours, the reader instances are overwhelmed with analytical queries from internal dashboards. The team wants to automatically add read capacity during peak hours and remove it during off-hours to control costs. Which solution should a solutions architect implement?
options: Enable Aurora Auto Scaling with a target metric on the average CPU utilization of reader instances to automatically add and remove replicas
Configure Application Auto Scaling on the Aurora cluster using a custom CloudWatch metric based on active database connections
Deploy ElastiCache for Redis in front of Aurora readers and use cache-aside pattern to reduce the query load on reader instances
Use AWS Lambda with a scheduled EventBridge rule to call the RDS API and manually create and delete Aurora replicas at fixed times
explain: Aurora Auto Scaling automatically adds and removes Aurora Replicas based on CloudWatch metrics such as average CPU utilization or connections, providing elastic read capacity with no manual intervention. Using Lambda with scheduled rules is a brittle, time-based approach that does not respond to actual load. Application Auto Scaling with custom metrics requires additional metric publishing infrastructure. ElastiCache reduces load but does not dynamically scale the Aurora reader tier itself.
hint: Native Aurora feature for scaling reader replicas

q: A startup is building a real-time leaderboard for a mobile game. The leaderboard must rank millions of players by score with sub-millisecond read latency and support efficient range queries to retrieve top-N players. The solution must scale without manual capacity management. Which AWS service is most appropriate?
options: Amazon ElastiCache for Redis using a sorted set data structure to store player scores and retrieve top-N rankings
Amazon DynamoDB with a GSI on the score attribute and scan operations filtered by rank range
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to ingest score updates and process rankings with AWS Lambda
Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with an index on the score column and an ORDER BY query
explain: ElastiCache for Redis natively supports sorted sets (ZSETs), which store members with scores and allow O(log N) rank queries and O(log N + M) range queries — exactly the pattern needed for leaderboards. It delivers sub-millisecond latency and scales horizontally with cluster mode. DynamoDB GSI scans for ranking are inefficient and expensive. RDS with ORDER BY works but cannot achieve sub-millisecond latency at scale. Kinesis is a streaming ingestion service, not a query engine.
hint: Redis sorted sets are ideal for ranking workloads

q: A company delivers static assets including images, CSS, and JavaScript to a global user base from an Amazon S3 bucket in us-east-1. Users in Asia report page load times exceeding 4 seconds. The company wants to reduce latency for all users worldwide to under 500 milliseconds without changing the origin infrastructure. Which solution should a solutions architect recommend?
options: Replicate the S3 bucket to multiple AWS regions using S3 Cross-Region Replication and use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users to the nearest bucket
Create an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as the origin and configure appropriate cache behaviors for static assets
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket and update application URLs to use the accelerate endpoint
Move the static assets to an EC2 instance in each AWS region and use Route 53 geolocation routing to serve users from the nearest region
explain: CloudFront is a globally distributed CDN with edge locations in every major region, including Asia. By caching static assets at the edge, subsequent requests are served locally with sub-100ms latency, far below the 500ms target. S3 CRR with latency routing reduces origin distance but still serves from S3 rather than a true edge cache. Running EC2 in every region is costly and operationally complex. S3 Transfer Acceleration improves upload speeds from clients to S3, not download speeds to end users.
hint: CDN caches assets at global edge locations

q: An e-commerce company uses Amazon CloudFront in front of an Application Load Balancer serving dynamic product pages. The product catalog changes infrequently — at most once per hour. The team wants to reduce origin load during peak traffic while ensuring users never see stale content older than 1 hour. Which CloudFront configuration achieves this?
options: Use Lambda@Edge at the viewer request event to check a DynamoDB table for content freshness before serving cached responses
Set the CloudFront cache behavior TTL to a maximum of 3600 seconds and configure the origin to send Cache-Control: max-age=3600 headers
Enable CloudFront Origin Shield to add an additional caching layer between edge locations and the ALB origin
Configure CloudFront with a default TTL of 0 and rely on conditional GET requests with ETags to validate freshness on every request
explain: Setting the maximum TTL to 3600 seconds and having the origin send Cache-Control: max-age=3600 ensures CloudFront caches content for exactly one hour, reducing origin requests significantly while guaranteeing freshness. Origin Shield adds an extra caching layer but does not control per-object TTL. Lambda@Edge for freshness checks adds latency and complexity when a simple TTL accomplishes the same goal. A TTL of 0 forces validation on every request, providing no caching benefit.
hint: Cache-Control headers control CloudFront TTL

q: A company has a CloudFront distribution serving content from an Amazon S3 origin in us-east-1 to users across multiple AWS regions. The operations team notices that cache hit ratios at regional edge locations are low because each edge location independently fetches from the origin. The company wants to improve the cache hit ratio and reduce origin traffic without changing the application. Which CloudFront feature should be enabled?
options: Enable CloudFront field-level encryption to allow edge locations to share encrypted cache entries
Enable CloudFront Origin Shield in the AWS region closest to the S3 origin to consolidate origin fetches through a single additional caching layer
Use CloudFront signed URLs with a short expiry so edge locations cache objects for the duration of the signed URL validity
Configure multiple origins in the CloudFront distribution using S3 buckets in each region to serve local edge caches
explain: Origin Shield adds a centralized caching layer between CloudFront regional edge caches and the origin. When a regional edge cache misses, it checks Origin Shield before going to the origin, dramatically increasing the consolidated cache hit ratio and reducing origin requests. Multiple origins per region would require application changes and duplicated data. Field-level encryption is a security feature unrelated to cache hit ratios. Signed URLs control access, not cache consolidation.
hint: Origin Shield consolidates cache misses before hitting the origin

q: A news website serves personalized content based on the reader's country and subscription tier. The personalization logic is lightweight but must run as close to the user as possible to minimize latency. The backend runs on Amazon EC2 behind an ALB. Which solution adds per-request personalization with the lowest latency overhead?
options: Use Amazon API Gateway with a Lambda authorizer to inspect the request and add personalization headers before routing to the EC2 backend
Deploy a Lambda@Edge function at the viewer request event to read cookies for country and subscription tier and rewrite the request URL before forwarding to the origin
Configure an Application Load Balancer listener rule to route requests based on HTTP headers representing country and subscription
Deploy an AWS WAF rule with custom conditions to rewrite request headers based on the viewer's IP geolocation
explain: Lambda@Edge runs at CloudFront edge locations globally, executing within milliseconds of the user's request. A viewer request trigger can inspect cookies, add headers, or rewrite URLs to select personalized content without a round-trip to the origin. API Gateway with Lambda authorizer runs in a single region, adding cross-region latency. WAF does not support header rewriting for personalization. ALB rules route based on existing headers but cannot generate personalization logic.
hint: Run logic at the edge closest to users

q: A company processes sensor data from IoT devices using Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. The stream has 10 shards and a single consumer application running on EC2. As the data volume grows, the consumer cannot keep up with the incoming record rate, causing processing lag. The company wants to allow multiple consumer applications to each process the full stream in parallel without increasing shard count. Which feature should be enabled?
options: Enable Enhanced Fan-Out on the Kinesis Data Stream so each registered consumer receives its own dedicated 2 MB/s throughput per shard
Use Kinesis Data Firehose instead of Kinesis Data Streams to deliver data to multiple destinations simultaneously
Deploy multiple EC2 instances each running the same Kinesis client library consumer pointing to the same stream shard assignments
Increase the number of shards from 10 to 50 to provide more read throughput for the existing consumer application
explain: Enhanced Fan-Out provides each registered consumer with a dedicated 2 MB/s read throughput per shard using HTTP/2 push, regardless of how many other consumers are reading the stream. This allows multiple independent consumer applications to process the full stream simultaneously without competing for the standard 2 MB/s shared read limit. Increasing shard count helps with write throughput, not multiple consumer parallelism. Firehose is a delivery service, not a multi-consumer fan-out mechanism. Multiple EC2 instances sharing shard assignments still compete for the same shared throughput.
hint: Dedicated per-consumer throughput in Kinesis

q: A data engineering team ingests clickstream data into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams and processes it with a Kinesis Client Library (KCL) application on EC2. During traffic spikes, the KCL application falls behind and records approach the 24-hour retention limit before processing. The company needs a scalable, managed solution to process records reliably at scale. Which approach should a solutions architect recommend?
options: Replace Kinesis with Amazon SQS so that messages can be consumed at any rate without falling behind
Use AWS Lambda with the Kinesis trigger to process records in parallel, setting the parallelization factor to process multiple batches per shard concurrently
Deploy the KCL application on AWS Fargate with auto scaling based on a custom CloudWatch metric for iterator age
Increase the Kinesis Data Stream retention period to 7 days so records are not lost during processing lag
explain: Deploying KCL on Fargate with auto scaling based on GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds allows the processing tier to scale out automatically when lag increases, reducing iterator age and catching up with the stream. Lambda with parallelization factor is a valid option for simpler processing but KCL on Fargate is recommended when complex stateful processing or exactly-once semantics are required. Extending retention to 7 days delays data loss but does not reduce processing lag. Replacing Kinesis with SQS changes the streaming paradigm and loses ordering guarantees.
hint: Scale consumers based on iterator age metric

q: A company hosts a REST API on Amazon API Gateway backed by AWS Lambda. During peak hours, thousands of requests per second arrive for the same set of 10 product detail endpoints. The API responses rarely change — at most once per 5 minutes. The team wants to reduce Lambda invocations and improve response times without changing the Lambda code. Which solution should be implemented?
options: Use provisioned concurrency on the Lambda function to keep instances warm and reduce cold start latency
Add an ElastiCache for Redis cluster inside the Lambda function to cache responses and reuse them across invocations
Enable API Gateway caching with a TTL of 300 seconds on the GET methods for the product detail endpoints
Deploy an Amazon CloudFront distribution in front of API Gateway and configure a cache behavior for the product endpoints
explain: API Gateway has a built-in caching feature that stores endpoint responses in a dedicated cache. Setting a TTL of 300 seconds on the GET product endpoints means API Gateway serves cached responses for up to 5 minutes without invoking Lambda at all, directly reducing invocations and improving response times. CloudFront in front of API Gateway also works but adds infrastructure complexity. ElastiCache inside Lambda requires code changes and still invokes Lambda on every request. Provisioned concurrency reduces cold starts but does not cache responses.
hint: API Gateway has built-in response caching

q: A company builds a WebSocket-based chat application that needs to push messages to thousands of connected clients in real time. The application runs on AWS Lambda. The team wants to use a fully managed WebSocket service so they do not have to manage connection state on servers. Which AWS service supports this requirement?
options: AWS AppSync with subscriptions over GraphQL to push real-time data updates to connected clients
Amazon SQS with long polling, where each client polls its own queue for incoming messages with a 20-second wait time
Amazon SNS with HTTP subscriptions to push notifications to each client endpoint registered during connection
Amazon API Gateway WebSocket APIs, which maintain persistent connections and allow Lambda to push messages to connected clients using the connection ID
explain: API Gateway WebSocket APIs manage persistent WebSocket connections natively. Each client gets a unique connection ID, and Lambda can send messages to any connected client by calling the API Gateway Management API with the target connection ID. SQS long polling requires each client to actively poll — it does not push messages. SNS HTTP subscriptions push to registered URLs but cannot maintain client WebSocket connections. AppSync subscriptions are valid for GraphQL real-time use cases but add schema and query complexity not required for a simple chat application.
hint: API Gateway manages WebSocket connection state

q: An online retail company uses Amazon SQS to decouple order processing. The order processor Lambda function takes up to 30 seconds to process each message. The team notices that messages are being processed multiple times, causing duplicate orders. Which SQS configuration change should a solutions architect make to prevent duplicate processing?
options: Enable SQS FIFO queue and use message deduplication IDs to detect and discard duplicate messages within a 5-minute window
Increase the SQS queue visibility timeout to at least 6 times the Lambda function's maximum processing time, setting it to at least 180 seconds
Reduce the Lambda concurrency limit to 1 to ensure only one message is processed at a time
Enable long polling on the SQS queue and set the ReceiveMessageWaitTimeSeconds to 20 seconds
explain: The visibility timeout determines how long a message is hidden from other consumers after being received. If processing takes longer than the visibility timeout, the message becomes visible again and is picked up by another worker, causing duplicates. Setting the visibility timeout to at least 6x the function timeout (180 seconds for a 30-second function) ensures the message remains hidden throughout processing. FIFO with deduplication IDs prevents duplicate sends but not duplicate processing due to timeout expiry. Limiting concurrency to 1 serializes processing but does not prevent the timeout-driven duplication. Long polling reduces empty receives but does not affect visibility.
hint: Visibility timeout must exceed processing time

q: A company processes financial transactions using Amazon SQS Standard queues. Transaction messages must be processed in the exact order they are received, and each transaction must be processed exactly once. The current standard queue cannot guarantee ordering. Which change should the solutions architect implement?
options: Enable SQS message delay with a DelaySeconds value of 0 to ensure messages are processed in the order they arrive
Migrate to an Amazon SQS FIFO queue with content-based deduplication enabled and process messages using a single consumer per message group ID
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams instead of SQS to guarantee ordering within a shard using partition keys per transaction account
Add sequence numbers to each message body and implement ordering logic in the consumer application to reorder messages before processing
explain: SQS FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and strict first-in, first-out ordering within a message group. Using the transaction account ID as the message group ID ensures all transactions for a given account are processed in order. Content-based deduplication eliminates duplicate sends automatically. Implementing ordering in the consumer is complex and error-prone. DelaySeconds does not affect message ordering. Kinesis provides ordering per shard but requires shard management and does not provide the built-in exactly-once guarantee that FIFO offers.
hint: FIFO queues guarantee ordering and exactly-once processing

q: A company's Lambda function processes SQS messages and writes results to Amazon DynamoDB. The function currently retrieves one message at a time, but processing volume has increased. The team wants to increase throughput by having Lambda process multiple messages per invocation without changing the function's core logic. Which configuration achieves this?
options: Increase the Lambda event source mapping batch size from 1 to 10 so that Lambda retrieves up to 10 SQS messages per invocation and processes them in the function
Enable SQS long polling and set ReceiveMessageWaitTimeSeconds to 20 to accumulate messages before Lambda is triggered
Create 10 separate Lambda functions each subscribing to the same SQS queue to process messages in parallel
Use Lambda Destinations to chain multiple Lambda invocations for batch aggregation before final DynamoDB writes
explain: The Lambda event source mapping batch size controls how many SQS messages are included in a single Lambda invocation. Increasing it to 10 allows Lambda to process 10 messages per invocation, improving throughput by reducing invocation overhead and amortizing the DynamoDB write cost across more messages. Multiple Lambda functions on the same queue compete for messages and do not guarantee batching. Long polling affects when Lambda is triggered, not batch size. Lambda Destinations are for routing success/failure events, not batching.
hint: Event source mapping batch size controls messages per invocation

q: A company migrates a monolithic application to microservices on Amazon ECS with Fargate. One service handles image resizing and can receive burst traffic up to 500 concurrent tasks. The company wants Fargate tasks to scale out automatically in under 2 minutes during traffic spikes. Which configuration should a solutions architect implement?
options: Configure ECS Service Auto Scaling using Application Auto Scaling with a target tracking policy on the ECSServiceAverageCPUUtilization metric and set scale-out cooldown to 60 seconds
Use AWS Lambda instead of ECS Fargate for the image resizing service so it scales automatically without any configuration
Set the ECS service desired count to 500 permanently to always have capacity available for peak traffic
Create an EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function to call the ECS UpdateService API and increase the desired count when CPU exceeds 70%
explain: ECS Service Auto Scaling with Application Auto Scaling using a target tracking policy on CPU utilization responds automatically to load changes. A 60-second scale-out cooldown ensures new tasks are launched quickly during spikes. Lambda has a 15-minute timeout and memory limits that may not suit heavy image processing. Permanently running 500 tasks wastes money during low traffic. EventBridge with Lambda for manual scaling has operational overhead and slower response than native target tracking.
hint: Target tracking scales ECS services automatically

q: A company runs machine learning inference workloads on Amazon EKS. The inference pods need GPUs but the GPU node pool is expensive to run continuously. The team wants to scale GPU nodes to zero when not in use and scale back up when inference requests arrive. Which Kubernetes scaling mechanism on EKS supports this requirement?
options: Use the EKS Cluster Autoscaler to scale GPU node groups based on pending pod requests when existing nodes reach capacity
Deploy KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) with an SQS scaler so pods scale from zero to N based on the number of messages in the inference request queue
Configure Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) on the inference deployment with CPU utilization as the scaling metric
Use AWS Auto Scaling groups with scheduled scaling to pre-provision GPU nodes before predicted peak hours
explain: KEDA enables scale-from-zero by driving pod replicas from external event sources such as SQS queue depth. When the queue is empty, KEDA scales pods to zero, eliminating GPU node cost. When messages arrive, KEDA scales pods up, triggering the Cluster Autoscaler to add GPU nodes. The Cluster Autoscaler alone cannot scale to zero — it only adds nodes when pending pods exist. HPA requires running pods to generate CPU metrics, so it cannot scale from zero. Scheduled scaling requires predictable traffic patterns and does not react to actual queue depth.
hint: KEDA enables scale-from-zero based on event sources

q: A company's data warehouse on Amazon Redshift runs slowly during business hours when many analysts submit concurrent queries. The cluster already uses the largest available node type and further resizing is not cost-justified. The company wants to handle concurrent query spikes automatically. Which Redshift feature should be enabled?
options: Enable Redshift AQUA (Advanced Query Accelerator) to push computation closer to storage and reduce query execution time
Configure Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with multiple queues and assign analyst queries to a queue with higher memory allocation
Use Redshift Spectrum to offload ad-hoc queries to Amazon S3 so they do not compete with regular warehouse queries
Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling so that Redshift automatically adds transient capacity to handle query bursts and users experience consistent performance
explain: Concurrency Scaling automatically adds temporary Redshift cluster capacity when query concurrency increases, routing overflow queries to the additional clusters. Users see consistent performance without manual intervention. WLM with queues manages query prioritization but does not add compute capacity. Redshift Spectrum is for querying S3 data, not for scaling concurrent warehouse queries. AQUA accelerates specific query patterns but does not add concurrency capacity during bursts.
hint: Redshift adds transient clusters during concurrency spikes

q: A company loads terabytes of data into Amazon Redshift daily from Amazon S3. The COPY command takes over 4 hours, and the team wants to reduce load time significantly. The source files in S3 are currently stored as a single large CSV file. Which optimization reduces the COPY time the most?
options: Schedule the COPY operation during off-peak hours so it does not compete with analyst queries for cluster resources
Split the large CSV file into many smaller files matching a multiple of the number of Redshift slice count and store them in columnar format such as Parquet
Enable Redshift AQUA to parallelize the COPY ingestion operation across storage nodes
Use Amazon EMR to pre-process and compress the CSV file with gzip before copying it into Redshift
explain: Redshift COPY performs best when the source data is split into files that match a multiple of the total number of slices across all nodes. This allows Redshift to load files in parallel across all slices simultaneously. Using Parquet (columnar format) further improves load speed by reducing data volume. Gzip compression helps but a single large file still cannot be parallelized during COPY. Scheduling off-peak avoids contention but does not speed up the load itself. AQUA accelerates query execution, not COPY ingestion.
hint: Split files by slice count for parallel COPY

q: A company stores 500 TB of log data in Amazon S3 in JSON format. Data analysts run ad-hoc SQL queries on this data using Amazon Athena. Query performance is poor because Athena scans the entire dataset for most queries. The team wants to dramatically reduce query times and costs without moving data to a different service. Which optimization should be implemented?
options: Convert the JSON data to Parquet or ORC columnar format, partition the data by date, and create an AWS Glue Data Catalog table to allow Athena to use partition pruning
Enable Athena query result caching so repeated identical queries are served from cache without scanning S3
Create an Athena workgroup with a query result location in the same S3 region to reduce cross-region data transfer latency
Use Amazon Redshift Spectrum to query the S3 data instead of Athena, as Spectrum uses more powerful compute nodes
explain: Columnar formats like Parquet allow Athena to skip columns not referenced in the query, reducing data scanned. Partitioning by date enables partition pruning so Athena only scans relevant date ranges. Together these optimizations typically reduce data scanned by 80-99%, cutting both query time and cost. Query result caching only helps for repeated identical queries. Workgroup location settings affect result storage, not query performance. Redshift Spectrum queries the same S3 data but adds complexity and cost without addressing the root scan issue.
hint: Columnar format + partitioning reduces Athena data scanned

q: A solutions architect is designing an event-driven ETL pipeline. Raw events arrive in Amazon S3. The team wants to query and transform only new events without reprocessing the entire dataset each run. Amazon Athena is used for transformations. Which mechanism should be used to efficiently identify and query only new data?
options: Enable S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function that appends new file paths to a DynamoDB table which Athena queries via Federated Query
Use Amazon S3 Inventory to generate a daily list of all objects and compare it against the previous day's inventory to find new files
Configure S3 Lifecycle policies to move processed objects to S3 Glacier so Athena only sees unprocessed objects in the standard tier
Partition the S3 data by date and hour, use AWS Glue Crawlers to update the Data Catalog, and run Athena queries filtered to the latest partition
explain: Partitioning by date and hour combined with Glue Crawlers that update the catalog after each ingestion allows Athena to use WHERE clauses on partition columns to scan only the latest partition. This is the standard, low-overhead approach for incremental processing. S3 Inventory is a batch report useful for auditing, not for real-time incremental ETL filtering. Lambda + DynamoDB + Federated Query adds unnecessary complexity. S3 Lifecycle policies change storage class but do not affect Athena query filtering.
hint: Partitions enable Athena to skip old data

q: A company uses Amazon OpenSearch Service to power product search on its e-commerce site. As the catalog grows to 50 million documents, the cluster becomes expensive because all documents must remain in hot storage for sub-second search latency. Historical products older than 90 days are rarely searched. Which OpenSearch feature reduces storage costs for older data while keeping it queryable?
options: Delete indices older than 90 days using Index State Management lifecycle policies to reclaim hot storage
Enable cross-cluster search to split the catalogue across two OpenSearch clusters and route queries to the appropriate cluster based on document age
Use OpenSearch Serverless for old document indices, which automatically tiers data based on access patterns
Move indices older than 90 days to UltraWarm storage, which uses S3-backed warm nodes at a fraction of hot storage cost while still allowing search
explain: UltraWarm is an OpenSearch Service storage tier backed by Amazon S3 with local SSD caching. It costs significantly less than hot storage and keeps data fully searchable. Moving old indices to UltraWarm reduces cost while preserving query access. Deleting old indices removes them permanently, making them unsearchable. Cross-cluster search still requires running a second full hot cluster for old data. OpenSearch Serverless is a different deployment mode and does not automatically migrate existing domain indices.
hint: UltraWarm is OpenSearch's warm storage tier

q: A company uses Amazon DynamoDB in on-demand capacity mode to handle unpredictable traffic. During a product launch, traffic spikes to 10x the normal rate within seconds. DynamoDB throttles requests, and the team observes a spike in ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors despite using on-demand mode. What is the most likely cause and the correct remediation?
options: On-demand mode can double its previous peak capacity in 30 minutes; pre-warm the table by performing a load test or temporarily switching to provisioned mode with higher capacity before the launch
On-demand mode has no scaling limits and the errors indicate a bug in the application SDK retry logic that needs to be fixed
Switch to provisioned capacity mode and set read and write capacity units to the expected peak values before the launch
Enable DynamoDB Streams to absorb write traffic and process it asynchronously to prevent throttling during the spike
explain: DynamoDB on-demand mode scales automatically but can only double its previous traffic peak within a 30-minute window. A 10x spike during a launch can exceed this scaling envelope and cause throttling. The correct approach is to pre-warm the table — either by running a gradual load test to establish a higher traffic baseline or by temporarily using provisioned mode with target capacity set ahead of the launch. On-demand mode does have limits related to previous peaks. Switching to provisioned mode with exact capacity values is a valid alternative but less flexible. DynamoDB Streams handles change data capture, not write throttling.
hint: On-demand doubles previous peak; pre-warm before launches

q: A solutions architect is designing a data access layer for a multi-tenant SaaS application backed by Amazon DynamoDB. Each tenant's data must be isolated, and queries must retrieve all items for a given tenant efficiently. The table has millions of items across thousands of tenants. Which data modeling approach provides the best performance for per-tenant queries?
options: Use tenant_id as the partition key and a sort key on a meaningful attribute such as created_at so that all items for a tenant are co-located in the same partition
Create a separate DynamoDB table for each tenant to provide complete data isolation and maximum query performance
Create a Global Secondary Index with tenant_id as the index partition key and replicate all attributes as projected attributes
Use a single partition key for all tenants and rely on DynamoDB FilterExpression to retrieve tenant-specific items
explain: Using tenant_id as the partition key ensures all items for a tenant are stored in the same partition, enabling efficient Query operations without scans. Adding a sort key allows range queries and ordering within a tenant's items. Separate tables per tenant is a valid isolation approach but creates management overhead at thousands of tenants and hits account-level limits. FilterExpression on a single shared partition key performs full table scans. A GSI with tenant_id works but is redundant if the base table already uses tenant_id as the partition key.
hint: Partition key determines data co-location in DynamoDB

q: A company runs an Aurora PostgreSQL cluster. A critical nightly batch job generates reports by running dozens of complex analytical queries against the same cluster used by the OLTP application. During batch hours, OLTP query latency doubles. The team wants to isolate batch workload from OLTP with minimum operational overhead. Which solution should be implemented?
options: Run the batch job during off-peak hours at 3 AM when OLTP traffic is minimal to avoid resource contention
Export Aurora data to Amazon S3 and use Amazon Athena to run the batch analytical queries against the S3 export
Add a dedicated Aurora read replica and direct all batch reporting queries to the replica endpoint, keeping OLTP queries on the writer endpoint
Increase the Aurora cluster's instance size to a db.r6g.16xlarge to provide enough compute for both workloads simultaneously
explain: Aurora read replicas share the same underlying storage as the writer but have their own compute resources. Directing analytical queries to a dedicated reader instance isolates them from OLTP queries on the writer, eliminating resource contention. Upsizing the instance helps but does not fundamentally separate workloads and is expensive. Scheduling at 3 AM defers the problem rather than solving it and may not fit business requirements. Exporting to S3 and using Athena adds data freshness latency and requires additional workflow management.
hint: Dedicated reader instance isolates analytical from OLTP workloads

q: A company operates a global e-commerce platform and wants to provide low-latency database access for users in the US, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Write operations occur in the US and must be readable in all regions within seconds. The company needs a fully managed solution that supports automatic failover for the writer region. Which database architecture should be used?
options: Deploy separate Aurora clusters in each region and use AWS Database Migration Service to replicate changes continuously across clusters
Deploy an Aurora Global Database with the primary cluster in us-east-1 and secondary clusters in eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1, using the read endpoint in each region
Use Amazon DynamoDB Global Tables with active-active replication across three regions so all regions can serve reads and writes
Deploy RDS for MySQL with Multi-AZ in us-east-1 and manually replicate data to EC2-hosted MySQL instances in Europe and Asia
explain: Aurora Global Database replicates data from the primary region to up to 5 secondary regions with typical replication lag under 1 second. Each secondary cluster provides read endpoints for local users, and in the event of a regional failure, a secondary can be promoted to primary in under 1 minute. Manual EC2 replication lacks management features and reliability. DynamoDB Global Tables with active-active is excellent for DynamoDB workloads but the company's application uses Aurora/relational. DMS for cross-cluster replication adds latency and operational complexity compared to native Global Database.
hint: Aurora Global Database provides sub-second cross-region replication

q: A company wants to migrate from Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached to ElastiCache for Redis. The current Memcached cluster is used for session storage and simple key-value caching. The team wants to know what additional capabilities Redis provides over Memcached that justify the migration. Which capability is exclusive to Redis and not available in Memcached?
options: Redis provides in-memory caching with microsecond latency for key-value data, which Memcached cannot match
Redis supports data persistence through RDB snapshots and AOF logging, and provides native pub/sub messaging, sorted sets, and replication with automatic failover
Redis integrates with Amazon CloudWatch for monitoring cache hit rates and eviction metrics, which Memcached does not support
Redis supports horizontal scaling by adding more nodes to the cluster, while Memcached only supports vertical scaling by upgrading node size
explain: Redis offers capabilities not present in Memcached: persistence via RDB snapshots and AOF (append-only file), replication with automatic failover (Multi-AZ), pub/sub messaging, sorted sets, lists, hashes, and other rich data structures. Memcached supports multi-threaded horizontal scaling but does not offer persistence, replication, or complex data structures. Both Memcached and Redis support horizontal scaling via sharding. Both support sub-millisecond latency. Both integrate with CloudWatch.
hint: Redis adds persistence, replication, and rich data structures

q: A company's Lambda function experiences cold starts of 3-4 seconds when processing sporadic requests, causing poor user experience. The function is written in Java and uses a large Spring Boot framework. The team wants to reduce cold start latency to under 500 milliseconds for a subset of critical functions handling production traffic. Which solution best addresses this with minimum code changes?
options: Use a CloudWatch Events rule to invoke the Lambda function every 5 minutes to keep it warm and prevent cold starts
Rewrite the function in Python or Node.js which have significantly lower JVM startup overhead than Java
Increase the Lambda function memory allocation to 10 GB, which proportionally increases CPU and reduces initialization time
Enable Lambda SnapStart for the Java function, which snapshots the initialized execution environment and restores from snapshot on cold starts
explain: Lambda SnapStart is specifically designed for Java functions and addresses the JVM initialization overhead by taking a snapshot of the fully initialized execution environment after the init phase. On subsequent cold starts, Lambda restores from the snapshot instead of re-initializing, reducing cold start latency by up to 90% with no code changes required beyond enabling the feature. Increasing memory reduces CPU-bound initialization but does not eliminate framework loading time. Keep-warm pings are unreliable and waste compute. Rewriting in Python requires significant code changes and testing effort.
hint: SnapStart snapshots initialized Java Lambda environments

q: A company processes large datasets using AWS Lambda. The current function has a 128 MB memory limit and takes 45 seconds per invocation. After profiling, the team finds the function is CPU-bound, spending most time on data transformation. They want to reduce execution duration to under 10 seconds. Which change should be made first?
options: Break the function into smaller Lambda functions and use Step Functions to orchestrate parallel processing of data chunks
Enable Lambda Provisioned Concurrency to keep execution environments warm and eliminate cold start overhead
Increase Lambda memory allocation to 1,792 MB or higher, because Lambda allocates CPU proportional to memory and more CPU will reduce the CPU-bound processing time
Switch to Lambda container images with a custom runtime that includes native C++ libraries for faster data processing
explain: Lambda allocates CPU power linearly with memory. At 1,792 MB, a Lambda function receives one full vCPU. For CPU-bound workloads, increasing memory directly increases processing speed. This is the simplest change — no code modification required. Container images with native libraries require significant refactoring. Provisioned concurrency eliminates cold starts but does not speed up execution for CPU-bound work. Step Functions parallelism is effective but requires architectural changes; memory optimization should be tried first.
hint: Lambda CPU scales linearly with memory allocation

q: A company uses Amazon EMR to run Apache Spark jobs that process daily logs stored in Amazon S3. The jobs take 6 hours to complete. The operations team wants to reduce runtime to under 2 hours while controlling costs. The jobs are fault-tolerant and can be retried if interrupted. Which EMR configuration reduces runtime and cost simultaneously?
options: Enable EMR Managed Scaling to automatically add nodes during the job and remove them after completion
Switch from EMR on EC2 to EMR Serverless to eliminate cluster management and automatically provision the required compute
Use an EMR instance fleet with a mix of On-Demand master/core nodes and Spot instance task nodes to add low-cost compute capacity that accelerates Spark parallelism
Use all On-Demand instances with the largest instance type available to maximize cluster CPU and memory
explain: Spot instance task nodes can be 70-90% cheaper than On-Demand and can be added in large numbers to increase Spark parallelism. Because Spark jobs are fault-tolerant and can retry failed tasks, Spot interruptions are handled gracefully. Keeping On-Demand for master and core nodes ensures cluster stability. All On-Demand with large instances maximizes performance but at higher cost. EMR Managed Scaling helps with right-sizing but does not explicitly add Spot task nodes for acceleration. EMR Serverless is valid but optimizing the existing fleet configuration is simpler.
hint: Spot task nodes add cheap parallelism to EMR Spark jobs

q: A company uploads large video files (10-50 GB each) to Amazon S3 from on-premises servers. Uploads frequently fail midway due to network interruptions, forcing complete restarts. The team wants to make uploads resumable and reduce total transfer time. Which S3 feature should be used?
options: Use AWS DataSync to synchronize the on-premises video directory to S3, which automatically handles resumable transfers
Use S3 Multipart Upload to split each video into parts of 100 MB, upload parts in parallel, and resume from the last successful part after an interruption
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to route uploads through CloudFront edge locations for faster data transfer to S3
Configure an S3 Lifecycle policy to automatically retry failed uploads after 24 hours using the incomplete multipart upload expiration rule
explain: S3 Multipart Upload splits large objects into parts that are uploaded independently and in parallel. If a part fails, only that part needs to be retried, not the entire object. This makes uploads resumable after interruptions and significantly faster through parallelism. S3 Transfer Acceleration reduces latency by routing through edge locations but does not make uploads resumable. DataSync handles resumable sync but adds agent infrastructure overhead. Lifecycle policies can clean up incomplete multipart uploads but do not implement the upload resumption mechanism itself.
hint: Multipart Upload enables parallel, resumable S3 uploads

q: A company runs an analytics platform that queries large Parquet datasets stored in Amazon S3 using Amazon Athena. Users often need only specific columns from wide tables with 200+ columns. Query costs are high because Athena charges per TB scanned. Which optimization most directly reduces query costs for column-selective queries?
options: Compress the Parquet files with Snappy compression to reduce the data volume scanned per query
Use Athena workgroups with a data usage control limit to enforce per-query scan limits and prevent expensive queries
Store data in Parquet columnar format so Athena reads only the columns referenced in each query, skipping irrelevant columns
Use Athena query result caching to serve repeated queries from cache without scanning S3
explain: Parquet is a columnar storage format. Athena reads only the columns referenced in a SELECT statement, skipping all others. For a 200-column table queried on 5 columns, this can reduce data scanned by 97.5%. This directly reduces both query cost and execution time. Query result caching only helps for repeated identical queries. Snappy compression reduces file size and helps with cost but provides less benefit than column pruning for wide tables. Workgroup scan limits prevent overruns but do not reduce costs for legitimate column-selective queries.
hint: Columnar format skips unneeded columns in Athena queries

q: A company has a microservices application where Service A calls Service B synchronously via HTTP. Service B processes requests in 200-500 ms. Under load, Service B becomes a bottleneck and Service A times out, causing cascading failures. The team wants to decouple the services so Service A is not blocked waiting for Service B. Which architectural change achieves this?
options: Enable HTTP/2 multiplexing between Service A and Service B to allow concurrent requests over a single connection
Increase Service B's timeout threshold to 10 seconds and implement exponential backoff in Service A's retry logic
Deploy Service B behind an Application Load Balancer with multiple instances and configure connection draining to handle in-flight requests
Replace the synchronous HTTP call with an Amazon SQS queue: Service A enqueues requests and returns immediately, Service B polls and processes asynchronously
explain: Replacing synchronous HTTP with SQS decouples the services. Service A enqueues a message and immediately returns to the caller, eliminating blocking. Service B processes messages at its own pace and scales independently. This prevents cascading failures because Service B's slowness no longer blocks Service A. ALB with multiple Service B instances helps with throughput but Service A still waits for a synchronous response. HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces connection overhead but does not eliminate blocking. Increasing timeouts and retries makes cascading failures worse, not better.
hint: SQS decouples synchronous dependencies between services

q: A company wants to implement a global accelerator for its API hosted in us-east-1. Users in Asia and Europe report high latency of 300-400 ms due to long network paths. The API is dynamic and cannot be cached. The company wants to reduce round-trip latency for all global users without redeploying the API in multiple regions. Which service should be used?
options: Direct Connect connections from AWS to major internet exchange points to reduce last-mile latency for global users
Amazon CloudFront with the API Gateway origin and a TTL of 0 to bypass caching and route all requests to the origin through CloudFront's backbone
AWS Global Accelerator with anycast IP addresses routes user traffic to the nearest AWS edge location and tunnels it over the AWS global network backbone to us-east-1, reducing latency
Amazon Route 53 with latency-based routing to direct users to the nearest healthy endpoint, even though all endpoints resolve to the same us-east-1 API
explain: Global Accelerator uses anycast IP addresses that route users to the nearest AWS edge location over the public internet, then forwards traffic over AWS's private global network backbone to the origin in us-east-1. This avoids congested public internet hops across continents, reducing latency by 30-60% for global users. CloudFront with TTL=0 does route through the backbone but is optimized for cacheable content; Global Accelerator is the right tool for dynamic API traffic. Route 53 latency routing with all endpoints in the same region provides no benefit. Direct Connect is for private connectivity from on-premises, not end-user internet traffic.
hint: Global Accelerator routes over AWS backbone for lower latency

q: A company's REST API on Amazon API Gateway is accessed by enterprise customers who have different usage requirements. Some customers need guaranteed throughput while others pay per request. The team wants to enforce per-customer request limits and offer tiered pricing. Which API Gateway feature enables this?
options: Use Lambda authorizers to count each customer's requests in DynamoDB and return a deny response when their monthly quota is exceeded
