options: Use Amazon S3 as a backup store by enabling DynamoDB Streams and a Lambda function that writes changed items to S3 with cross-region replication
Enable DynamoDB point-in-time recovery (PITR) and use a scheduled Lambda function to export the table to S3 cross-region every day

Use AWS Backup with a backup plan that schedules daily backups of the DynamoDB table, specifies a cross-region copy rule to a secondary region, and sets a retention period of 90 days in the secondary region vault
Enable DynamoDB Global Tables to replicate the table to a secondary region and use that region's table as the backup
explain: AWS Backup provides fully managed, policy-driven backups for DynamoDB. A backup plan can schedule daily DynamoDB backups, copy them to a backup vault in a secondary region, and enforce 90-day retention — all without custom code. PITR with Lambda exports requires custom script maintenance. Global Tables provides live replication, not isolated backups — data corruption replicates to all tables. Streams + Lambda is a custom solution with operational overhead and no native 90-day retention management.
hint: AWS Backup provides managed cross-region backup with retention policies

q: A company's application runs on multiple EC2 instances behind an ALB. The ALB currently uses a round-robin algorithm. Some users report session-related errors when their requests are routed to different instances that do not have their session state. The application stores sessions in local memory. The team wants to fix this without re-architecting session storage. Which ALB feature helps?
options: Enable ALB sticky sessions (session affinity) using duration-based or application-based cookies so that requests from the same user are consistently routed to the same target
Deploy all instances in the same AZ and disable cross-zone load balancing to ensure all requests stay local
Use AWS Global Accelerator in front of the ALB to pin users to specific endpoints based on source IP
Switch from round-robin to least outstanding requests algorithm so users are routed to the instance with the fewest active connections
explain: ALB sticky sessions configure the load balancer to route all requests from a given client to the same target using a cookie (either ALB-generated or application-generated). This ensures in-memory session state on one instance is consistently available for that user. Least outstanding requests changes routing fairness but does not provide user affinity. Restricting to one AZ limits availability. Global Accelerator routes to regional endpoints, not individual instances, and does not implement instance-level session affinity.
hint: ALB sticky sessions route users to the same target consistently

q: A company's serverless application uses AWS Step Functions Standard Workflows. During a step that calls an external payment API, the external API occasionally times out. The step needs to wait up to 2 hours for the external payment system to respond before considering the transaction failed. The Step Functions task timeout cannot exceed 15 minutes per invocation. Which pattern handles this asynchronous wait?
options: Use the Step Functions callback pattern with a task token — the task sends the token to the payment API, pauses execution, and the payment API calls back the Step Functions API with the token when complete, up to 1 year later
Implement a polling loop in Step Functions using a Wait state and a Lambda function that checks the payment status every minute for up to 2 hours
Use Step Functions Express Workflows which have no task timeout limit and can wait indefinitely for external callbacks
Configure the Step Functions task with a HeartbeatSeconds of 7200 to allow the task to wait 2 hours before timing out
explain: The Step Functions callback pattern with task tokens is designed exactly for this use case. The Lambda or resource task sends a task token to the external system and Step Functions pauses the execution (waitForTaskToken). When the external payment system completes, it sends a SendTaskSuccess or SendTaskFailure to Step Functions with the token, resuming execution. Tasks can wait up to 1 year. HeartbeatSeconds is a liveness check interval, not a task duration extension. Express Workflows run for 5 minutes maximum, not indefinitely. Polling loops work but waste compute and add complexity compared to the native callback pattern.
hint: Task tokens enable Step Functions to wait for external callbacks

q: A company runs a production RDS for PostgreSQL instance. The database needs to be available at least 99.95% of the time. Planned maintenance and patching sometimes cause unexpected brief outages during business hours. The team wants to control when maintenance operations occur. Which configuration prevents unexpected maintenance during business hours?
options: Create a custom parameter group with aggressive connection settings to minimize downtime during maintenance-initiated restarts
Configure the RDS maintenance window to a recurring low-traffic period outside business hours (e.g., Sunday 02:00-04:00 UTC) so AWS applies maintenance only during the specified window
Disable automatic minor version upgrades on the RDS instance so the team controls when patches are applied
Enable RDS Multi-AZ so that maintenance is applied to the standby first and the primary continues serving traffic, with a brief failover when the primary is updated
explain: RDS Multi-AZ significantly reduces maintenance impact. AWS applies updates to the standby instance first. Once the standby is updated, a failover occurs and the old primary becomes the new standby and is then updated. The failover typically takes 20-60 seconds and occurs outside business hours if the maintenance window is also set to off-peak hours. The maintenance window controls timing but Multi-AZ minimizes disruption during the brief failover. Using both together is best practice. Disabling auto minor version upgrades postpones security patches, creating risk. Parameter group settings do not affect maintenance-triggered restarts.
hint: Multi-AZ applies updates to standby first, minimizing primary downtime

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation to deploy its application stack. The team wants to prevent developers from accidentally modifying or deleting critical production resources such as the RDS database and VPC during stack updates. Which CloudFormation feature enforces this protection?
options: Use CloudFormation change sets so all stack updates require explicit review and approval before being applied
Create a CloudFormation stack policy that explicitly denies Update and Delete actions on the RDS and VPC resources so stack updates cannot modify those resources
Add a DeletionPolicy: Retain attribute to the RDS and VPC resources in the template so they are not deleted if the stack is deleted
Enable CloudFormation termination protection on the stack to prevent the entire stack from being deleted accidentally
explain: Stack policies allow stack owners to define IAM-like policy documents that control which resources can be updated or replaced during stack updates. Explicitly denying Update and Delete actions on critical resources prevents any stack update from modifying them. DeletionPolicy: Retain protects against deletion when the stack is deleted but does not prevent updates to those resources during stack updates. Termination protection prevents the entire stack from being deleted but does not protect individual resources from updates. Change sets require review but do not enforce restrictions — a developer could still approve a change set that modifies critical resources.
hint: Stack policies restrict which resources can be updated in a stack

q: A company is designing a disaster recovery strategy for its on-premises SAP application. The company cannot afford a warm standby in AWS due to cost constraints. The RTO is 4 hours and RPO is 24 hours. Which DR strategy is most appropriate and cost-effective?
options: Implement a backup and restore strategy: back up the SAP application data daily to Amazon S3 and store CloudFormation templates for the AWS environment; restore from backup and launch infrastructure in AWS if the on-premises environment fails, targeting 4-hour RTO
Implement a pilot light strategy with minimal AWS resources running continuously — just the database replicating from on-premises — and launch remaining infrastructure from AMIs during a failure
Implement active-active with the SAP application running in both on-premises and AWS simultaneously, with Route 53 distributing traffic between them
Implement a warm standby with a scaled-down version of the SAP environment running in AWS with real-time data replication from on-premises
explain: Backup and restore is the most cost-effective DR strategy. With a 24-hour RPO, daily backups to S3 satisfy the requirement. Pre-built CloudFormation templates and AMIs reduce restore time. With proper preparation, 4-hour RTO is achievable for a well-automated restore process. Pilot light keeps some infrastructure running, reducing RTO but increasing ongoing cost compared to pure backup/restore. Warm standby runs scaled-down infrastructure continuously — significantly more expensive and unnecessary given the 4-hour RTO tolerance. Active-active is the most expensive option, requiring full duplicated infrastructure.
hint: Backup/restore is lowest cost DR with acceptable RTO/RPO trade-off

q: A company's application uses Amazon EFS for shared file storage across a fleet of EC2 instances in multiple Availability Zones. The EFS file system has not been backed up. A ransomware attack encrypts all files. The company wants to recover the file system to its state 24 hours before the attack with minimum data loss. Which solution enables this recovery?
options: Enable AWS Backup for EFS with a daily backup schedule and 30-day retention; restore the EFS file system from the backup taken 24 hours before the attack to a new EFS file system
Enable EFS Lifecycle Management to automatically transition files to EFS Infrequent Access storage class for preservation
Use AWS DataSync to create an on-demand sync of the EFS data to Amazon S3 and restore from the S3 copy
Enable EFS replication to a secondary EFS file system in another region; restore from the replica which maintains a near-real-time copy
explain: AWS Backup for EFS creates automated, scheduled backups of EFS file systems. A daily backup taken 24 hours before the attack provides an unencrypted restore point. Restoring from this backup to a new EFS file system recovers all files as they were at backup time. EFS replication continuously replicates changes — if ransomware encrypted files on the primary, the encryption is replicated to the secondary within seconds, making the replica equally compromised. Lifecycle Management moves files to cheaper storage but does not create recoverable backups. DataSync on-demand sync does not create point-in-time backups with versioning.
hint: AWS Backup creates point-in-time EFS backups for recovery

q: A company's application publishes messages to Amazon SNS which fans out to 5 SQS queues. One downstream service's SQS queue is consistently falling behind and messages are aging out before being processed. The team wants to alert when messages in any SQS queue are older than 5 minutes. Which monitoring configuration detects this condition?
options: Configure an SQS message retention period of 5 minutes so messages older than 5 minutes are automatically deleted
Enable SQS long polling with a wait time of 20 seconds so messages are retrieved faster, reducing queue age
Create CloudWatch alarms on the SQS ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage metric for each queue with a threshold of 300 seconds, and configure SNS notifications for the operations team
Use Amazon EventBridge to monitor SQS queue depth and trigger an alert Lambda function when queue depth exceeds 100 messages
explain: ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage is the SQS CloudWatch metric that measures how long the oldest message in the queue has been waiting. Setting a CloudWatch alarm at 300 seconds (5 minutes) on this metric triggers when processing falls behind, alerting the operations team before messages expire. Long polling reduces empty receive requests but does not speed up consumer processing. Setting retention to 5 minutes would cause messages to be deleted rather than alerting on the problem. Queue depth alarms trigger on volume, not message age — a queue could be small but with very old messages.
hint: ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage detects stale messages in SQS queues

q: A company runs a web application behind an Application Load Balancer. During software deployments, they want to update EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group without taking the application offline. The deployment should replace 25% of instances at a time, waiting for new instances to pass health checks before replacing the next batch. Which Auto Scaling feature implements this?
options: Deploy a second Auto Scaling group with new instances and use ALB weighted target groups to gradually shift traffic from old to new
Configure the Auto Scaling group instance refresh with a MinHealthyPercentage of 75% so that at most 25% of instances are replaced at a time, waiting for new instances to be healthy before continuing
Manually terminate 25% of Auto Scaling group instances at a time and wait for replacement instances to become healthy before terminating the next batch
Use AWS CodeDeploy with a rolling deployment configuration targeting 25% of instances in each deployment wave
explain: Auto Scaling group instance refresh with MinHealthyPercentage=75% ensures at most 25% of instances are replaced at any time. Auto Scaling launches new instances with the updated launch template, waits for them to pass health checks and reach InService state, then terminates old instances. This continues batch by batch until all instances are updated. CodeDeploy rolling deployments are valid but require CodeDeploy agent setup and deployment group configuration. Manual termination is error-prone and operationally intensive. Blue/green with weighted target groups is a valid zero-downtime strategy but more complex than instance refresh for rolling updates.
hint: Instance refresh with MinHealthyPercentage controls rolling replacement rate

q: A company uses Amazon Route 53 for DNS. Their application endpoint in us-east-1 serves global traffic. During an application-level failure (the EC2 instances are running but the application returns 500 errors), Route 53 health checks do not detect the failure because they only check TCP connectivity. The team wants health checks that detect HTTP 5xx responses. Which configuration detects application-level failures?
options: Enable ALB access logging to S3 and create an Athena query to detect 5xx rates, triggering a Route 53 failover when the rate exceeds a threshold
Configure Route 53 health checks to use HTTP or HTTPS protocol and check for a specific string in the response body, or check that the HTTP response code is not 5xx
Deploy an AWS Lambda function that makes HTTP requests to the application every minute and updates the Route 53 health check status via the API
Use CloudWatch alarms to detect 5xx responses from the ALB and configure Route 53 calculated health checks that combine the CloudWatch alarm with the TCP health check
explain: Route 53 health checks support HTTP and HTTPS protocols and can verify the response code and optionally search for a specific string in the response body. Configuring the health check to fail on 5xx responses allows Route 53 to detect application-level failures and trigger DNS failover. CloudWatch alarm-based calculated health checks work but add complexity. Lambda-driven health status updates require custom code and introduce potential single points of failure. ALB access log analysis with Athena introduces significant latency before failover is triggered.
hint: Route 53 HTTP health checks can validate response codes and body content

q: A company uses AWS Lambda for event processing. During a Lambda function update, the new version introduces a bug that causes failures. The company wants to detect failures quickly and automatically revert to the previous stable version without manual intervention. Which deployment strategy achieves this?
options: Deploy the new Lambda version using a Lambda alias with a CodeDeploy Canary or Linear traffic shifting strategy, with pre-traffic and post-traffic hooks running automated tests; CodeDeploy automatically rolls back to the previous version if the tests fail or CloudWatch alarms trigger
Enable Lambda function versioning and create a new alias for each deployment; update Route 53 weighted routing to send 10% of traffic to the new alias for testing
Use AWS SAM to deploy Lambda functions with blue/green deployment by creating a new function with a different name and updating API Gateway to point to it
Deploy the new Lambda version manually and monitor CloudWatch Logs for errors; manually update the alias to point to the previous version if issues are detected
explain: AWS CodeDeploy with Lambda supports traffic shifting strategies (Canary, Linear, All-at-Once). Pre-traffic hooks run automated validation before any traffic is shifted. Post-traffic hooks validate after the shift. If CloudWatch alarms defined in the deployment configuration trigger, CodeDeploy automatically rolls back by shifting 100% traffic back to the previous version. Manual monitoring and rollback does not meet the requirement for automation. Lambda aliases with Route 53 weighted routing requires manual traffic management. SAM blue/green requires manual cutover and rollback.
hint: CodeDeploy traffic shifting with alarms enables automated Lambda rollback

q: A company's production Aurora MySQL cluster experiences an unexpected engine crash. The team needs to recover the database as quickly as possible. After the crash, Aurora automatically restarts but the team wants to understand the fastest recovery path if the database does not automatically recover. Which Aurora feature minimizes data loss during a crash recovery?
options: Restore the Aurora cluster from the most recent automated backup, which is taken daily and stored in Amazon S3

Aurora uses a distributed storage architecture where the data is replicated across 6 storage nodes in 3 AZs; after a crash, Aurora automatically recovers without requiring replay of database logs, typically completing in under 60 seconds
Failover the Aurora primary to a read replica in another AZ, which takes over as the new primary within 30 seconds
Use Aurora point-in-time recovery to restore to the exact moment before the crash with zero data loss
explain: Aurora's distributed storage architecture replicates data across 6 copies in 3 AZs. Because writes are committed to the storage layer before being acknowledged, the database engine does not need to replay redo logs after a crash — the storage is already consistent. Aurora crash recovery completes in under 60 seconds (often under 10 seconds) by design. Restoring from a daily backup introduces hours of RPO. PITR can restore close to the crash point but requires a restore operation that takes time. Failing over to a read replica is valid if replicas exist, but native crash recovery is automatic and faster.
hint: Aurora's 6-copy distributed storage eliminates log replay on crash recovery

q: A company needs to replicate Amazon S3 objects between two buckets in different accounts for cross-account data sharing. The source account owns the objects but the destination account's team should be able to read them. Which S3 configuration must be in place for cross-account replication to work correctly?
options: Enable S3 Object Ownership on the destination bucket set to Bucket Owner Enforced and rely on ACLs to grant the destination account access to replicated objects
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on both buckets and configure the source bucket to replicate objects using the accelerated endpoint in the destination account
Use AWS DataSync to copy objects from the source S3 bucket to the destination S3 bucket on a schedule with cross-account IAM role assumption

Configure S3 Cross-Region Replication with a replication rule in the source bucket, create an IAM role in the source account with permission to replicate to the destination bucket, and add a destination bucket policy granting the source account's IAM role permission to write objects
explain: Cross-account S3 CRR requires three components: a replication rule on the source bucket, an IAM role in the source account that S3 assumes to perform replication, and a bucket policy on the destination bucket that explicitly grants the source account's IAM role s3:PutObject and s3:ReplicateObject permissions. Without the destination bucket policy, S3 cannot write replicated objects. Transfer Acceleration is for upload speed optimization, not cross-account replication. DataSync works but is a scheduled sync service, not native CRR. Object Ownership with Bucket Owner Enforced is about ACL control, not about enabling cross-account writes.
hint: Cross-account CRR needs source IAM role + destination bucket policy

q: A company runs a global application with users in North America and Europe. The application uses a single Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL instance in us-east-1. European users experience 150ms database query latency due to cross-Atlantic round trips. The company wants to reduce European read query latency to under 20ms without changing write routing. Which solution achieves this?
options: Create an RDS read replica in eu-west-1 and update the application's European read traffic to use the replica endpoint in eu-west-1
Deploy ElastiCache for Redis in eu-west-1 and cache all read queries in the European cache with a 30-second TTL
Enable Amazon CloudFront in front of the RDS PostgreSQL instance to cache database query results at European edge locations
Use Amazon Global Accelerator to route European users to the us-east-1 RDS instance over the AWS global backbone, reducing cross-Atlantic latency
explain: An RDS read replica in eu-west-1 replicates data from the primary and serves read queries locally with sub-20ms latency for European users. Write queries continue to go to the primary in us-east-1. This is the standard pattern for geographically distributed read-heavy workloads. CloudFront cannot cache database queries — it is a web CDN, not a database proxy. Global Accelerator reduces network hops over the AWS backbone but cannot eliminate the fundamental physics of a transatlantic round trip to a database. ElastiCache caching works but requires cache invalidation logic for data consistency.
hint: Cross-region read replica serves reads locally for low latency

q: A company uses an Auto Scaling group with a target tracking scaling policy based on CPU utilization. During a flash sale, traffic spikes suddenly and 50 new EC2 instances are needed. EC2 instances take 8 minutes to initialize and join the ALB. By the time instances are ready, the spike has subsided. The team wants additional capacity to be immediately available during the next flash sale. Which Auto Scaling feature addresses this?
options: Configure a warm pool with pre-initialized, stopped EC2 instances that can start and join the ALB in under 2 minutes instead of requiring full initialization from scratch
Use Spot instances in the Auto Scaling group to reduce cost and launch additional Spot instances as a faster scaling mechanism
Use predictive scaling to analyze historical traffic patterns and pre-launch instances 30 minutes before the expected spike
Increase the Auto Scaling group's maximum capacity to 200 and trust that the target tracking policy will launch enough instances in time
explain: Warm pools maintain a pool of pre-initialized EC2 instances in a stopped or running state. During flash sales, instances from the warm pool can be started and added to the ALB in 1-2 minutes instead of 8 minutes, dramatically reducing the gap between demand and capacity. Predictive scaling helps with regular patterns but may not predict flash sale traffic accurately. Increasing max capacity does not reduce initialization time. Spot instances can launch quickly but still require the full initialization time if the application needs to install software or fetch configuration on startup.
hint: Warm pools keep pre-initialized instances for fast scale-out

q: A company uses AWS Organizations to manage multiple AWS accounts. A shared services team runs AWS Config rules to ensure compliance across all accounts. Currently, each account has its own Config recorder and rules, making it difficult to get a consolidated compliance view. The company wants centralized compliance reporting with consistent rules across all accounts. Which AWS Config feature achieves this?
options: Manually export Config compliance data from each account to Amazon S3 and use Amazon Athena to query a unified compliance dataset
Create a central Config recorder in the management account and grant it permission to read resource configurations from all member accounts
Use AWS Security Hub to import Config compliance findings from all accounts into a single management account view

Use AWS Config conformance packs deployed through an organization-level aggregator, which deploys the same Config rules across all member accounts and aggregates results in a central compliance dashboard
explain: AWS Config conformance packs bundle multiple Config rules into a single deployable artifact. When deployed through AWS Organizations, conformance packs are automatically deployed to all member accounts, ensuring consistent rules. A Config aggregator in the management account collects compliance data from all accounts, providing a unified compliance view. A single central Config recorder cannot access other accounts' resources directly. Security Hub aggregates findings but is a separate security orchestration service, not a Config deployment mechanism. Manual S3 exports require custom tooling and provide no real-time compliance view.
hint: Config conformance packs deploy rules organization-wide with central aggregation

q: A company's application uses Amazon S3 to store user-uploaded files. The S3 bucket has versioning enabled. Over time, old versions accumulate and storage costs increase significantly. The company wants to automatically remove non-current versions older than 90 days while keeping the current version and the most recent 2 non-current versions indefinitely. Which S3 feature implements this?
options: Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering to automatically move old versions to lower-cost storage tiers instead of deleting them

Create an S3 Lifecycle policy with a rule that expires noncurrent versions with a NoncurrentDays of 90 and NoncurrentVersionExpiration with NewerNoncurrentVersions set to 2 to retain the two most recent noncurrent versions
Enable S3 Object Lock in Governance mode on noncurrent versions to prevent deletion while allowing the team to manually delete old versions
Write an AWS Lambda function triggered by a daily EventBridge schedule to list and delete old S3 object versions using the S3 API
explain: S3 Lifecycle policies support noncurrent version expiration with both NoncurrentDays (minimum age before deletion) and NewerNoncurrentVersions (number of newer versions to retain). Combining these parameters implements exactly the described retention policy: keep the 2 most recent noncurrent versions forever but delete any noncurrent version older than 90 days (unless it is one of the retained 2). Intelligent-Tiering moves data to cheaper storage but retains all versions. Lambda automation adds custom code maintenance. Object Lock prevents deletion, the opposite of the requirement.
hint: Lifecycle NoncurrentVersionExpiration with NewerNoncurrentVersions retains specific version counts

q: A company runs an AWS Lambda function that processes messages from an Amazon SQS queue. Occasionally, messages are malformed and cause the Lambda function to throw an exception. These messages are retried repeatedly, blocking processing of valid messages behind them. The team wants malformed messages to be removed from the main queue after 3 failed attempts and preserved for investigation. Which configuration should be implemented?
options: Add try-catch blocks in the Lambda function to silently discard malformed messages without throwing exceptions
Enable SQS FIFO with message group IDs to process each group independently so malformed messages only block their own group

Attach an SQS Dead Letter Queue to the source queue with a maxReceiveCount of 3 and configure the Lambda event source mapping to not retry indefinitely, so malformed messages move to the DLQ after 3 failures
Set the SQS message visibility timeout to 1 second so malformed messages are quickly made visible for retry and eventually expire
explain: Attaching a DLQ with maxReceiveCount=3 moves messages to the DLQ after 3 failed receive-and-not-delete cycles. This removes the malformed message from the source queue after 3 failures, unblocking subsequent messages. The DLQ preserves the malformed messages for investigation and potential reprocessing after fixing the root cause. Silently discarding messages loses potentially important data that should be investigated. FIFO with message groups isolates failures per group but messages still block their group and may not move to DLQ without explicit configuration. Short visibility timeout accelerates retries but does not automatically route to DLQ.
hint: SQS DLQ with maxReceiveCount isolates poison-pill messages

q: A company runs its application across three AWS Availability Zones using an Auto Scaling group. After a deployment, instances in one AZ start failing health checks and the Auto Scaling group begins launching replacement instances. However, all replacement instances are being launched in the same AZ as the failures, creating an AZ imbalance. What should the solutions architect check?
options: Disable the AZ rebalancing feature in the Auto Scaling group to prevent it from moving instances between AZs

Verify the AZ rebalancing behavior and check that the Auto Scaling group's AZ configuration includes all three AZs; also check if capacity is available in the affected AZ before assuming a configuration error
Create a CloudWatch alarm on EC2 status checks to detect AZ failures and trigger a Lambda function that removes the failed AZ from the Auto Scaling group
Enable cross-zone load balancing on the ALB to distribute traffic to healthy instances regardless of which AZ they are in
explain: Auto Scaling group AZ rebalancing replaces terminated instances in the same AZ they were terminated in to maintain balance. If all three AZs are properly configured, replacements should distribute across AZs. If they are all launching in one AZ, it could indicate the other AZs lack capacity (Spot) or are misconfigured. The first step is verifying AZ configuration and capacity availability. Disabling rebalancing does not solve the root cause and may worsen the imbalance. Cross-zone load balancing affects traffic distribution, not instance placement. Lambda-driven AZ removal is a drastic action that should not be taken without first diagnosing the root cause.
hint: Auto Scaling rebalances across configured AZs when capacity is available

q: A company wants to set up automated backups for its Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL instance with a recovery point objective of 5 minutes. The automated backup window is 1 hour daily. Which combination of RDS features meets the 5-minute RPO?
options: Use AWS Backup with a backup frequency of every 5 minutes to meet the RPO requirement

Enable RDS automated backups with a 35-day retention period and enable point-in-time recovery, which uses continuous transaction log backups to allow restoration to any second within the retention window, achieving RPO close to 5 minutes
Configure RDS to take automated backups every 5 minutes using a scheduled Lambda function that calls CreateDBSnapshot
Enable RDS Multi-AZ so the standby is continuously synchronized, providing an RPO of zero for AZ-level failures
explain: RDS automated backups combined with point-in-time recovery (PITR) provide an effective RPO of approximately 5 minutes. RDS continuously uploads transaction logs to S3 in near-real-time (typically every 5 minutes). PITR allows restoration to any specific second within the backup retention window. Snapshots every 5 minutes via Lambda would work but is not a supported RDS feature (minimum snapshot interval is 1 hour and individual snapshots cannot be chained for PITR). Multi-AZ provides zero RPO for AZ failures but does not protect against data corruption or accidental deletion that replicate to the standby. AWS Backup minimum frequency for RDS is 1 hour.
hint: RDS PITR uses continuous log uploads for near-5-minute RPO

q: A company's application uses AWS Lambda and stores state in Amazon DynamoDB. The company wants to ensure the application can recover from a DynamoDB table deletion (accidental or malicious) within 1 hour with data loss of no more than 1 day. Which combination of features achieves this?
options: Enable DynamoDB Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) which retains continuous backups for 35 days, and ensure the account has delete table protection using resource-based policies; PITR allows restoration to any point within the last 35 days
Enable DynamoDB Streams and use Lambda to export all stream events to Amazon S3, reconstructing the table from events in case of deletion
Use DynamoDB Global Tables to replicate the table to a secondary region; restore from the secondary region's table if the primary is deleted
Configure daily DynamoDB on-demand backups using AWS Backup with a 30-day retention period and restore from the most recent backup
explain: DynamoDB PITR maintains continuous backups for up to 35 days, allowing restoration to any second within that window. If the table is accidentally deleted, PITR allows restoration to the state immediately before deletion. Combined with IAM resource-based policies that restrict table deletion to specific principals, this meets the 1-hour RTO (restoration typically completes in 30-60 minutes) and 1-day RPO (data from the previous day's backup is always available). Daily backups via AWS Backup have RPO of 24 hours but restoration time may exceed 1 hour for large tables. Streams to S3 requires reconstructing the table from event history, which is complex and time-consuming. Global Tables replication means table deletion in one region does not delete the other, but may not prevent accidental multi-region deletion.
hint: PITR allows DynamoDB restoration to any point in 35-day window

q: A company has an application that processes high-priority and low-priority tasks. Both task types are currently in the same SQS queue. High-priority tasks are sometimes delayed because low-priority tasks occupy all consumer threads. The team wants high-priority tasks to always be processed before low-priority tasks. Which architectural change implements this?
options: Enable SQS FIFO queues and assign a higher message group ID priority score to high-priority messages to ensure they are delivered first
Configure SQS message delay on low-priority messages with a DelaySeconds of 300 to ensure high-priority messages are processed first

Create two separate SQS queues — one for high-priority and one for low-priority tasks — and configure the consumer application to always poll the high-priority queue first and only poll the low-priority queue when the high-priority queue is empty
Use Amazon EventBridge with event pattern matching to route high-priority events to a Lambda function with higher reserved concurrency
explain: Two separate queues with priority-aware polling logic is the standard pattern for SQS priority queuing. The consumer checks the high-priority queue first; if there are messages, it processes them. Only when the high-priority queue is empty does it poll the low-priority queue. SQS FIFO does not support message priority — it guarantees order within a message group but all groups are treated equally. EventBridge routing with different concurrency levels helps with throughput but does not guarantee high-priority tasks finish before low-priority ones start. Message delay postpones low-priority processing but not reliably in all cases.
hint: Two SQS queues with priority-aware polling implements task priority

q: A company uses Amazon CloudFront to distribute content. They want to ensure that their CloudFront distribution automatically falls back to a backup origin if the primary origin returns 5xx errors. Which CloudFront feature implements origin failover?
options: Configure a CloudFront cache behavior with multiple origins and weighted routing to distribute traffic between primary and secondary
Configure a CloudFront origin group with a primary origin and a secondary failover origin, specifying the HTTP status codes (500, 502, 503, 504) that trigger failover to the secondary origin
Use Lambda@Edge at the origin response event to detect 5xx responses and rewrite the request to the secondary origin URL
Create two separate CloudFront distributions with different origins and use Route 53 health check-based failover to switch between them
explain: CloudFront origin groups enable automatic origin failover. When the primary origin returns specified error codes (5xx status codes), CloudFront automatically retries the request against the secondary failover origin. This is a native CloudFront feature requiring no custom code. Two separate distributions with Route 53 failover works but requires DNS TTL propagation time and adds operational complexity. Lambda@Edge can implement custom origin failover logic but adds latency and code complexity for something CloudFront handles natively. Weighted routing distributes traffic rather than failing over.
hint: CloudFront origin groups provide automatic failover on 5xx responses

q: A company's application uses AWS Fargate for containerized services. During a container failure, the ECS service replaces failed tasks but new tasks take 3 minutes to start and reach a healthy state. The company wants to reduce the impact of container failures by maintaining application availability during the task restart period. Which configuration minimizes service disruption during Fargate task failures?
options: Use ECS task placement strategies to distribute tasks across AZs so a single AZ failure affects fewer tasks simultaneously
Increase the Fargate task CPU and memory allocation to make individual tasks more resilient and less likely to fail
Configure ECS service Auto Scaling with a target tracking policy to launch additional tasks whenever any task fails
Set the ECS service's minimum healthy percent to 100% and maximum percent to 200%, so the service always maintains the desired number of healthy tasks and can run double capacity during replacements
explain: Setting minimum healthy percent to 100% and maximum percent to 200% means ECS maintains the full desired count of healthy tasks at all times. When a task fails, ECS can launch a replacement (up to the 200% maximum) without terminating healthy tasks, ensuring no reduction in serving capacity during the 3-minute restart period. Service Auto Scaling responds to metrics but does not proactively maintain capacity during failures. AZ distribution reduces blast radius but does not eliminate service disruption per failure. Increasing task resources makes them less likely to fail due to resource exhaustion but does not address any failure type.
hint: 100% minimum healthy percent ensures full capacity during task replacement

q: A company runs Amazon EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group and wants to implement a blue/green deployment strategy to release new application versions with zero downtime and the ability to instantly roll back. Which approach achieves this with minimum complexity?
options: Create a new Auto Scaling group (green) with the new version, register it with the same ALB as a new target group, use ALB weighted target groups to shift traffic gradually from the old (blue) group to the new (green) group, and instantly roll back by shifting traffic back to blue
Terminate all instances in the existing Auto Scaling group and launch new instances with the updated AMI, minimizing transition time
Deploy the new version on separate EC2 instances and use Route 53 weighted routing to shift traffic from old to new instances
Use AWS CodeDeploy in-place deployment with the AllAtOnce deployment configuration to update all instances simultaneously
explain: ALB weighted target groups with two Auto Scaling groups (blue and green) is the cleanest blue/green pattern on AWS. Traffic is shifted gradually using ALB weights, and rollback is instantaneous by setting the blue target group weight back to 100%. Both versions run simultaneously during the transition. CodeDeploy in-place AllAtOnce replaces all instances at once, causing downtime and slow rollback. Terminating all instances and relaunching causes downtime. Route 53 weighted routing works but DNS TTL introduces delay in traffic shifting and rollback, unlike ALB which shifts instantly.
hint: ALB weighted target groups enable instant blue/green traffic shifting

q: A startup is building a multi-tenant SaaS application on AWS. Each tenant's data must be isolated in separate Amazon RDS databases for compliance reasons. As the number of tenants grows, managing hundreds of individual RDS instances becomes operationally complex. The company wants to maintain data isolation while reducing operational overhead. Which solution should be implemented?
options: Consolidate all tenants onto a single large RDS Multi-AZ instance with separate schemas and row-level security policies to enforce isolation
Use Amazon DynamoDB with a tenant_id partition key for all tenant data, providing logical isolation with a single managed service

Use Amazon RDS Custom or migrate to Amazon Aurora with separate schemas or databases per tenant within shared Aurora clusters, and use IAM database authentication with tenant-specific credentials to enforce isolation at the database level
Deploy each tenant's database in a separate AWS account to provide maximum isolation with separate billing and IAM boundaries
explain: For compliance-mandated database isolation with reduced operational overhead, migrating from hundreds of individual RDS instances to a managed service like DynamoDB with tenant-scoped access patterns is valid. However, if the compliance requirement mandates physical isolation (separate databases), the best balance between isolation and operational simplicity is Aurora clusters with separate databases per tenant cluster — sharing Aurora infrastructure (backups, patching managed by AWS) while maintaining database-level isolation. Separate AWS accounts per tenant maximizes isolation but creates extreme billing and management overhead. DynamoDB with tenant_id provides logical, not physical isolation. A single RDS instance with row-level security provides logical, not physical isolation.
hint: Aurora managed clusters reduce ops overhead while maintaining DB-level isolation

q: A company's application uses Amazon SQS FIFO queues for ordered message processing. The application processes 500 messages per second. The team notices that processing has stopped and discovers the FIFO queue has hit its throughput limit. What is the maximum throughput limit for SQS FIFO queues without high throughput mode enabled?
options: 10,000 messages per second for API calls without batching, matching Standard queue throughput
300 messages per second for API calls without batching, or 3,000 messages per second with batching (10 messages per batch); enabling high throughput mode increases this to 70,000 messages per second
1,000 messages per second with no batching option, with no ability to increase beyond this limit
Unlimited throughput, same as SQS Standard queues, but with ordering guarantees added
explain: SQS FIFO queues support 300 API calls per second without batching, or up to 3,000 messages per second when sending in batches of 10. For higher throughput, FIFO High Throughput Mode can be enabled, increasing the limit to 70,000 messages per second (as of recent updates). Since the application needs 500 messages/second, enabling batching (3,000 msg/s limit) or high throughput mode resolves the bottleneck. Standard queues have effectively unlimited throughput but no ordering guarantees. FIFO queues do not have unlimited throughput by default.
hint: FIFO queues support 3,000 msg/s with batching or 70,000 with high throughput mode

q: A company uses AWS Lambda functions for a critical payment processing workflow. The company wants to ensure that if a Lambda function invocation fails asynchronously (invoked by S3 or SNS), the failure is captured and the event is not silently dropped. Which configuration handles asynchronous Lambda failure events?
options: Configure Lambda reserved concurrency to prevent throttling, ensuring all invocations succeed on the first attempt
Enable Lambda function versioning and create an alias with routing configuration to automatically retry on another version
Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to query Lambda execution logs and identify failed invocations for manual reprocessing
Configure a Lambda Destination for On Failure to route failed asynchronous invocation events to an SQS queue or another Lambda function for investigation and reprocessing
explain: Lambda Destinations for asynchronous invocations capture success and failure events. Configuring an On Failure destination to SQS ensures that if a Lambda function fails all retry attempts (2 retries by default), the event metadata is sent to SQS for investigation. This prevents silent failure. Lambda aliases with routing configuration manage traffic splitting between versions, not failure handling. Reserved concurrency prevents throttling but does not capture events if the function fails for other reasons. CloudWatch Logs queries are reactive and require manual investigation — they do not automatically capture and preserve failed events.
hint: Lambda Destinations route failed async invocations to SQS or Lambda

q: A company operates a global application and wants to implement an active-active architecture across us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Both regions must serve read and write traffic simultaneously. The database layer must replicate writes between regions with sub-second latency. Which database solution supports active-active multi-region writes?
options: Amazon RDS Multi-AZ in each region with AWS Database Migration Service continuously replicating changes between the two regions
Amazon Aurora Global Database with the primary in us-east-1 accepting all writes and the secondary in eu-west-1 serving reads only
Amazon DynamoDB Global Tables with active-active replication, where writes to any region are automatically replicated to all other replica regions within approximately 1 second
Amazon ElastiCache Global Datastore with Redis replication between regions allowing writes in all regions
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables is the AWS-managed solution for active-active multi-region database architectures. Each replica table accepts writes, and DynamoDB automatically replicates changes to all other replicas within approximately 1 second using last-writer-wins conflict resolution. Aurora Global Database has a single primary writer — secondaries are read-only. RDS with DMS replication is not truly active-active and lacks built-in conflict resolution. ElastiCache Global Datastore with Redis supports active-active for Redis Cluster but is a cache, not a durable database — data loss on eviction is expected.
hint: DynamoDB Global Tables is the native active-active multi-region database

q: A company uses AWS CloudTrail to audit API calls across its AWS accounts. The operations team discovers that CloudTrail logs in one account were deleted by a misconfigured automation script. The company wants to prevent CloudTrail logs from being deleted in any account and ensure log integrity. Which configuration should be implemented?
options: Use AWS Security Hub to monitor CloudTrail status across accounts and automatically re-enable trails when disabled
Configure AWS Config rules to check that CloudTrail is enabled in all accounts and trigger an SNS alert when logs are deleted

Create an organization-level CloudTrail trail in the management account and enable log file validation; store logs in an S3 bucket with Object Lock in Compliance mode and a bucket policy that denies s3:DeleteObject from all principals including the management account
Enable CloudTrail log file integrity validation to detect any tampering or deletion of log files after the fact
explain: An organization trail centralizes CloudTrail logging from all accounts into a single S3 bucket managed by the management account. S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode prevents deletion of log objects even by the root account. A bucket policy denying s3:DeleteObject adds an additional IAM layer. Log file validation ensures integrity of stored logs. Log file integrity validation alone detects tampering after the fact but does not prevent deletion. Config rules alert on deletion but cannot prevent it. Security Hub monitoring is reactive and re-enabling CloudTrail does not recover deleted logs.
hint: Organization trail + S3 Object Lock Compliance prevents CloudTrail log deletion

q: A company wants to achieve a warm standby disaster recovery posture for its application running on Amazon EC2, RDS, and Elastic Load Balancing. The RTO is 30 minutes. The warm standby should have a reduced but functional version of the application running in the secondary region at all times. Which configuration represents a correct warm standby implementation?
options: Deploy a scaled-down version of the application stack in the secondary region — a smaller EC2 Auto Scaling group at minimum capacity, a read replica promoted to primary during failover, and an ALB configured — with Route 53 health check failover to automatically redirect traffic
Store CloudFormation templates and AMIs in the secondary region and deploy the full application stack from scratch during a failure, targeting 30-minute RTO
Run only the database layer in the secondary region as a read replica and launch the application tier from AMIs only during a failover event
Deploy an identical full-scale application stack in the secondary region and distribute 50% of traffic to each region at all times
explain: Warm standby maintains a reduced-capacity but functional version of the entire stack running continuously. A scaled-down Auto Scaling group (e.g., 1-2 instances instead of 10) runs the application with minimal cost. The read replica is already created and synchronized. During failover, the Auto Scaling group scales up to full capacity, the read replica is promoted to primary, and Route 53 health checks automatically redirect traffic. Full-scale in both regions is active-active, not warm standby. Deploying from scratch in 30 minutes is possible but this is backup/restore or pilot light, not warm standby. Running only the database without app tier is pilot light.
hint: Warm standby runs reduced-capacity full stack in secondary region

q: A company uses Amazon RDS for MySQL with automated backups and a 7-day retention period. A developer accidentally drops a production table at 2:30 PM. The last automated backup was taken at 3 AM. The DBA needs to recover the dropped table with minimum data loss. Which recovery approach is most appropriate?
options: Restore from the 3 AM automated backup and replay the 11.5 hours of transactions manually using binary logs
Use RDS point-in-time recovery to restore the database to 2:29 PM (just before the drop), creating a new RDS instance, then extract and re-import the dropped table into the production database
Use AWS Database Migration Service to migrate data from a read replica to the production instance to restore the dropped table
Enable RDS read replica and promote it to a standalone instance, as it would have the dropped table in its data
explain: RDS point-in-time recovery (PITR) allows restoration to any second within the backup retention period by replaying transaction logs. Restoring to 2:29 PM creates a new RDS instance with the table intact. The team can then dump just the dropped table from the restored instance and re-import it into production, minimizing data loss to less than 1 minute. Restoring from the 3 AM backup and manually replaying binary logs is far more complex and time-consuming. Read replicas replicate all changes including the DROP TABLE — they would not have the table. DMS from a replica is the same issue — the replica also dropped the table.
hint: RDS PITR restores to any second — ideal for accidental DDL recovery

q: A company's application consists of multiple Lambda functions orchestrated by AWS Step Functions. One function in the middle of the workflow calls a third-party API that charges per request. If a Step Functions execution fails after the API call, retrying from the beginning would result in duplicate charges. The team wants to ensure the API call is not made twice if the execution is retried. Which Step Functions feature addresses this?
options: Store the API call result in DynamoDB before proceeding; on retry, check DynamoDB first and skip the API call if a result already exists for the execution ID

Use Step Functions Express Workflows with idempotency tokens passed to the third-party API so duplicate calls can be detected and ignored by the API, and configure the workflow to retry from the failed step using a catch block rather than from the beginning
Use Step Functions Standard Workflows which provide exactly-once execution semantics, ensuring each state is executed at most once per execution
Configure the Lambda function to check an SQS queue for duplicate invocation events before calling the third-party API
explain: Storing the result of the expensive API call in DynamoDB keyed by the execution ID implements idempotency at the application level. On retry, the Lambda function checks DynamoDB first — if a result exists for this execution ID, it returns the cached result without calling the external API again. Step Functions Standard Workflows provide at-least-once execution semantics per state — they do not guarantee exactly-once for external side effects. Express Workflows have at-least-once semantics as well. Idempotency tokens only work if the third-party API supports them, which is not guaranteed for all external APIs. SQS duplicate detection adds complexity without providing the atomic check-and-set needed here.
hint: Store API results in DynamoDB to implement idempotent step retries

q: A company's web application uses Amazon CloudFront to serve both static content from S3 and dynamic content from an ALB. During a major traffic event, the ALB origin becomes temporarily unavailable. The team wants CloudFront to serve stale cached content for up to 10 minutes if the origin is unavailable, rather than returning an error to users. Which CloudFront configuration enables this?
options: Configure the CloudFront cache policy with a stale-if-error value of 600 seconds, so CloudFront serves stale cached objects for up to 10 minutes if the origin returns 5xx errors or is unreachable
Set the CloudFront default TTL to 600 seconds so all objects are cached for 10 minutes before being refreshed from the origin
Use CloudFront Origin Shield to cache an additional copy of content between regional caches and the origin, increasing availability
Configure CloudFront with a custom error response that returns a friendly error page after a 600-second timeout
explain: The CloudFront stale-if-error directive (configurable in cache policies) instructs CloudFront to serve stale cached content when the origin returns a 5xx error or is unreachable, for up to the specified duration. Setting stale-if-error to 600 seconds means users continue receiving content for up to 10 minutes during an origin outage. Default TTL controls how long content is considered fresh, not what happens when the origin fails. Origin Shield adds caching resilience but does not specifically handle origin unavailability for already-cached content differently. Custom error responses return error pages rather than stale content.
hint: stale-if-error serves cached content during origin failures

q: A company uses AWS Elastic Beanstalk to deploy a web application. During a new version deployment, Beanstalk replaces all instances simultaneously, causing 30-60 seconds of downtime. The team wants future deployments to have zero downtime while minimizing additional infrastructure cost. Which Beanstalk deployment policy should be selected?
options: Use Rolling with additional batch deployment policy, which launches a new batch of instances before taking down old ones, ensuring capacity is maintained throughout the deployment without full downtime
Use All at once deployment policy but increase the instance count before deployment to absorb the 30-second gap
Use Blue/Green deployment with a separate Beanstalk environment and swap the CNAME when the new version is ready
Use Immutable deployment policy which launches a completely new Auto Scaling group with the new version, switches traffic, then terminates old instances
explain: Immutable deployment launches new instances in a separate temporary Auto Scaling group with the new application version. Health checks run on the new instances. If successful, the new instances are moved to the original Auto Scaling group and old instances are terminated. Traffic never goes to untested new instances, and rollback is as simple as terminating the temporary group. There is zero downtime and zero capacity reduction. Rolling with additional batch also provides zero downtime but requires more orchestration. All at once always causes downtime. Blue/Green with CNAME swap works but involves the complexity of a full separate environment.
hint: Immutable deployment launches new instances before replacing old ones

q: A company's application architecture uses Amazon EventBridge to trigger AWS Lambda functions. During a service disruption, EventBridge was unable to invoke Lambda for 2 hours. The team wants to understand what happens to events that occurred during the disruption and how to prevent event loss. Which EventBridge behavior and configuration addresses this?
options: EventBridge buffers all events in a FIFO queue for up to 14 days and delivers them all at once when Lambda recovers
EventBridge automatically replays all events from the event bus for the 2-hour window after Lambda recovers, with no additional configuration required

EventBridge retries failed invocations for up to 24 hours with exponential backoff; to prevent loss for events older than 24 hours or those that exhaust retries, configure a Dead Letter Queue on the EventBridge rule target to capture undeliverable events
EventBridge drops events that cannot be delivered immediately; to prevent loss, configure the rule to use SQS as the target instead of Lambda
explain: EventBridge retries failed invocations with exponential backoff for up to 24 hours by default (configurable). If all retries are exhausted, the event is dropped unless a Dead Letter Queue (SQS) is configured on the target. The DLQ captures events that could not be delivered after all retries, allowing for manual or automated reprocessing. EventBridge does not use an internal FIFO buffer for long-term buffering. Using SQS as the target is a valid architecture to absorb Lambda throttling but does not address the EventBridge retry behavior. EventBridge Event Replay is a feature of event archives, not automatic post-failure recovery.
hint: EventBridge retries 24 hours; DLQ captures events that exhaust retries

q: A company is building a new application and wants to follow the AWS Well-Architected Framework guidance for reliability. The team is designing the data storage tier. Which combination of practices best improves data durability and resilience for an Amazon RDS PostgreSQL database?
options: Deploy RDS in a single AZ with automated backups enabled and rely on the high durability of the underlying EBS storage
Use RDS Multi-AZ and disable automated backups to reduce storage costs, relying on the standby for all recovery scenarios
Enable automated backups only and use RDS read replicas as the high-availability mechanism, promoting a replica during primary failures

Enable Multi-AZ deployment for automatic failover, enable automated backups with a 35-day retention period, enable point-in-time recovery, and store snapshots in multiple regions using cross-region copy
explain: The comprehensive approach combines Multi-AZ (AZ-level HA with automatic failover), automated backups with maximum retention (35 days, providing PITR for 35 days), and cross-region snapshot copies (protection against regional failures). This satisfies the Well-Architected reliability pillar by addressing infrastructure failures, accidental data loss, and regional disaster scenarios. Single-AZ with EBS durability does not protect against AZ failures. Disabling backups removes the ability to recover from data corruption or accidental changes. Read replicas as HA require manual promotion and have replication lag, making them less reliable for failover than Multi-AZ.
hint: Multi-AZ + PITR + cross-region snapshots provides layered RDS resilience

q: A company runs a read-heavy e-commerce application backed by Amazon RDS for MySQL. The database handles 90% read traffic and 10% writes. During peak hours the primary instance CPU exceeds 80% and customers experience slow product page loads. The solutions architect must improve read performance with minimum operational overhead. Which solution should be implemented?
options: Enable RDS Multi-AZ deployment so the standby instance can serve read traffic during peak hours
Create Amazon RDS read replicas and update the application to direct all SELECT queries to the read replica endpoint while writes go to the primary endpoint
Upgrade the RDS primary instance to a larger instance class to handle the increased concurrent read load
Enable RDS automated backups and use the backup window to offload read queries during off-peak hours
explain: RDS Read Replicas use asynchronous replication to maintain up-to-date copies of the primary database and serve read traffic. By routing SELECT queries to the read replica endpoint the primary instance handles only writes, dramatically reducing CPU load. RDS Multi-AZ standby instances do not serve read traffic — they exist only for automatic failover. Upsizing the instance class increases capacity but does not distribute the read load. Automated backups are for point-in-time recovery, not query distribution.

q: A financial services company runs a trading application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The application stores session data in each instance's local memory. When Auto Scaling adds new instances during market hours, users are routed to new instances and lose their sessions. The company wants session persistence without changing the core application logic. Which solution resolves this with the least operational overhead?
options: Increase the ALB idle timeout to 3600 seconds so sessions are maintained even if the instance is briefly unreachable
Store session data in Amazon ElastiCache for Redis and configure the application to read and write sessions to ElastiCache instead of local memory
Configure the Auto Scaling group to use Instance Protection on running instances so they are never terminated while a session is active
Enable ALB sticky sessions (session affinity) using duration-based cookies so each user is routed to the same instance for their session lifetime
explain: ALB sticky sessions bind a user's requests to a specific EC2 instance using a cookie (AWSALB), ensuring session data stored in local memory is always accessible for that user. This requires no application code changes. ElastiCache for Redis is the architecturally better long-term solution but requires modifying the application's session handling code. Instance Protection prevents scale-in termination but does not solve the problem of new users hitting new instances. Increasing idle timeout extends connection lifetime but does not guarantee routing to the same instance.

q: A media streaming company serves video content globally. The origin server is in us-east-1. Users in Asia and Europe report buffering and high latency. The company wants to improve playback experience for international users with minimal infrastructure changes. Which solution provides the greatest performance improvement?
options: Deploy EC2 instances in eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 and use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users to the nearest region
Deploy Amazon CloudFront with the origin set to the us-east-1 load balancer; configure cache behaviors to cache video segments at CloudFront edge locations closest to viewers
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the origin S3 bucket so video content is delivered faster to international viewers
Increase the EC2 instance type in us-east-1 to provide more bandwidth for international users
explain: Amazon CloudFront is a global CDN with 400+ edge locations. Caching video segments at edge locations closest to viewers eliminates the round-trip to us-east-1 for cached content, reducing latency from hundreds of milliseconds to single-digit milliseconds. Multi-region EC2 deployment requires replicating content and managing multiple stacks. Upsizing the origin instance doesn't reduce network distance. S3 Transfer Acceleration accelerates uploads to S3, not downloads to end users.

q: An online gaming company has a leaderboard that is updated thousands of times per second. The current MySQL RDS implementation cannot keep up with write throughput and the leaderboard shows stale data. The company needs a solution that handles high write throughput and returns leaderboard results in real time with sub-millisecond latency. Which service is most appropriate?
options: Amazon ElastiCache for Redis using sorted sets (ZSET) which support atomic score updates and range queries to retrieve top-N players in sub-millisecond time
Amazon Aurora with read replicas to distribute leaderboard read queries while writes go to the primary
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to buffer leaderboard updates and process them in micro-batches using Lambda
Amazon DynamoDB with a GSI on the score attribute to allow efficient leaderboard queries across all players
explain: Amazon ElastiCache for Redis sorted sets (ZSET) are purpose-built for leaderboard use cases. ZINCRBY atomically updates a player's score in microseconds, and ZREVRANGE retrieves the top-N players in O(log N) time. Redis operates entirely in memory delivering sub-millisecond responses regardless of dataset size. DynamoDB cannot efficiently sort across all partition key values without expensive scans or complex GSI designs. Aurora read replicas improve read throughput but cannot match Redis in-memory speed. Kinesis introduces processing latency incompatible with real-time leaderboard requirements.

q: A company processes customer orders through a Lambda function triggered by API Gateway. During flash sales the function is invoked 10,000 times per minute, causing throttling errors because the function hits the account-level concurrency limit. The function completes in under 200ms. Which solution allows the application to handle the traffic spike without throttling?
options: Use SQS as a buffer between API Gateway and Lambda; the function polls SQS and processes orders at a controlled rate preventing throttling
Request a service quota increase for Lambda concurrency and configure reserved concurrency for the order processing function to guarantee its share of account concurrency
Enable Lambda provisioned concurrency to pre-initialize execution environments and eliminate cold starts during the flash sale
Deploy the Lambda function across multiple AWS regions and use Route 53 latency-based routing to distribute traffic
explain: Using SQS as a buffer decouples the API from Lambda processing. API Gateway writes order messages to SQS synchronously (accepting the request immediately), while Lambda consumes from the queue at a controlled rate within concurrency limits. No orders are lost and the API remains responsive even during 10,000 RPS spikes. A service quota increase takes time to process and may not be granted before the flash sale. Reserved concurrency guarantees capacity but doesn't increase the account limit. Provisioned concurrency reduces cold starts but doesn't solve the fundamental concurrency ceiling.

q: A retail company stores product catalog data in Amazon DynamoDB. The catalog has 5 million items and the application performs 50,000 GetItem calls per second. Response time is increasing under load and the development team wants to add caching. The cache must be transparent to the application with no code changes required. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Add Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as a caching layer and refactor the application to check the cache before querying DynamoDB
Enable DynamoDB global tables to distribute read load across multiple AWS regions
Configure DynamoDB Auto Scaling to increase read capacity units automatically when throttling occurs

Deploy Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in front of DynamoDB; update the application to use the DAX cluster endpoint instead of the DynamoDB endpoint — DAX is API-compatible so no other code changes are needed
explain: Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is a fully managed, in-memory cache for DynamoDB that is API-compatible with the DynamoDB SDK. Changing the endpoint from DynamoDB to the DAX cluster is the only required code change — all existing GetItem, Query, and Scan calls work identically. DAX reduces read latency from milliseconds to microseconds and handles millions of requests per second. ElastiCache for Redis requires significant application refactoring to implement cache-aside logic. Auto Scaling adjusts provisioned capacity but doesn't reduce per-request latency. Global Tables replicate data across regions but don't cache frequently read items.

q: A company's web application experiences unpredictable traffic patterns with periods of very low traffic and sudden spikes to thousands of concurrent users. The application tier runs on EC2 with Auto Scaling. The database tier uses RDS for PostgreSQL with a db.r5.2xlarge instance. During spikes the database becomes the bottleneck because connection establishment overhead is high with hundreds of new Lambda and EC2 connections opening simultaneously. Which solution addresses this with the least code changes?
options: Configure RDS to use multi-threading mode to accept more concurrent connections
Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy between the application and RDS; RDS Proxy maintains a warm pool of database connections and multiplexes application connections onto them reducing connection overhead
Implement connection pooling in the application code using PgBouncer running on each EC2 instance
Increase the RDS instance to db.r5.8xlarge to handle more simultaneous connections
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy maintains a persistent pool of database connections and multiplexes thousands of application connections onto a much smaller number of actual database connections. This eliminates the overhead of establishing new TCP connections and TLS handshakes for each request. RDS Proxy is transparent — the application simply points to the proxy endpoint instead of the RDS endpoint. Upsizing the RDS instance increases the connection limit but doesn't solve connection establishment overhead. Multi-threading is not a configurable RDS mode. PgBouncer on each EC2 instance requires deployment, configuration management, and doesn't solve the Lambda cold-start connection problem.

q: A company uses Amazon S3 to store large genomics datasets (each file is 50–500 GB). Data scientists run ad-hoc queries to extract specific gene sequences from portions of each file. Currently the scientists download entire files before processing, which takes hours. They want to run queries directly against S3 without downloading files. Which solution enables this?
options: Use Amazon S3 Select to run SQL expressions directly on individual S3 objects and retrieve only the relevant subset of data rather than the entire object
Use Amazon Athena to query S3 objects using standard SQL; partition the data by chromosome to reduce data scanned per query
Move the genomics data to Amazon Redshift Spectrum and create an external schema to query the S3 data using SQL
Configure S3 Object Lambda to transform the S3 GET response and filter out irrelevant gene sequences before returning data to the scientist
explain: Amazon Athena is the correct choice when scientists need to run complex SQL queries across many large S3 files with varying predicates. Partitioning by chromosome means Athena only scans relevant partitions, dramatically reducing data processed and query time. Athena supports columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) for further optimization. S3 Select queries a single object per request and cannot join or aggregate across multiple files. Redshift Spectrum requires an active Redshift cluster (ongoing cost) and cluster provisioning. S3 Object Lambda is for transforming GET responses for specific objects, not for analytical SQL queries.

q: A social media application serves user profile images through Amazon S3. The images are accessed by millions of users worldwide. The company wants to reduce latency for image delivery globally and reduce the load on S3. The images are rarely updated (once per year on average). Which is the most cost-effective solution?
options: Replicate the S3 bucket to every AWS region using S3 Cross-Region Replication and update DNS to route users to the nearest region bucket
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up image downloads for international users
Deploy EC2 instances in each major AWS region to serve as image proxy servers caching content locally
Configure Amazon CloudFront with the S3 bucket as origin; set a long TTL (1 year) in the cache policy; use Origin Access Control to restrict direct S3 access
explain: CloudFront caches content at 400+ global edge locations. Since images change rarely, a 1-year TTL means CloudFront serves virtually all requests from cache without hitting S3, reducing both latency (edge vs. origin round-trip) and S3 request costs. Origin Access Control ensures images are only accessible through CloudFront. S3 Cross-Region Replication copies data to every region (storage cost multiplied by regions) and requires complex DNS routing. EC2 proxy servers require infrastructure management in every region. S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds uploads and downloads slightly but doesn't cache content at edge locations.

q: An e-commerce company runs product search backed by Amazon OpenSearch Service. As the product catalog grows to 100 million items the search cluster is struggling to keep up with query volume. The team notices that 80% of searches use the same 500 popular search terms. They want to reduce OpenSearch cluster load without changing the search relevance or adding new services beyond what AWS already offers. Which solution addresses this?
options: Configure OpenSearch auto-scaling to add data nodes automatically when CPU utilization exceeds 70%
Enable caching at the API Gateway level for the search API with a cache TTL of 5 minutes; frequently searched terms return cached responses without hitting OpenSearch
Add Amazon ElastiCache for Redis between the application and OpenSearch to cache search results for popular queries
Increase the OpenSearch cluster node count and instance type to handle the query volume
explain: API Gateway's built-in response cache stores responses keyed by request parameters. With 80% of searches using 500 popular terms, cache hit rates will be extremely high. A 5-minute TTL balances freshness with load reduction. This solution requires only an API Gateway configuration change with no new services or application code changes. ElastiCache for Redis is effective but requires application code changes and managing an additional service. Increasing cluster size improves capacity but doesn't reduce redundant computation for repeated queries. Auto-scaling addresses load spikes but not the inefficiency of processing the same 500 queries repeatedly.

q: A company's data analytics platform ingests 1 million events per second from IoT sensors. The events must be stored durably and made available for real-time dashboard queries within 10 seconds of arrival. The team wants a fully managed solution with no server management. Which architecture achieves this?
options: Amazon MSK (Managed Kafka) → Amazon EMR (Spark Streaming) → Amazon Redshift; dashboard connects to Redshift
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams → AWS Lambda (transform) → Amazon DynamoDB (store); dashboard queries DynamoDB using PartiQL
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams → Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) → Amazon DynamoDB; dashboard queries DynamoDB with millisecond latency
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose → Amazon S3 → Amazon Athena; dashboard runs Athena queries against the latest S3 data
explain: Kinesis Data Streams ingests 1M events/second across multiple shards. Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) processes the stream in real time with sub-second processing latency. Results are written to DynamoDB which serves dashboard queries in single-digit milliseconds. The entire pipeline delivers events to queryable storage well within the 10-second requirement. Kinesis Firehose has a minimum 60-second buffer window before delivering to S3, exceeding the 10-second requirement. Lambda-based transformation introduces processing latency for 1M events/second that may exceed the requirement. MSK+EMR+Redshift involves significant operational overhead and setup complexity.

q: A company hosts a REST API on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The API handles 100 requests per second normally but spikes to 10,000 requests per second during promotional events. The current EC2-based architecture takes 5 minutes to scale out, causing errors during sudden spikes. The company wants to handle instantaneous traffic spikes with zero scaling delay. Which re-architecture achieves this?
options: Enable ALB connection draining with a longer timeout so existing connections are maintained during the scale-out period
Configure EC2 Auto Scaling predictive scaling to forecast the promotional event traffic and pre-warm instances before the spike occurs
Add an SQS queue in front of the ALB to buffer excess requests and drain them as EC2 instances come online during scale-out
Migrate the API to Amazon API Gateway with AWS Lambda backends; API Gateway scales instantly to handle any request volume without pre-provisioning
explain: Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that scales automatically to handle any number of API requests per second without provisioning. Lambda functions also scale concurrently without delay. The serverless architecture eliminates the 5-minute EC2 scale-out window entirely. Predictive scaling reduces scale-out time but cannot predict instantaneous unannounced spikes and still has a warm-up delay. SQS buffering improves resilience but doesn't prevent errors during the 5-minute period before new EC2 instances are ready.
