Connection draining is for graceful instance removal, not for handling scale-out delays.

q: A gaming company stores player inventory data in Amazon DynamoDB. Items have complex nested attributes and the application frequently updates deeply nested list elements within a single item. Currently the application reads the entire item, modifies it in memory, and writes it back — causing race conditions when multiple game servers update the same player's inventory simultaneously. Which DynamoDB feature resolves race conditions without adding external coordination infrastructure?
options: Use DynamoDB Transactions (TransactWriteItems) to wrap all inventory updates in a transaction guaranteeing atomicity across items
Use DynamoDB's UpdateItem with atomic counters and list_append expressions to update specific attributes without reading the full item first
Use DynamoDB conditional expressions with a version attribute; increment the version with each write and fail the write if the version doesn't match the expected value (optimistic locking)
Enable DynamoDB Streams and use a Lambda function to serialize inventory updates, applying them sequentially to avoid concurrent conflicts
explain: DynamoDB's UpdateItem supports atomic updates to specific attributes using update expressions like SET inventory = list_append(inventory, :new_item) or ADD quantity :delta. These operations are atomic at the item level — they update exactly the specified attribute without reading or overwriting unmodified attributes. This eliminates read-modify-write race conditions. Conditional expressions with version numbers (optimistic locking) require reading first and retrying on conflict — valid but adds latency. Transactions span multiple operations but add overhead for single-item updates. DynamoDB Streams with Lambda introduces significant architectural complexity and latency.

q: A company uses Amazon Redshift for its data warehouse. Complex analytical queries take 45–60 minutes to complete. Analysis shows the bottleneck is disk I/O from sorting large tables during query execution. The data is loaded nightly and queries always filter on the order_date column. Which optimization reduces query execution time most significantly?
options: Use Redshift Spectrum to offload the complex queries to S3-based storage which has higher I/O throughput
Define order_date as the sort key on the primary fact table; Redshift stores data sorted by this key so queries filtering on order_date read only relevant disk blocks
Add more nodes to the Redshift cluster to distribute the I/O load across more storage
Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling to add additional cluster capacity for complex queries
explain: Redshift sort keys determine the on-disk order of data. When order_date is the sort key, rows for a given date range are stored contiguously. Queries filtering on order_date skip irrelevant blocks using zone maps (min/max per block) — dramatically reducing I/O. This can reduce query time from 60 minutes to minutes. Concurrency Scaling adds capacity for concurrent users but doesn't speed up a single query's I/O bottleneck. Adding nodes distributes I/O across more drives but doesn't eliminate the sorting overhead. Spectrum queries S3 directly and is useful for historical data, not for improving sort efficiency on Redshift-resident data.

q: A company's application generates large reports by querying Amazon Athena. Each report query scans several terabytes of JSON data stored in S3. The company pays $5 per TB scanned and report generation costs are very high. The underlying data does not change. Which two changes to the data storage format reduce Athena query costs by the greatest amount?
options: Compress the JSON files using gzip before storing in S3 so Athena scans fewer bytes per query
Create Athena saved queries for common report types to reuse execution plans without re-scanning data
Convert the JSON files to Apache Parquet columnar format and partition the S3 data by date and region; Parquet allows Athena to read only queried columns and partitioning skips irrelevant data
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on the data bucket so frequently scanned data is in the low-latency tier reducing per-scan time
explain: Two optimizations together provide the largest cost reduction. First, Parquet is a columnar format — if a report needs only 5 of 50 columns, Athena reads only those 5 columns (90% data reduction). JSON requires reading every field. Second, partitioning by date/region enables partition pruning — queries filtering on date only scan matching partitions. Combining both can reduce scanned data by 95%+. Intelligent-Tiering affects storage cost, not query scanning cost. Gzip compression reduces bytes read but JSON is still row-based; all columns must be decompressed to read any field. Saved queries cache execution plans but re-scan the same underlying data.

q: A photo sharing application uploads user photos to Amazon S3 then generates three thumbnail sizes synchronously before returning a response to the user. Thumbnail generation takes 3–8 seconds causing poor user experience. The company wants to return a response immediately after upload and generate thumbnails asynchronously. Which architecture achieves this?
options: Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up the initial upload so thumbnail generation has more time before the timeout
Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate thumbnail generation as a workflow with parallel states for each thumbnail size
Deploy a separate EC2 instance running a thumbnail worker that polls S3 for new uploads every 30 seconds

Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function when a new photo is uploaded; the Lambda function generates all three thumbnails asynchronously and stores them back in S3; the API returns immediately after the upload completes
explain: S3 Event Notifications trigger Lambda automatically when an object is created. The upload API endpoint writes the original image to S3 and immediately returns HTTP 202 Accepted to the user — decoupling upload acknowledgment from processing. Lambda then generates thumbnails asynchronously without the user waiting. Step Functions could orchestrate thumbnail generation but adds complexity and still requires a trigger mechanism (S3 event notifications). S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads but doesn't change the synchronous processing model. EC2 polling introduces up to 30-second delays and is not event-driven.

q: A startup's REST API handles user authentication using JWT validation in every Lambda function. Each function imports and initializes a cryptographic library (adds 800ms cold start time) and downloads the JWT public key from an SSM Parameter Store endpoint on every invocation (adds 50ms per request). The team wants to reduce both cold start time and per-request latency without changing the authentication logic. Which solution addresses both issues?
options: Increase Lambda memory allocation from 128MB to 1024MB to speed up cryptographic operations and SSM API calls

Move the JWT library initialization and SSM public key fetch to the Lambda global scope (outside the handler function); cache the public key in a module-level variable; Lambda reuses the execution environment across warm invocations eliminating re-initialization cost
Use Lambda provisioned concurrency to pre-initialize all function instances and eliminate cold starts entirely
Use API Gateway Lambda authorizers to validate JWT tokens once per request instead of in each function
explain: Lambda execution environments are reused across invocations (warm invocations). Code in the global scope runs once per environment initialization and is available to all subsequent invocations of that environment. Moving library initialization and SSM fetches outside the handler means warm invocations skip both operations — the 800ms library init becomes a one-time cost and the cached public key eliminates the 50ms SSM call on every request. Provisioned concurrency eliminates cold starts but doesn't optimize per-request latency for the SSM call on warm invocations. Lambda authorizers centralize auth but don't fix the SSM per-call overhead. More memory speeds CPU but doesn't eliminate I/O latency.

q: A content platform allows users to upload videos up to 10GB in size. Currently videos are uploaded via a web server which buffers the entire file before writing to S3, creating timeouts and high memory consumption on the web tier. The company wants users to upload directly to S3 without routing through the web servers. Which mechanism allows browser-based direct uploads to S3 with appropriate access control?
options: Configure the S3 bucket as a public bucket with write-only permissions so browsers can upload without authentication

Generate a presigned S3 URL from the backend; the client uploads directly to S3 using the presigned URL which includes temporary credentials scoped to the specific object and expires after a configurable time
Use Amazon CloudFront with a custom origin to proxy S3 uploads so CloudFront handles the buffering instead of the web server
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration combined with the web server upload approach to speed up the upload without changing architecture
explain: S3 presigned URLs allow the backend to generate a time-limited URL that includes IAM credentials scoped to a specific S3 operation (PutObject) on a specific key. The browser uploads directly to S3 using this URL — the web server is only involved in URL generation, not data transfer. Uploads of any size stream directly to S3 without buffering. Making the bucket public introduces a security risk of unauthorized uploads. CloudFront proxying still routes through infrastructure (CloudFront instead of web servers) and doesn't eliminate buffering for large uploads. S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up the upload route but doesn't remove the web server from the upload path.

q: A company serves static web content through Amazon CloudFront. The origin is an S3 bucket in us-east-1. Analytics show that 15% of requests are cache misses that hit the origin, each adding 120ms of origin fetch latency. The CloudFront cache hit ratio is lower than expected because the same content is being requested with different query string parameters (e.g., ?v=1 and ?v=2 both fetch the same object). How should the solutions architect improve the cache hit ratio?
options: Set the S3 bucket as a website endpoint instead of REST endpoint so query strings are ignored by default
Configure the CloudFront cache policy to not forward query strings to the origin for static content; CloudFront treats URLs with different query strings as the same cache key
Increase the CloudFront default TTL from 24 hours to 7 days to reduce the frequency of cache expirations
Enable CloudFront origin shield as an additional caching layer between edge locations and the S3 origin
explain: By default CloudFront includes query strings in the cache key — requests for /image.jpg?v=1 and /image.jpg?v=2 are treated as different objects even if the underlying S3 object is identical. Configuring the cache policy to exclude query strings from the cache key means both requests serve the same cached object, dramatically improving hit ratio. Origin Shield adds a second layer of caching but doesn't solve the cache key fragmentation problem. S3 website endpoints handle query strings differently but this doesn't control CloudFront's cache key behavior. Increasing TTL reduces expiration-driven misses but doesn't fix query-string-driven cache fragmentation.

q: A company's application uses Amazon Aurora MySQL. During business hours the database handles 2,000 write transactions per second. The application also runs complex reporting queries that take 30–120 seconds and block OLTP performance. The solutions architect needs to isolate reporting workloads from OLTP workloads. Which solution achieves workload isolation with the least operational overhead?
options: Add Aurora Read Replicas and route reporting queries to the reader endpoint; Aurora's reader endpoint load-balances across all available replica instances isolating reporting from the primary write instance
Create a separate Aurora cluster for reporting; use AWS DMS to continuously replicate data from the production cluster to the reporting cluster
Use Aurora Serverless v2 for the reporting workload so it scales automatically based on query complexity
Enable Aurora Multi-Master so all instances handle both writes and reads distributing the load across all nodes
explain: Aurora Read Replicas share the same underlying storage volume as the primary but have independent compute. Routing reporting queries to the reader endpoint ensures long-running analytical queries consume replica CPU without competing with OLTP transactions on the primary. Aurora can have up to 15 read replicas and the reader endpoint load-balances automatically. A separate Aurora cluster with DMS replication adds operational complexity and replication lag. Aurora Multi-Master supports write scaling, not read workload isolation. Aurora Serverless v2 for reporting still requires routing query traffic appropriately, which read replicas already provide natively.

q: A company runs a microservices application on Amazon EKS. Several services communicate synchronously via HTTP, creating tight coupling and latency accumulation across the call chain. When one service is slow, the entire request chain backs up. The team wants to decouple the services and improve overall throughput. Which approach best decouples inter-service communication?
options: Replace synchronous HTTP calls between services with Amazon SQS queues for commands and Amazon SNS topics for events; each service processes messages independently at its own pace
Use Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as a shared cache between services so each service can retrieve data without making synchronous HTTP calls to dependencies
Deploy an Amazon API Gateway between each pair of services to throttle request rates and prevent backpressure
Use an AWS App Mesh service mesh to add circuit breakers and retries to existing HTTP calls between services
explain: Replacing synchronous HTTP with SQS/SNS decouples producer and consumer lifetimes. A slow consumer accumulates messages in the queue without blocking the producer or causing timeout cascades. Each service processes at its own capacity. SNS fan-out distributes events to multiple interested consumers without the publisher knowing about them. App Mesh with circuit breakers improves resilience but preserves synchronous coupling — a slow service still blocks callers. API Gateway throttling prevents overload but doesn't decouple request lifecycles. ElastiCache reduces calls for cacheable data but doesn't address synchronous coupling for write operations or uncacheable data.

q: An application uses Amazon DynamoDB for its main data store. The DynamoDB table uses a composite key (partition key: user_id, sort key: timestamp). The application frequently runs queries filtering by a product_id attribute which is not part of the key schema, requiring full table scans. Which solution allows efficient queries on product_id without changing the application's primary key schema?
options: Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) with product_id as the partition key; the GSI maintains a separate index that the application can query with O(1) partition lookup instead of a full scan
Create a Local Secondary Index (LSI) with product_id as the sort key to allow range queries on product_id within each user's partition
Enable DynamoDB Parallel Scan with multiple threads to scan the table faster when filtering by product_id
Use DynamoDB Streams to maintain a separate sorted list of items by product_id in an additional DynamoDB table
explain: A Global Secondary Index (GSI) creates a separate data structure with product_id as the partition key. Queries against the GSI use product_id as a direct lookup key — O(1) lookup performance regardless of table size. GSIs can have any attributes as partition and sort keys independent of the base table's key schema. Local Secondary Indexes (LSI) use the same partition key as the base table (user_id) and only allow range queries within a single user's items — they don't allow cross-user queries on product_id. DynamoDB Streams maintain change logs, not query indexes. Parallel Scan still reads all data — it's faster than sequential scan but fundamentally still O(n).

q: A company's API runs on AWS Lambda with a response time SLA of 200ms for 99% of requests. Cold starts are causing 5% of requests to exceed 800ms. The Lambda function uses a 512MB memory allocation and initializes a database connection pool and loads a 50MB ML model during startup. Which change most reduces cold start latency for this function?
options: Convert the Lambda function to a container image and store the ML model in the container so it doesn't need to be loaded from S3 at runtime

Enable Lambda provisioned concurrency for the function; AWS pre-initializes a specified number of execution environments and keeps them warm, eliminating cold start initialization for those environments
Package the ML model as a Lambda Layer so it is pre-deployed to the execution environment before the function code runs
Increase Lambda memory to 3,008MB; Lambda allocates CPU proportionally to memory so more memory means faster initialization of the connection pool and ML model
explain: Lambda Provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes a specified number of execution environments and keeps them initialized. Requests routed to provisioned instances have zero cold start latency because the function is already initialized — connection pool is open, ML model is loaded. The 5% of cold-start requests will hit provisioned environments and respond in <200ms. Increasing memory speeds up initialization somewhat but doesn't eliminate it. Lambda Layers are extracted to /opt during initialization — they don't bypass the init phase. Container images are another deployment mechanism but the model loading still occurs at cold start unless provisioned concurrency is used.

q: A company runs hundreds of batch jobs on Amazon EC2 using AWS Batch. Jobs process CSV files from S3 and each job runs for 2–4 hours. The jobs have been failing at a high rate because Spot Instances are being terminated mid-job. The team needs to handle Spot interruptions gracefully so jobs can resume rather than restart from scratch. Which solution enables job resumption after Spot interruption?
options: Switch from Spot Instances to On-Demand Instances in the Batch compute environment; On-Demand Instances are never interrupted and jobs always complete
Configure the Batch job retry strategy with 10 retries; when a Spot Instance is interrupted the job automatically retries on a new instance
Use AWS Batch Array Jobs to split each CSV into 1,000 smaller tasks; each small task completes quickly before Spot interruption can occur

Enable checkpointing in the job application code: periodically write progress (last processed S3 key and byte offset) to Amazon DynamoDB; when a Spot interruption occurs and the job restarts on a new instance, read the checkpoint and resume from where it left off
explain: Application-level checkpointing is the correct pattern for long-running Spot workloads. Writing progress to DynamoDB (or S3) at regular intervals (e.g., every 5 minutes of processing) means that when a Spot interruption occurs and the job restarts on a new instance, it reads the latest checkpoint and skips already-processed data. This converts a 2-4 hour restart into a minutes-long resume. On-Demand eliminates interruptions but eliminates Spot cost savings. Retry strategies restart the job from the beginning — with 2-4 hour jobs this wastes significant compute. Array jobs reduce per-task duration but don't solve resumption for tasks that are interrupted mid-execution.

q: A company processes financial transactions using a Lambda function connected to an Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL database in a VPC. During peak hours, the Lambda function creates thousands of new database connections per minute because each Lambda invocation opens a new connection. The database is running out of connections. The company wants to solve this without modifying the Lambda function's database interaction code. Which solution resolves this?
options: Increase the max_connections parameter in the RDS parameter group to allow more simultaneous connections
Move the Lambda function out of the VPC so connections go through the public endpoint which has higher connection limits

Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy in the same VPC; update the Lambda function's database connection string to point to the RDS Proxy endpoint instead of the RDS endpoint; RDS Proxy pools and reuses connections transparently
Enable RDS Multi-AZ so the standby can absorb some connection load from the primary
explain: Amazon RDS Proxy sits between Lambda and RDS, maintaining a warm pool of persistent database connections. Lambda invocations connect to RDS Proxy (fast, lightweight) and Proxy multiplexes these onto a much smaller number of actual database connections. Since the connection string simply changes from the RDS endpoint to the Proxy endpoint, no database interaction code changes are needed. RDS Multi-AZ standby doesn't serve connections from the primary. Increasing max_connections increases the limit but PostgreSQL still has per-connection memory overhead — at thousands of connections this causes out-of-memory issues. Moving Lambda outside the VPC doesn't reduce connection count.

q: A company hosts a mobile gaming API on Amazon API Gateway backed by Lambda. During a game tournament, API calls spike to 50,000 requests per second. The Lambda functions start throttling because the account's concurrency limit is reached. The API must handle the spike without increasing the Lambda concurrency limit. Which solution handles the spike without throttling?
options: Use API Gateway throttling to limit requests to 5,000 per second and reject excess requests with HTTP 429 responses
Deploy the Lambda functions to multiple AWS regions and use Route 53 weighted routing to split traffic between regions

Place Amazon SQS between API Gateway and Lambda; API Gateway writes requests to SQS synchronously and returns 202 Accepted immediately; Lambda polls SQS and processes requests at a controlled rate within concurrency limits; clients poll a separate status endpoint for results
Enable API Gateway caching for all endpoints with a 60-second TTL to reduce the number of Lambda invocations during the tournament
explain: The async SQS pattern decouples API acceptance from Lambda processing. API Gateway accepts all 50,000 RPS immediately (SQS is virtually unlimited throughput), writes to the queue, and returns 202. Lambda processes from the queue at whatever rate the concurrency limit allows without throttling errors visible to clients. This changes the API from synchronous to asynchronous, which is appropriate for game tournament actions that don't need immediate responses. API Gateway caching only helps for identical read requests — tournaments have unique user actions. Multi-region deployment splits the throttling across regions (each still hits limits). API Gateway throttling with 429 responses is exactly the user-visible throttling the question wants to avoid.

q: A company's application queries an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL cluster. Queries filtering on the customer_segment column (values: 'enterprise', 'mid-market', 'smb') with fewer than 5% of rows per segment are extremely slow even with an index on customer_segment. The explain plan shows Aurora is using a full table scan instead of the index. What is the most likely cause and solution?
options: Convert the customer_segment column from VARCHAR to an ENUM type so Aurora can use bitmap index scans instead of B-tree scans

Aurora's query optimizer determines that with only 3 distinct values covering millions of rows, a full table scan is more efficient than the index for broad segment queries; create a partial index or composite index including a more selective additional column such as created_at to improve selectivity
The customer_segment column needs VACUUM ANALYZE run to update table statistics; stale statistics cause the planner to choose incorrect execution plans
Add more Aurora read replicas to distribute the query load so full table scans complete faster
explain: PostgreSQL query planner avoids indexes when the indexed column has low cardinality and many matching rows (high percentage of table). For customer_segment with 3 values and millions of rows per segment, a sequential scan is often faster than random I/O index access. A composite index (customer_segment, created_at) dramatically increases selectivity — queries for a specific segment in a date range match far fewer rows. Alternatively, a partial index only on a specific segment (WHERE customer_segment='enterprise') reduces index size. VACUUM ANALYZE helps with stale statistics but won't fix a fundamentally low-selectivity index design. More read replicas speed up scans but don't fix the inefficiency.

q: A company needs to migrate 200TB of on-premises data to Amazon S3 within 5 days. The on-premises data center has a 1Gbps internet connection shared with business operations. The company cannot dedicate the full bandwidth to migration without impacting business operations. The maximum dedicated bandwidth for migration is 200Mbps. Which solution completes the migration within the time constraint?
options: Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to maximize throughput and transfer all 200TB over the 200Mbps connection within 5 days
Use AWS DataSync over the existing internet connection throttled to 200Mbps; DataSync will complete within 5 days
Use AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized devices; ship multiple devices to the on-premises location, load 72TB per device, and ship them back to AWS for import into S3
Use AWS Direct Connect to provision a 1Gbps dedicated circuit and transfer the data at full speed within 5 days
explain: At 200Mbps, the theoretical maximum transfer rate is 200Mbps × 86400 sec × 5 days = 86.4TB — well below the 200TB requirement. Snowball Edge Storage Optimized holds 80TB usable per device. Three devices handle 200TB+ and physical shipment to AWS takes 1-2 days, well within 5 days. DataSync at 200Mbps cannot transfer 200TB in 5 days (physical bandwidth math). S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes routing over existing connections but cannot exceed the available 200Mbps. Direct Connect provisioning takes weeks, not days, making it unavailable within the 5-day window.

q: A news website uses Amazon CloudFront to serve articles. Breaking news articles change frequently (every 5 minutes) while archived articles rarely change. Currently all articles have a 24-hour TTL causing breaking news to show stale content for up to 24 hours. The team wants breaking news to be fresh within 1 minute while archived articles continue using long TTLs. Which CloudFront feature addresses this?
options: Enable CloudFront real-time logs and use a Lambda function to detect stale breaking news and trigger invalidations automatically

Create separate CloudFront cache behaviors: one for /breaking/* paths with a 60-second TTL and another for /archive/* paths with a 30-day TTL; CloudFront selects the behavior based on URL path pattern
Use CloudFront invalidations to clear the cache for breaking news articles whenever they are updated
Use Lambda@Edge at the origin response event to set different Cache-Control headers based on the article type
explain: CloudFront cache behaviors match request URL patterns to different caching policies. A behavior for /breaking/* with min/max/default TTL of 60 seconds ensures breaking news is fetched from origin every minute. A behavior for /archive/* with 30-day TTL avoids origin fetches for stable content. This is declarative, requires no code, and applies precisely to the right content. Cache invalidations require a call per invalidated object — with many breaking news URLs updated frequently this becomes expensive and complex. Lambda@Edge setting Cache-Control headers works but requires deploying and maintaining code when a simple cache behavior achieves the same result. Real-time log monitoring adds unnecessary complexity.

q: A company runs a document processing pipeline where uploaded PDFs are converted to text, analyzed for sentiment, and stored in a search index. Each step has different compute requirements and durations. Currently all steps run sequentially in a single Lambda function causing timeouts for large documents. The company wants to break the pipeline into distinct steps that can be monitored, retried independently, and scaled separately. Which AWS service orchestrates this best?
options: AWS Batch with job dependencies; define a job queue for each step and configure dependencies so each job runs after the previous completes

AWS Step Functions Standard Workflows; define each processing step as a state in the state machine with retry/catch policies; Step Functions tracks state, handles errors, and allows visual monitoring of pipeline execution
Amazon EventBridge rules chaining Lambda functions; each Lambda publishes a completion event that triggers the next Lambda in the pipeline
Amazon SQS queues chaining Lambda functions; each Lambda writes its output to the next SQS queue in the pipeline
explain: AWS Step Functions is purpose-built for multi-step workflow orchestration. Each pipeline step is a distinct state with its own retry configuration, timeout, error handling, and Lambda (or ECS/Batch) integration. The Step Functions console shows a visual execution graph for monitoring. State machines handle error branching automatically. EventBridge chaining works but error handling and state tracking must be implemented in each Lambda. AWS Batch job dependencies are designed for compute-intensive batch workloads, not event-driven pipeline steps with different latency profiles. SQS chaining provides decoupling but lacks built-in error handling, retry management, and execution visibility.

q: A company's application uses Amazon S3 for object storage. The application generates millions of objects with keys like 'logs/2024/01/15/server-1/access.log.gz'. Queries regularly need all logs for a specific day across all servers. Currently these queries use ListObjects with a prefix filter but are slow due to the volume of objects. The team wants queries to run in seconds. Which solution enables fast time-based retrieval?
options: Use S3 Inventory to generate daily manifest files listing all S3 objects; query the inventory manifest using Amazon Athena with SQL filtering on last-modified date
Restructure the S3 key prefix to put date at the top level: 'logs/2024-01-15/server-1/access.log.gz'; create an S3 Inventory + Athena table partitioned by date for metadata queries
Enable S3 Event Notifications to maintain a DynamoDB table as an index of all objects keyed by date; query DynamoDB to find relevant object keys then retrieve from S3
Use Amazon S3 Select on each log file to extract relevant entries without downloading full files
explain: Restructuring the key prefix to date-first allows S3 ListObjects with prefix 'logs/2024-01-15/' to enumerate all servers' logs for a specific day without scanning the entire bucket. S3 Inventory generates daily manifests in Parquet format which Athena can query using partitions on the date field — this scales to billions of objects. Together these enable sub-second date-filtered object discovery. S3 Inventory alone with default key structure still requires full scans of the inventory file. S3 Select works per-object but doesn't solve the metadata query problem of finding which objects to read. DynamoDB indexing works but requires maintaining the index on every object creation — complex and costly at millions of objects.

q: A ride-sharing company stores trip records in Amazon DynamoDB. Each trip record contains a large amount of telemetry data (GPS points, speed readings) stored as an attribute, averaging 380KB per item. The application retrieves the full item for display on a driver dashboard. Read costs are very high because each GetItem retrieves the full 380KB item. Most dashboard reads only need trip summary attributes (origin, destination, duration, fare). Which optimization reduces read costs most significantly?
options: Compress the telemetry data before storing in DynamoDB to reduce item size below 400KB and lower read unit consumption
Use a DynamoDB projection expression in GetItem requests to retrieve only the summary attributes needed for the dashboard; DynamoDB charges per KB read and smaller projections cost less
Enable DynamoDB DAX caching; once a trip is read once it is served from cache on subsequent reads eliminating DynamoDB read costs for hot items

Split the DynamoDB item into two separate items: one with summary attributes (keyed by trip_id) and one with telemetry data (keyed by trip_id#telemetry); retrieve only the summary item for dashboard queries
explain: DynamoDB charges for read capacity units based on the actual size of the data read per request. A ProjectionExpression specifies which attributes to return — DynamoDB still reads the full item from storage but the RCU cost is based on the projected item size. If summary attributes are 2KB of the 380KB item, each GetItem costs ~1 RCU (2KB) instead of ~60 RCUs (380KB). This is a 60x cost reduction with a one-line code change. Splitting the item requires schema changes, dual-write logic, and key management complexity. DAX reduces costs for frequently read items but doesn't help for first-read or rarely-read trips. Compression reduces storage but DynamoDB charges based on uncompressed item size for capacity units.

q: A company uses AWS Lambda to process incoming webhooks from a payment provider. The Lambda function calls downstream APIs that sometimes take 8–10 seconds to respond. Lambda is configured with a 30-second timeout. During load testing the team notices that when downstream APIs are slow, Lambda accumulates thousands of concurrent invocations while waiting, hitting concurrency limits. Which pattern prevents concurrency accumulation from slow downstream dependencies?
options: Use Lambda Power Tuning to find the optimal memory/timeout configuration that minimizes the impact of slow downstream calls
Increase the Lambda timeout to 5 minutes so there is more headroom before timeout errors
Set Lambda reserved concurrency to 1,000 to guarantee capacity for the webhook processing function

Implement a circuit breaker pattern in the Lambda function: track downstream API failure/timeout rates in ElastiCache; when the failure rate exceeds a threshold, fail fast and return an error immediately rather than waiting for the timeout
explain: The circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures. When the downstream API is slow, without a circuit breaker each Lambda invocation waits the full 8-10 seconds while consuming a concurrency slot. With a circuit breaker (state stored in ElastiCache): when failure rate exceeds threshold (e.g., 50% of calls timing out in 30 seconds), the circuit opens and subsequent invocations fail immediately in <1ms without waiting. This frees concurrency slots rapidly. Increasing timeout makes each slow invocation hold its concurrency slot longer, worsening the accumulation. Reserved concurrency guarantees capacity but doesn't reduce accumulation. Power Tuning optimizes memory allocation but doesn't address the fundamental slow dependency problem.

q: A company hosts a multi-tenant SaaS application where each tenant has a separate Amazon Aurora MySQL database cluster. As the company grows to 1,000 tenants, managing 1,000 separate clusters becomes operationally challenging and expensive. The company wants to reduce the number of clusters while maintaining tenant isolation at the database level. Which approach achieves this?
options: Migrate to Aurora with separate schemas per tenant within a single cluster; use IAM database authentication with per-tenant IAM roles that have access only to their schema; row-level security in MySQL views enforces data isolation
Use Aurora Custom Endpoints to create per-tenant endpoints that route queries to dedicated read replicas serving only that tenant's data
Use Amazon DynamoDB with a composite primary key of tenant_id and record_id to consolidate all tenant data in one managed service
Migrate all tenants to a single Aurora cluster with a tenant_id column in each table; use application-level filtering to ensure each tenant only sees their data
explain: Aurora allows multiple schemas (databases) within a single cluster. Assigning each tenant a dedicated schema with MySQL GRANT privileges restricted to that schema provides database-level isolation without separate clusters. IAM database authentication maps IAM roles to MySQL users — each tenant's application role authenticates with a token and connects as a MySQL user with only their schema permissions. This reduces 1,000 clusters to a handful while maintaining isolation. Single-table with tenant_id relies entirely on application filtering — a bug exposes all tenant data (no database-level enforcement). DynamoDB requires a NoSQL migration and architectural change. Aurora Custom Endpoints route to read replicas but don't provide schema-level isolation.

q: A company's Lambda function processes images uploaded to S3. The function downloads the image from S3, resizes it to five different resolutions, and uploads all five variants back to S3. The current implementation resizes images sequentially, taking 15–25 seconds per image. The team wants to reduce processing time to under 5 seconds. Which solution achieves this without changing the overall architecture?
options: Enable Lambda reserved concurrency of 5 so five resize operations can execute in parallel across five concurrent Lambda invocations

Refactor the Lambda function to run the five resize operations concurrently using Python's concurrent.futures ThreadPoolExecutor; since resizing is CPU-bound, also increase Lambda memory to allocate more CPU proportionally
Use AWS Elemental MediaConvert for image resizing which uses GPU acceleration and completes five resizes in parallel in under 1 second

Break the function into six separate Lambda functions: one coordinator that triggers five parallel resizer functions via SNS fan-out or Step Functions Map state; each resizer handles one resolution concurrently
explain: The most effective approach is parallelism at the function level. A Step Functions Express Workflow with a Map state (or SNS fan-out) invokes five separate Lambda resizer functions concurrently — total time is the duration of the slowest resize rather than the sum of all five. If each resize takes 4 seconds, parallel execution completes in 4 seconds instead of 20. ThreadPoolExecutor within a single Lambda provides concurrency but Lambda vCPU allocation is proportional to memory — at 512MB this is limited CPU for image processing. Resize is CPU-bound, so threads share a single GIL in Python and provide limited benefit. Reserved concurrency of 5 guarantees capacity but each invocation still processes one resize — you need orchestration to invoke them in parallel. MediaConvert is a video service, not an image processing service.

q: A company processes clickstream data from its website. Events arrive at 50,000 events per second. The data science team needs to run machine learning models on sliding 5-minute windows of data to detect fraud patterns in real time with less than 30 seconds of end-to-end latency. Which architecture supports this requirement?
options: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams → Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) with tumbling 5-minute windows → Lambda for ML inference → Amazon DynamoDB for results
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams → AWS Glue Streaming ETL job (5-minute micro-batches) → SageMaker endpoint → DynamoDB
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose → Amazon S3 → Amazon SageMaker batch transform job every 5 minutes → results to DynamoDB
Amazon SQS → Lambda (aggregate 5 minutes of events) → SageMaker endpoint → DynamoDB
explain: Kinesis Data Streams ingests 50,000 events/second with low latency. Kinesis Data Analytics with Apache Flink supports sliding and tumbling time windows — a 5-minute tumbling window aggregates events and triggers downstream processing. Flink then invokes a Lambda function or SageMaker endpoint for ML inference, with total latency well under 30 seconds. Kinesis Firehose has a minimum 60-second buffer window before S3 delivery, immediately violating the 30-second latency requirement. SQS + Lambda accumulating 5 minutes of events requires custom windowing logic and doesn't scale to 50,000 events/second within Lambda memory limits. Glue Streaming ETL has higher latency for micro-batch processing compared to Flink's native streaming.

q: A company runs a high-traffic e-commerce API using Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda. During sales events, API Gateway receives 100,000 requests per second. Some product detail endpoints are called 80% of the time with the same product IDs. Each Lambda invocation calls DynamoDB to fetch product data. The team wants to reduce DynamoDB calls and Lambda invocations for popular products. Which solution achieves this with the least code change?
options: Add Amazon ElastiCache for Redis in the application layer; modify Lambda to check Redis before querying DynamoDB
Create CloudFront in front of API Gateway; configure CloudFront to cache product detail responses at edge locations for 60 seconds
Use DynamoDB DAX to cache DynamoDB responses; Lambda invocations still occur but DynamoDB is offloaded

Enable API Gateway caching for the product detail endpoint; configure a cache TTL of 60 seconds keyed on the product ID path parameter; API Gateway serves cached responses directly without invoking Lambda or DynamoDB
explain: API Gateway's built-in response cache stores responses keyed by method and path parameters. With caching enabled for the product detail endpoint, requests for the same product ID return the cached response directly from API Gateway without invoking Lambda or calling DynamoDB. This eliminates Lambda invocation costs and DynamoDB read costs for popular products during the 60-second TTL window. At 80% hit rate on popular products, Lambda invocations drop by 80%. ElastiCache for Redis requires Lambda code changes to implement cache-aside logic. DAX reduces DynamoDB latency but still invokes Lambda for every request. CloudFront caching is valid but adds a CDN layer — API Gateway's built-in cache is simpler and achieves the same goal for API responses.

q: A company runs a distributed application across three AWS regions. The application needs a globally consistent configuration store that replicates configuration changes to all regions within seconds. When a configuration value is updated in one region, services in all other regions must read the updated value within 5 seconds. Which AWS service provides this capability?
options: Use Amazon S3 Cross-Region Replication with event notifications triggering Lambda in each region to update local configuration files on EC2 instances
Deploy AWS CloudFormation StackSets to push configuration updates to all regions; StackSets completes updates across regions within minutes
Amazon DynamoDB Global Tables; store configuration key-value pairs in a Global Table that automatically replicates writes to all configured regions within 1-2 seconds
AWS AppConfig with AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store replication across regions using EventBridge cross-region event buses to propagate changes and trigger SSM parameter updates in each region
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables provide active-active multi-region replication with typical replication lag under 1 second, well within the 5-second requirement. Writes to any region replica are automatically replicated to all other configured region replicas. Services in each region read from their local DynamoDB replica for low latency. Global Tables use last-writer-wins conflict resolution for concurrent writes. AppConfig + EventBridge cross-region propagation involves multiple service hops and could exceed 5 seconds in complex scenarios. S3 Cross-Region Replication has variable replication time (typically seconds to minutes) without strong latency guarantees. CloudFormation StackSets takes minutes to complete cross-region updates.

q: A company's web application uses Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL. During a database version upgrade the application experienced 2 minutes of downtime during the failover. The company wants future upgrades to result in zero application downtime. Which approach achieves zero-downtime PostgreSQL upgrades?
options: Create a read replica running the target PostgreSQL version; promote the read replica and update the application connection string during a maintenance window
Configure RDS Multi-AZ and perform the upgrade during the maintenance window; Multi-AZ reduces upgrade downtime from 20 minutes to under 2 minutes
Use AWS DMS to replicate from the current RDS instance to a new RDS instance running the upgraded version; perform a cutover during a low-traffic period
Use Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL instead of RDS PostgreSQL; Aurora supports zero-downtime patching (ZDP) for minor version upgrades which migrates connections to the new version without dropping them
explain: Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL's Zero-Downtime Patching (ZDP) feature preserves existing database connections during minor version upgrades by migrating them to upgraded instances transparently. Application queries experience minimal interruption (sub-second) without connection drops. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ reduces upgrade downtime to the failover time (30-60 seconds) but doesn't achieve zero downtime. AWS DMS replication for major version upgrades works but requires a maintenance cutover window with brief downtime. Promoting a read replica requires application connection string changes and causes brief downtime during DNS propagation.

q: A company stores sensor data in Amazon DynamoDB. Each sensor writes a reading every second. With 100,000 sensors the table receives 100,000 writes per second. The table uses sensor_id as the partition key. DynamoDB is returning ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors for some sensors despite the overall table throughput appearing sufficient. What is the most likely cause and solution?
options: Enable DynamoDB DAX caching to absorb the write traffic before it reaches DynamoDB
Switch to DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode to eliminate the need for capacity planning entirely
The table has insufficient provisioned capacity; enable DynamoDB Auto Scaling to automatically increase capacity when throttling occurs

Hot partition keys: some sensors write at a much higher rate than others, overwhelming specific DynamoDB partitions; add a random suffix (1-10) to the sensor_id partition key and write parallel items; aggregate by sensor on read using query with the base sensor_id prefix
explain: DynamoDB distributes data across partitions by partition key. If some sensors are more active (e.g., sensor for a critical system writes 100x/second while others write 1x/second), those sensors' partition keys become hot — that partition's 3,000 RCU/3,000 WCU limit is exceeded even though aggregate table capacity is sufficient. Write sharding by appending a random suffix distributes writes across multiple partition key values. Reads aggregate across all suffixes. Auto Scaling increases overall capacity but doesn't solve hot partition throughput limits per partition. DAX is a read cache and doesn't improve write performance. On-demand capacity eliminates manual provisioning but doesn't prevent hot partition throttling — on-demand also has per-partition limits.

q: A company needs to process 10TB of data daily using Amazon EMR. Jobs currently run on On-Demand instances and complete in 6 hours. The company wants to reduce EMR costs by 60% without significantly increasing job completion time. Which approach achieves this?
options: Use Spot Instances for EMR task nodes and On-Demand for core/master nodes; configure EMR instance fleets to bid on multiple Spot instance types across multiple AZs to minimize interruption probability; task nodes can be terminated and replaced without job failure
Configure EMR auto-scaling to scale down to 0 nodes between jobs to avoid paying for idle cluster time
Replace EMR with AWS Glue for all data processing; Glue's serverless model eliminates cluster management overhead
Use EMR reserved instances by purchasing 1-year reservations for the master and all core nodes
explain: Using Spot Instances for task nodes achieves 60-70% cost savings vs On-Demand. Task nodes in EMR process data but don't store HDFS data — if a task node is interrupted, YARN reschedules the task on another node without job failure. Core and master nodes must be On-Demand since they store HDFS data and manage the cluster. EMR instance fleets with multiple instance types across AZs minimize Spot interruption probability by bidding on the spot pool with available capacity. AWS Glue requires code migration and doesn't guarantee 60% savings for all workload types. Reserved instances require 1-year commitment and are less flexible than Spot. EMR clusters take 5-10 minutes to start — scaling to 0 between jobs adds startup delay.

q: A company uses Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with 10 shards. Each shard processes 1MB/s of incoming data. The company's Lambda consumer function processes records but struggles to keep up with the throughput, causing consumer lag to grow continuously. The Lambda function processes records in batches of 100 and completes each batch in 500ms. How can the company increase consumer throughput?
options: Increase the Lambda batch size from 100 to 10,000 records per batch so each Lambda invocation processes more records in the same 500ms window
Increase Lambda memory from 128MB to 3,008MB to allocate more CPU for faster record processing

Enable Enhanced Fan-Out on the Kinesis stream; Enhanced Fan-Out gives each registered consumer a dedicated 2MB/s pipe per shard (instead of sharing 2MB/s across all consumers) and pushes records to consumers via HTTP/2 eliminating polling latency
Increase the number of Kinesis shards from 10 to 20; more shards means more parallelism for the Lambda consumer
explain: The consumer lag growing indicates the consumer cannot process data as fast as it arrives. Increasing shards from 10 to 20 doubles the stream's throughput capacity (from 10MB/s to 20MB/s ingestion) AND doubles Lambda consumer parallelism — Lambda processes up to 1 concurrent invocation per shard per batch. With 20 shards, 20 Lambda invocations run in parallel vs 10. Enhanced Fan-Out gives dedicated 2MB/s per shard but Lambda event source mappings already use Enhanced Fan-Out automatically — enabling it again for an existing Lambda trigger doesn't add benefit. Increasing batch size processes more records per invocation but if processing time exceeds the Kinesis iterator age, lag still grows. Memory affects Lambda CPU speed but the bottleneck is throughput capacity, not per-record processing speed.

q: A company runs a SaaS application where each customer has thousands of configuration settings stored in Amazon DynamoDB. When a customer logs in, the application loads all their configuration settings in a single GetItem call returning a 380KB item. Page load times are slow due to the large item reads. The company wants to reduce the data transferred without changing the DynamoDB schema or primary key design. Which approach reduces data transferred per login?
options: Use DynamoDB Streams to detect configuration changes and push updates to clients using WebSockets, eliminating bulk reads on login
Enable DynamoDB DAX caching; subsequent logins for the same user serve the full 380KB item from memory with sub-millisecond latency instead of millisecond DynamoDB reads
Enable DynamoDB compression by setting the data encoding option to compress all items automatically before storage, reducing the 380KB to approximately 80KB

Use DynamoDB ProjectionExpression in GetItem to retrieve only the configuration attributes needed for initial page load (typically 10-20 settings); fetch remaining settings on demand as the user navigates to different sections
explain: ProjectionExpression allows retrieving a subset of attributes from a DynamoDB item. If the login page requires only 15 of 1,000 configuration settings, a ProjectionExpression fetching those 15 attributes reads and transfers only the bytes needed for those attributes (possibly 5-10KB vs 380KB). Additional settings are fetched on demand using subsequent ProjectionExpression queries. DynamoDB still charges RCUs based on the projected item size, reducing both cost and latency. DAX improves read latency for warm cache hits but transfers the full 380KB from DynamoDB to DAX on first read and back to the application. DynamoDB Streams is for change propagation, not reducing initial load data. DynamoDB does not have a built-in automatic compression option.

q: A company's application runs on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The application serves both API requests (JSON, small payloads) and file downloads (large PDFs, 10–100MB each). Large file downloads are consuming most of the EC2 bandwidth and CPU, leaving insufficient resources for API requests. The company wants to offload file downloads without changing the API endpoint URLs. Which solution offloads file serving without URL changes?
options: Move files to S3 and deploy an S3 static website endpoint; update DNS to route file download paths to the S3 website endpoint

Store files in Amazon S3; configure the application to generate presigned S3 URLs for download requests and redirect clients with HTTP 302 to the presigned URL; clients download directly from S3 bypassing EC2
Use AWS Global Accelerator to route file download traffic to an S3 bucket directly, bypassing the ALB and EC2 instances
Put Amazon CloudFront in front of the ALB; configure CloudFront to cache file downloads at edge locations so subsequent requests for the same file are served from CloudFront cache without hitting EC2
explain: Generating S3 presigned URLs and responding with HTTP 302 redirects transparently offloads file downloads from EC2. The application receives the download request, generates a presigned URL for the S3 object, and returns a redirect — the client then downloads directly from S3. EC2 handles only the tiny redirect response, not the 10-100MB file transfer. The API endpoint URLs remain unchanged. CloudFront caching is effective for repeated downloads of the same file but EC2 still serves each unique file download for the first request. Updating DNS for specific paths changes URLs. Global Accelerator optimizes routing to endpoints but doesn't eliminate EC2 from the path.

q: A company uses AWS Lambda to run event processing functions. Functions are triggered by Amazon SQS. During testing the team notices that when a function fails to process a message, it retries three times and then the message is lost. The company needs to capture all failed messages for analysis and reprocessing without losing any. Which configuration preserves failed messages?
options: Enable Lambda function error handling with a try/catch that publishes failures to SNS before re-raising the exception
Configure the Lambda function's destination with an OnFailure destination pointing to an SQS queue; Lambda routes failed invocations to the destination queue
Set Lambda reserved concurrency to 0 temporarily to pause message processing when failure rates are high, preventing message loss
Configure a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) on the SQS source queue with maxReceiveCount=3; failed messages that exceed the retry count are automatically moved to the DLQ for later analysis and reprocessing
explain: SQS Dead Letter Queues capture messages that fail processing after the configured maxReceiveCount attempts. With maxReceiveCount=3, a message that fails all three Lambda retries is automatically moved to the DLQ rather than being deleted. The DLQ retains messages for the configured retention period (up to 14 days) for analysis and reprocessing. Lambda's OnFailure destination works for asynchronous invocations (EventBridge, S3, SNS triggers) but not for SQS-triggered Lambda — the SQS trigger uses polling and the DLQ must be on the source SQS queue. Lambda try/catch with SNS notification requires code changes and adds complexity. Setting concurrency to 0 stops processing entirely and causes SQS messages to expire.

q: A company's data lake on Amazon S3 is queried by hundreds of analysts using Amazon Athena. Each analyst runs similar queries but with slightly different date ranges and filters, resulting in full partition scans for each unique query. The company wants to enable faster repeated query execution for common query patterns. Which Athena feature caches and reuses previous query results?
options: Use Athena prepared statements for common query templates; prepared statements are pre-compiled and execute faster than ad-hoc queries
Configure Athena workgroups with per-workgroup query result locations; queries in the same workgroup share result files reducing duplicate scans

Enable Athena query result reuse; Athena stores query results in S3 and automatically serves cached results for identical queries submitted within a configurable reuse age (up to 7 days) without re-scanning underlying data
Create Athena named queries for common patterns; Athena pre-executes named queries daily and stores results for instant analyst access
explain: Athena's query result reuse feature (introduced 2022) allows analysts to reuse results from a previous identical query within a configurable time window. When an analyst submits a query that is identical to a recent query whose results are still within the reuse age, Athena returns the cached results from S3 immediately without scanning any data — zero scan cost, sub-second response. This is ideal for common query patterns like daily dashboards run by multiple analysts. Workgroup result locations organize output but don't cache or reuse previous query results. Prepared statements improve parameterized query parsing but don't cache results. Named queries save query text but don't pre-execute.

q: A company operates a multi-tier application on Amazon EC2 with an ALB at the front, application servers in a private subnet, and an RDS MySQL database. The application is CPU-bound on the application tier during peak hours. Auto Scaling is configured to add EC2 instances when CPU exceeds 70%. New instances take 8 minutes to start, pass health checks, and begin serving traffic. The company wants new instances to be ready within 2 minutes of a scaling event. Which approach reduces scale-out time?
options: Configure Auto Scaling warm pools: maintain a pool of stopped or running EC2 instances in a pre-initialized state; when scaling out, instances from the warm pool are activated immediately rather than launching new instances from scratch
Use a lighter-weight EC2 instance type so instances start faster
Enable EC2 detailed monitoring (1-minute intervals) so Auto Scaling triggers earlier, giving more lead time before traffic overwhelms existing instances
Switch from EC2 Auto Scaling to AWS Lambda for the application tier to eliminate instance startup time entirely
explain: Auto Scaling warm pools maintain pre-initialized EC2 instances in either stopped or running state (but out of the ALB). When a scale-out event occurs, warm pool instances are activated (started from stopped state) in 30-90 seconds rather than launching from scratch (8+ minutes). The application is pre-installed and configured on warm pool instances — only the final startup steps (instance initialization, health check passing) are needed. Instance type doesn't significantly affect application bootstrap time — the 8-minute delay is dominated by application startup, not hardware provisioning. Migrating to Lambda requires full application re-architecture. Earlier monitoring triggers faster, but instances still take 8 minutes to launch even if the trigger fires earlier.

q: A company's application uses Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as a session store. The Redis cluster is a single node. If the node fails, all user sessions are lost requiring users to re-authenticate. The company wants to ensure sessions survive a node failure with zero data loss. Which ElastiCache configuration provides this?
options: Enable ElastiCache Redis Cluster Mode (sharding) with multiple shards; sharding distributes session data across multiple nodes so a single node failure only affects a fraction of sessions
Use ElastiCache Redis with AOF (Append Only File) persistence enabled; AOF logs every write to disk so a failed node can recover its data after restart without data loss
Create two separate ElastiCache Redis clusters in different AZs and write session data to both clusters simultaneously using the application's write-through logic

Switch to an ElastiCache Redis cluster with Multi-AZ enabled and at least one read replica; ElastiCache automatically promotes the replica to primary within 1-2 minutes of primary failure with no data loss due to synchronous replication
explain: ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ with a read replica uses synchronous replication — every write to the primary is confirmed on the replica before acknowledging the client. When the primary fails, ElastiCache promotes the replica to primary (typically within 1-2 minutes) with zero data loss. The application's DNS endpoint automatically points to the new primary. Cluster Mode (sharding) improves scalability but a single node failure in a shard still loses that shard's session data without replicas. AOF persistence enables recovery after restart but requires the node to come back online — if the node hardware fails, AOF data may be lost. Dual-cluster write requires complex application logic and has no automatic failover.

q: A company runs a machine learning inference service on Amazon ECS using Fargate. The service uses GPU-accelerated models but Fargate does not support GPU instances. The team needs GPU acceleration for inference with minimal infrastructure management. Which solution provides GPU-based inference with the least operational overhead?
options: Use AWS Inferentia chips via Amazon EC2 Inf1 instances managed by the team using ECS EC2 launch type

Deploy the ML inference service on Amazon ECS with EC2 launch type using p3 or g4dn GPU instances; configure ECS to deploy containers with GPU resource requirements; EC2-based ECS provides GPU access with less management than self-managed EC2
Use Amazon SageMaker real-time inference endpoints with GPU instance types; SageMaker manages the underlying GPU infrastructure, auto-scales based on request volume, and handles model deployment
Migrate the inference service to AWS Lambda with a GPU-enabled runtime layer available in AWS Lambda for ML inference workloads
explain: Amazon SageMaker real-time inference endpoints are the fully managed option for GPU-accelerated ML inference. SageMaker handles provisioning GPU instances (ml.g4dn, ml.p3), auto-scaling based on invocation volume, model deployment, health monitoring, and A/B testing. The team deploys a model artifact and SageMaker manages the rest. ECS with EC2 launch type and GPU instances works but requires managing EC2 instances, GPU drivers, ECS agent configuration, and cluster capacity. Lambda does not support GPU execution environments. EC2 Inf1 instances provide AWS custom ML accelerators but require self-managed EC2 infrastructure.

q: A company serves personalized product recommendations through an API. Recommendations are computed by a complex ML model and take 2 seconds to generate per user. With 100,000 concurrent users the API cannot meet a 200ms response time SLA. The recommendations are valid for 10 minutes (user behavior doesn't change significantly in that window). Which architecture meets the 200ms SLA?
options: Pre-compute recommendations for all active users every 10 minutes using an offline batch process; store results in Amazon ElastiCache for Redis keyed by user_id; the API reads from Redis in <1ms and returns cached recommendations
Use AWS Global Accelerator to route recommendation requests to the nearest AWS region, reducing network latency to under 200ms
Use Amazon SageMaker Async Inference to process recommendation requests asynchronously; the API immediately acknowledges the request and clients poll for results
Increase the ML inference instance type to the largest available GPU instance to reduce computation time from 2 seconds to under 200ms
explain: Pre-computing recommendations and caching in ElastiCache Redis converts a 2-second synchronous ML computation into a sub-millisecond Redis GET operation. Since recommendations are valid for 10 minutes, a background process (Lambda scheduled every 10 minutes) regenerates recommendations for active users and updates Redis. The API reads from Redis and responds in <10ms — well within the 200ms SLA. This pattern (pre-computation + cache) is the standard solution for expensive computations with acceptable staleness. Global Accelerator reduces network latency by 10-20ms but doesn't address the 2-second ML computation. Even the largest GPU instance cannot compute complex recommendations in 200ms at scale. Async Inference doesn't help when the SLA requires a synchronous response.

q: A company uses Amazon CloudFront to serve a React single-page application from S3. When developers deploy a new version of the application, users still see the old version because CloudFront has cached the old HTML and JavaScript files. Cache invalidations are run manually but sometimes forgotten. Which automated solution ensures users always receive the latest application version after a deployment?
options: Set the CloudFront TTL for all objects to 0 seconds so CloudFront always fetches from the S3 origin; this ensures users always get the latest version

Configure the deployment pipeline to run a CloudFront invalidation (aws cloudfront create-invalidation --paths '/*') automatically after each successful S3 deployment; this clears the cache immediately after every deploy

Use versioned filenames for all assets (main.abc123.js) generated by the webpack build process; set long TTLs for versioned assets and no caching for index.html; webpack generates new filenames on each build automatically invalidating old cached assets
Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function that calls CloudFront CreateInvalidation whenever objects in the S3 bucket are updated
explain: Content-addressed (hash-based) filenames are the industry-standard solution for cache busting without invalidations. Webpack and modern build tools generate filenames containing a hash of file content (main.abc123.js). When code changes, the hash changes, producing a new filename. CloudFront has never seen this filename and fetches it fresh from origin. The entry point (index.html) is served with no-cache headers so browsers always fetch the latest HTML, which then references the new hashed asset filenames. Automated invalidations are simpler but cost $0.005 per 1,000 path invalidations and add pipeline complexity. TTL=0 eliminates caching entirely, losing CloudFront performance benefits. S3 Event Notifications + Lambda achieves automation but adds infrastructure overhead vs. build-time filename versioning.

q: A company runs a data pipeline that ingests CSV files from Amazon S3 using AWS Glue ETL jobs. Each job runs for 90 minutes processing 500GB of data. The Glue DPU utilization shows that workers are 15% utilized on average, with spikes to 40% during join operations. The company pays for 20 DPUs per job. Which optimization reduces cost without increasing job duration?
options: Switch from Glue ETL to Glue Streaming jobs which have lower per-DPU pricing
Use Glue Worker Type G.2X instead of G.1X to get more memory per DPU so fewer total DPUs are needed
Reduce the Glue job DPU allocation from 20 to 6 DPUs; the 15% average utilization indicates 20 DPUs is significantly over-provisioned; test with reduced DPUs and measure job duration impact
Enable Glue job bookmarks to skip already-processed files; this reduces the data processed per job run rather than the DPU allocation
explain: Average 15% DPU utilization with 20 DPUs means approximately 3 DPUs worth of work are being done on average. Even accounting for the 40% spike during joins (8 DPUs utilized), 20 DPUs is significantly over-provisioned. Reducing to 6 DPUs reduces cost by 70% while the job may run slightly longer (Glue distributes work across available workers). Testing validates the duration impact. Most ETL jobs are I/O bound rather than CPU bound — reducing DPUs often has minimal duration impact. Glue Streaming uses continuous resources at different pricing — not appropriate for batch jobs. Glue bookmarks reduce data processed (useful for incremental loads) but don't reduce DPU cost for the data currently being processed. G.2X doubles memory per DPU but costs 2x per DPU — only beneficial if the job is memory-constrained.

q: A startup builds a real-time bidding platform that must evaluate incoming ad bid requests in under 10ms. The evaluation requires looking up campaign budget data that is updated every 30 seconds. The platform receives 500,000 bid requests per second. The lookup data fits in 2GB of memory. Which architecture achieves sub-10ms lookup with this volume?
options: Load bid evaluation data into EC2 instance memory (in-process dictionary/hashmap) at startup and refresh from S3 or DynamoDB every 30 seconds; in-process lookups are nanoseconds, orders of magnitude faster than any network call
Use Amazon MemoryDB for Redis which provides durable, Redis-compatible storage with sub-millisecond reads suitable for financial bid data

Store bid evaluation data in Amazon ElastiCache for Redis; each bid evaluation server performs a GET operation against Redis for campaign budget data; Redis sub-millisecond responses keep total evaluation time under 10ms
Store bid data in Amazon DynamoDB with DAX; DAX provides sub-millisecond reads that keep total evaluation under 10ms at any scale
explain: For a 10ms SLA on a 500,000 RPS system, in-process memory lookups (nanosecond range) are the only approach that guarantees sufficient headroom. Network calls to Redis (0.5-2ms round-trip) or DAX consume a significant fraction of the 10ms budget before any computation occurs. Loading the 2GB dataset into each evaluation server's heap memory at startup and refreshing every 30 seconds from S3/DynamoDB eliminates network latency entirely for lookups. Redis is excellent for shared state across many servers but adds network latency. DAX is DynamoDB-specific and has higher latency than in-process memory. MemoryDB provides durability guarantees unnecessary for campaign budget data refreshed every 30 seconds.

q: A company's Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL cluster handles an e-commerce database with a 2TB dataset. Peak traffic is 5,000 transactions per second. The cluster has one writer and two reader instances. During the holiday season the team expects 3x normal traffic and wants to pre-scale before the event. Which Aurora feature allows pre-scaling without over-provisioning year-round?
options: Create an Aurora Global Database and add regions before the holiday season; traffic from additional regions reduces load on the primary region
Upgrade the Aurora writer instance to the largest available instance class before the holiday season; revert after

Configure Aurora Auto Scaling to use scheduled scaling actions: increase the minimum replica count to 5 before the holiday season and return to 2 after; Aurora Auto Scaling adds/removes read replicas within 5 minutes based on the schedule
Enable Aurora Serverless v2 for all instances; Aurora Serverless v2 automatically scales capacity instantly in response to traffic without pre-scheduling
explain: Aurora Serverless v2 scales database capacity in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU increments) within seconds in response to actual load — no pre-scheduling needed. Each Aurora Serverless v2 instance scales from minimum to maximum ACU configuration automatically. During the holiday spike Aurora Serverless v2 scales up to handle 3x traffic, then scales back down immediately after, paying only for consumed capacity. Scheduled Auto Scaling works but requires predicting the exact scaling need and managing schedules. Upsizing the writer increases write capacity but read scaling (reader instances) is the primary need during traffic spikes. Aurora Global Databases address multi-region latency, not single-region capacity scaling.

q: A company uses Amazon S3 to store 1 billion small objects (average 10KB each). An application makes 100,000 GetObject requests per second. S3 is rate-limiting some requests. The objects are accessed with no predictable pattern. Which approach increases S3 GET throughput beyond default limits?
options: Add random prefixes to object keys; S3 automatically scales to very high request rates per prefix but partitions by prefix; randomizing prefixes distributes requests across multiple S3 partitions multiplying available throughput
Enable S3 Requester Pays to shift bandwidth costs to requesters, which removes throttling applied to the bucket owner's account
Add Amazon CloudFront in front of S3 to cache objects at edge locations; even with low cache hit rates, CloudFront distributes request handling across the global edge network
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration to use the CloudFront network for GetObject requests, which provides higher aggregate throughput than direct S3 endpoints
explain: Amazon CloudFront distributes GET requests across hundreds of edge locations globally. Even with low cache hit rates, CloudFront's origin request distribution across the S3 endpoint pool dramatically increases effective throughput. CloudFront also benefits from S3's native performance at the origin while caching even unpredictable access patterns for a TTL period. Random prefixes help with S3's internal partitioning (S3 has improved prefix-based scaling and auto-partitions after sustained high rates) but S3 now scales to very high request rates per prefix automatically — random prefixes are less necessary with modern S3. Transfer Acceleration optimizes upload and download routing but doesn't distribute request handling across multiple endpoint pools the way CloudFront does. Requester Pays changes billing, not throughput limits.

q: A company runs a popular REST API that receives traffic in bursts. During burst periods the DynamoDB table behind the API is throttled because provisioned capacity is insufficient. The team wants to handle burst traffic without over-provisioning capacity for normal traffic. Which DynamoDB capacity mode is most appropriate?
options: Implement exponential backoff and retry logic in the application; throttled requests retry with delays ensuring all requests eventually succeed
Configure DynamoDB Auto Scaling with a target utilization of 70% and maximum capacity 10x the normal provisioned capacity; Auto Scaling increases capacity proactively before throttling occurs

Switch the DynamoDB table to on-demand capacity mode; DynamoDB instantly accommodates up to double the previous peak traffic and scales further within minutes without manual capacity management or throttling during bursts
Enable DynamoDB global tables to distribute read and write load across multiple regions reducing per-region request volume
explain: DynamoDB On-Demand capacity mode instantly handles workload spikes without provisioning decisions. The table scales to accommodate up to double the previous peak traffic instantaneously (no scale-out delay) and can scale further within minutes. There is no throttling during bursts — DynamoDB serves every request at the specified throughput. On-Demand eliminates capacity planning entirely. Auto Scaling with high maximum capacity reduces (but doesn't eliminate) throttling — scale-out takes minutes and may not respond fast enough for sudden bursts. Exponential backoff handles throttling but adds latency and doesn't increase throughput. Global Tables distribute geographic load but don't solve burst capacity problems within a single region.

q: A company needs to run complex financial risk calculations that require 10,000 CPU cores for 2 hours, once per week. The calculations are embarrassingly parallel with no inter-process communication. The company does not have on-premises HPC infrastructure. Which AWS solution provides 10,000 CPU cores on-demand with zero idle cost?
options: Use AWS Batch with Spot Instances; define a managed compute environment with maximum vCPUs of 40,000; Batch automatically provisions EC2 Spot Instances when jobs are submitted and terminates them when jobs complete; Spot pricing provides 60-90% discount over On-Demand
Use AWS ParallelCluster to create an HPC cluster on-demand; ParallelCluster provisions and tears down clusters for each weekly run
Use Amazon EC2 dedicated hosts to reserve physical servers for the weekly calculation run; dedicated hosts ensure resource availability
Deploy an Amazon EKS cluster with 10,000 nodes scaled by Cluster Autoscaler; submit the calculation as Kubernetes jobs
explain: AWS Batch with Spot Instances is purpose-built for this pattern: large-scale, time-bounded, parallel batch workloads. Batch provisions exactly the requested compute capacity when jobs are submitted, runs the workload, and terminates all instances when complete — zero idle cost. Spot Instances provide 60-90% cost savings over On-Demand. Embarrassingly parallel calculations (no inter-process communication) are ideal for Spot since instance interruptions only affect individual task nodes (not the entire calculation). Dedicated Hosts require advance reservation and hourly billing even when unused. EKS with Cluster Autoscaler works but adds Kubernetes overhead for a simple batch workload. ParallelCluster is excellent for tightly-coupled MPI workloads but is overkill for embarrassingly parallel jobs.

q: A company runs a critical order management application on Amazon EC2 instances in a single Availability Zone. The RTO is 15 minutes and the RPO is 1 hour. A recent AZ outage caused 45 minutes of downtime. The company needs to redesign the architecture to meet its RTO and RPO requirements. Which design achieves both targets at the lowest cost?
options: Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to continuously replicate EC2 instances to another AZ; failover launches pre-configured instances meeting the 15-minute RTO

Deploy EC2 instances across two Availability Zones behind an Application Load Balancer; use RDS Multi-AZ for the database; Auto Scaling maintains minimum capacity in each AZ so traffic automatically shifts to the surviving AZ within 1-2 minutes of an AZ failure
Create an Amazon EC2 AMI and store it in S3; when an AZ fails, launch new EC2 instances from the AMI in another AZ; this keeps standby costs to zero while meeting the 15-minute RTO
Deploy EC2 instances in a second AWS Region using pilot light architecture; keep only core database components running; scale out the application tier within 15 minutes when needed
explain: Multi-AZ Active-Active architecture with ALB meets the requirements at the lowest cost. ALB health checks detect AZ failure within 1-2 minutes and route all traffic to healthy instances in the surviving AZ — well within the 15-minute RTO.
