RDS Multi-AZ provides synchronous replication with zero RPO (no data loss). This approach runs continuously in both AZs, providing instant failover. AMI-based recovery takes 10-15 minutes for instance launch alone and may not reliably meet the 15-minute RTO. Pilot light in a second region meets RPO but incurs cross-region latency and higher costs. Elastic Disaster Recovery adds cost and complexity beyond what Multi-AZ provides.

q: A company's application uses Amazon SQS to decouple its order processing pipeline. A bug in the consumer Lambda function causes it to fail when processing orders containing international characters, throwing an exception and returning the message to the queue. The message is repeatedly retried and blocking other messages in the queue. The company wants failed messages to stop blocking processing and be preserved for investigation. Which SQS configuration addresses this?
options: Increase the SQS visibility timeout to 24 hours so the failed message is hidden long enough for the bug to be fixed and reprocessed

Set the SQS queue's maxReceiveCount to 3 and configure a Dead Letter Queue; after 3 failed processing attempts the message is automatically moved to the DLQ where it can be investigated without blocking other messages
Set the Lambda reserved concurrency to 1 so only one message is processed at a time, preventing the failed message from blocking parallel processing
Enable SQS FIFO queue message deduplication to prevent the same message from being processed more than once
explain: The SQS Dead Letter Queue with maxReceiveCount is the purpose-built solution for this scenario. After the configured number of failed receive attempts (3), SQS automatically moves the problematic message to the DLQ. The main queue continues processing other orders normally — no blocking occurs. The DLQ preserves the failed message for root cause analysis and reprocessing after the bug is fixed. Increasing visibility timeout hides the message temporarily but it re-appears when the timeout expires and continues blocking. Deduplication prevents duplicate messages in FIFO queues but doesn't handle processing failures. Single concurrency serializes processing but the failed message still blocks all subsequent messages.

q: A media company uses Amazon CloudFront to serve video content from an Amazon S3 origin. The S3 origin is in us-east-1. The company's business continuity plan requires that video delivery continues even if us-east-1 becomes completely unavailable. Videos are 1-10GB in size. Which architecture provides origin redundancy?
options: Configure CloudFront with an origin group containing two S3 bucket origins in different regions (us-east-1 and us-west-2); use S3 Cross-Region Replication to keep both buckets synchronized; CloudFront automatically failover to the secondary origin if the primary returns errors
Store all videos in S3 with versioning enabled; CloudFront automatically uses the latest version if a previous version is unavailable
Create a separate CloudFront distribution in us-west-2 and use Route 53 failover routing to switch to the backup distribution
Use CloudFront Origin Shield in us-west-2 as a regional cache; if the origin fails, Origin Shield serves cached content without hitting the origin
explain: CloudFront origin groups enable active-passive origin failover. S3 Cross-Region Replication continuously replicates objects from the primary bucket (us-east-1) to the secondary (us-west-2). The CloudFront origin group is configured with the primary origin and the failover origin. If CloudFront receives HTTP 5xx errors from the primary origin, it automatically retries the request against the secondary origin — all transparent to viewers. A separate CloudFront distribution with Route 53 failover works but DNS propagation adds latency. Origin Shield improves cache performance but serves cached content only for objects already cached — not new or uncached videos. S3 versioning is for data protection, not origin failover.

q: A company hosts a three-tier web application. The web tier runs on EC2 with Auto Scaling. The application tier also uses Auto Scaling. The database is Amazon Aurora MySQL with Multi-AZ. During an incident, a wrong database migration was deployed that corrupted production data. Backups exist but restoring the full database takes 4 hours, exceeding the acceptable RTO. Which feature enables faster recovery from data corruption?
options: Enable Aurora Multi-Master to have multiple write nodes; if one writer's data is corrupted, another writer has the correct data
Create Aurora read replicas; promote a read replica that was replicating from before the corruption occurred
Use Aurora Global Database to replicate to a secondary region; promote the secondary region as the primary after the corruption is detected in the primary region

Use Aurora Backtrack to rewind the database to a point in time before the corruption occurred without restoring a snapshot; Backtrack completes in seconds to minutes depending on how far back the rewind goes
explain: Aurora Backtrack rewinds the database cluster to any point within the backtrack window (up to 72 hours) without creating a snapshot or restoring a backup. Backtrack modifies the existing cluster in-place — the operation completes in seconds to minutes regardless of database size. This is orders of magnitude faster than snapshot restoration for data corruption recovery. Aurora Multi-Master provides write redundancy but all writers share the same storage — a corrupted data migration affects all writers simultaneously. Global Database replication is asynchronous and corrupted data replicates to the secondary region before detection. Read replicas apply the same corrupted changes from the writer — they contain the same corrupted data.

q: A financial services company processes payment transactions using a fleet of EC2 instances. The application is stateless and processes 500 transactions per second during business hours and near zero at night. The company wants to ensure availability during AZ failures while paying only for capacity needed at any given time. Which architecture achieves both requirements?
options: Reserve EC2 instances in three AZs with sufficient capacity for peak load; reserved instances ensure availability while the reservation discount reduces cost
Deploy Lambda functions triggered by the payment queue; Lambda scales automatically and has no AZ-specific provisioning requirements
Use EC2 On-Demand instances manually started and stopped based on business hours to control costs while keeping three-AZ deployment

Deploy EC2 instances with Auto Scaling across three Availability Zones with a minimum of 1 instance per AZ; configure scaling policies based on transaction volume; the ALB distributes traffic and automatically removes instances in failed AZs
explain: Multi-AZ Auto Scaling with minimum instances per AZ provides both fault tolerance and cost efficiency. During low-traffic periods Auto Scaling reduces to the minimum (1 per AZ = 3 total). During peak hours Auto Scaling adds instances in response to load. ALB cross-zone load balancing automatically excludes unhealthy AZ instances during failures. This delivers on-demand cost efficiency (pay per running instance) with AZ resilience. Reserved Instances provide a cost discount but reserve static capacity — you pay for idle overnight capacity. Manual start/stop is operational burden and error-prone. Lambda is valid for stateless workloads but financial transaction processing often has specific compliance requirements around execution environment.

q: A company runs a stateful application on Amazon EC2 that stores session state in local instance memory. Auto Scaling terminates instances during scale-in events causing active sessions to be lost. The company wants scale-in events to complete active sessions before the instance is terminated. Which Auto Scaling feature allows instances to complete in-flight work before termination?
options: Enable connection draining on the Application Load Balancer; the ALB stops sending new requests to the terminating instance and waits for existing connections to complete
Set the Auto Scaling group's Default Cooldown to a longer period so scale-in events happen less frequently giving sessions more time to complete naturally
Use EC2 Spot instances with interruption notices; the 2-minute notice allows the application to migrate sessions before the instance is reclaimed

Configure an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook for the EC2_INSTANCE_TERMINATING state; the lifecycle hook pauses termination for a configurable timeout (up to 48 hours); the application detects the terminating state and stops accepting new sessions, completing active sessions before sending a CONTINUE signal to proceed with termination
explain: Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks pause the instance termination process and notify the application (via EventBridge or SQS) that termination is pending. The application code detects the EC2_INSTANCE_TERMINATING state, stops accepting new sessions, and waits for active sessions to complete. When done, the application sends CompleteLifecycleAction with CONTINUE, and termination proceeds. The hook can pause up to 48 hours. ALB connection draining stops new requests but doesn't wait for application-level sessions that persist beyond HTTP connections. Default Cooldown reduces termination frequency but doesn't guarantee session completion during terminations that do occur. Spot interruption notices provide 2 minutes which may be insufficient for long sessions.

q: A company uses AWS Lambda for its order fulfillment workflow. Lambda functions write order status updates to Amazon DynamoDB. During a DynamoDB service disruption, Lambda function invocations fail and orders are lost. The company needs to ensure no order updates are lost during transient DynamoDB failures. Which architecture prevents data loss during DynamoDB outages?
options: Use DynamoDB Global Tables so if one region fails, the Lambda function writes to another region's replica automatically
Configure Lambda destinations for failure cases; failed invocations are sent to an SQS DLQ from which they can be reprocessed when DynamoDB recovers
Enable DynamoDB point-in-time recovery so lost data can be restored after an outage

Use SQS as a buffer between the Lambda trigger and DynamoDB writes; Lambda writes order updates to SQS first (guaranteed delivery); a separate Lambda polls SQS and writes to DynamoDB with retry logic; messages remain in SQS during DynamoDB outages and are processed when service recovers
explain: The SQS buffer pattern ensures zero data loss. Lambda receives the trigger, writes the order update to SQS (a durable message queue with at-least-once delivery), and acknowledges the trigger. A separate Lambda function with SQS as its event source attempts DynamoDB writes with exponential backoff retries. During a DynamoDB outage, messages accumulate safely in SQS (up to 14-day retention). When DynamoDB recovers, the consumer Lambda automatically processes the backlog. Lambda destinations capture failed invocations but the default behavior is asynchronous — if the Lambda itself fails, the invocation may be lost without proper DLQ configuration on the trigger. PITR restores data after an outage but cannot recover data that was never written. Global Tables require the application to explicitly route writes to the available region.

q: A startup uses Amazon Route 53 for DNS. Their primary application is in us-east-1. They want automatic failover to us-west-2 if the primary region becomes unhealthy. Route 53 health checks currently monitor the us-east-1 ALB endpoint. During a recent incident, Route 53 correctly detected the failure but DNS failover took 3 minutes while the TTL on the DNS record was 300 seconds. How can the failover time be reduced?
options: Use AWS Global Accelerator instead of Route 53; Global Accelerator uses anycast IP addresses that instantly reroute traffic without DNS propagation
Change the routing policy from Failover to Latency-based routing; latency-based routing automatically routes to the healthiest and closest region

Reduce the Route 53 DNS record TTL from 300 seconds to 60 seconds; clients will get the updated failover record faster as their cached TTL expires sooner; also set the Route 53 health check interval to 10 seconds (fast health check) to detect failures within 30 seconds
Enable Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall to block traffic to unhealthy endpoints faster than standard TTL expiration
explain: AWS Global Accelerator uses static anycast IP addresses that are globally announced. Traffic routing changes happen at the AWS network level (not DNS), taking effect within seconds rather than waiting for DNS TTL expiration and client cache refresh. When the us-east-1 endpoint fails Global Accelerator's health checks, traffic is instantly rerouted to us-west-2 with no DNS propagation delay. This achieves near-zero failover time. Reducing TTL to 60 seconds improves DNS failover time to ~60 seconds minimum but doesn't eliminate TTL propagation delay — clients cache the old record for the full TTL duration. Latency-based routing routes by latency, not purely by health. DNS Firewall controls DNS query resolution, not failover routing.

q: A company stores important documents in Amazon S3. A compliance requirement mandates that documents cannot be deleted or overwritten for 7 years after creation. Even administrators must not be able to delete the files during the retention period. Which S3 feature enforces this requirement?
options: Enable S3 Object Lock in compliance mode with a 7-year retention period; once applied, no user (including root) can delete or overwrite the object before the retention period expires
Apply a bucket policy that denies s3:DeleteObject for all principals; administrators cannot bypass a Deny in a bucket policy
Enable MFA Delete on the versioned bucket; deleting object versions requires MFA authentication which provides an additional protection layer
Enable S3 Versioning on the bucket; with versioning enabled, deleted objects become delete markers and original versions are preserved indefinitely
explain: S3 Object Lock compliance mode creates a WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection that cannot be overridden by any user, including the root account and AWS Support. The retention period is immutable — it can be extended but not shortened. This directly satisfies the compliance requirement that even administrators cannot delete files during the 7-year period. S3 Versioning preserves versions but administrators can still delete all versions with sufficient permissions. Bucket policies denying DeleteObject can be modified by administrators with s3:PutBucketPolicy permission. MFA Delete requires MFA for version deletion but the root user with MFA could still delete — it doesn't prevent all deletions.

q: A company runs a batch data processing workload on Amazon EC2 that takes 4 hours to complete. The workload is fault-tolerant and can be restarted if interrupted. The company runs this workload daily and wants to reduce EC2 costs. Which pricing model provides the greatest cost reduction for this workload?
options: Use On-Demand instances with detailed billing and analyze Cost Explorer to find optimization opportunities after 1 month of usage
Use Savings Plans with a 1-year commitment for compute capacity matching the daily peak usage
Purchase 1-year All Upfront Standard Reserved Instances; reserved instances provide 40% discount and guarantee capacity availability

Use EC2 Spot Instances for the batch compute fleet; Spot Instances provide up to 90% discount over On-Demand pricing; the workload's fault tolerance means Spot interruptions can be handled gracefully with checkpoint/restart logic
explain: Spot Instances provide up to 90% savings over On-Demand and are ideal for fault-tolerant batch workloads. A 4-hour batch job with checkpoint/restart capability can recover from Spot interruptions by restarting from the last checkpoint rather than the beginning. The 2-minute interruption notice allows graceful state saving. This is the maximum available discount. Standard Reserved Instances provide 40-60% discount but require 1-year commitment and don't account for batch workloads that may use different instance types. On-Demand with Cost Explorer analysis delays savings by weeks. Savings Plans provide flexibility but typically 20-60% discount — less than Spot for fault-tolerant batch.

q: A startup runs a web application that receives heavy traffic during US business hours (9 AM - 5 PM EST) and minimal traffic overnight. The application tier uses an Auto Scaling group with On-Demand EC2 instances. Minimum capacity is 10 instances (handles overnight traffic) and it scales up to 30 instances during peak hours. The company wants to reduce EC2 costs without changing availability. Which strategy reduces costs most effectively?
options: Use Savings Plans for all capacity with a commitment level matching average daily usage rather than peak usage

Purchase Reserved Instances for the base 10 instances (always running); use On-Demand or Spot Instances for the additional 20 instances needed during peak hours; reserved instances at the steady-state level provide maximum savings on committed capacity
Configure scheduled scaling to reduce minimum capacity to 2 instances overnight; save On-Demand costs by running only the minimum required instances
Use Spot Instances for all 30 instances including base capacity; configure Auto Scaling with multiple instance types and AZs to minimize Spot interruption impact
explain: Reserving capacity for the always-running baseline (10 instances) maximizes savings on committed workload — Reserved Instances provide 40-72% discount for predictable, steady-state usage. The additional 20 instances for peak hours are covered by On-Demand (flexibility) or Spot (maximum savings if fault-tolerant). This layered pricing model is the standard AWS cost optimization pattern: reserve the base, flex the spike. Using Spot for base capacity risks availability when Spot pools are scarce. Scheduled scaling to 2 instances overnight risks performance if traffic is higher than expected. Savings Plans at average usage commit to less than the base load, leaving base instances billed at On-Demand rates.

q: A company stores 500TB of log files in Amazon S3 Standard. Logs older than 30 days are rarely accessed (fewer than 5 accesses per month). Logs older than 1 year are never accessed but must be retained for 3 years for compliance. The company wants to minimize storage costs while meeting access requirements. Which S3 Lifecycle configuration achieves this?
options: Transition logs to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days (minimum 30-day requirement in Standard met); transition to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 365 days; expire objects after 1095 days (3 years); this reduces cost progressively as data ages
Transition logs directly to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 30 days for maximum storage savings
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all logs; Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between tiers based on access patterns without retrieval fees
Use S3 Replication to copy old logs to a cheaper bucket and delete from the original bucket
explain: A tiered lifecycle policy minimizes cost at each data age stage. S3 Standard-IA ($0.0125/GB vs $0.023/GB) serves the 30-90 day window where occasional access still occurs and retrieval fees are acceptable. After 1 year (365 days) with no access, S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval ($0.004/GB) provides archival pricing while retrieval in hours is acceptable for compliance access. Objects expire automatically after 3 years. Direct Glacier after 30 days incurs retrieval fees for any of the occasional accesses in the 30-365 day window. Intelligent-Tiering adds per-object monitoring fees ($0.0025/1,000 objects/month) which at 500TB of small log files can exceed storage savings. Replication to a cheaper bucket requires object duplication and deletion — no benefit over lifecycle policies.

q: A company runs a development environment with 20 EC2 instances for developers. The instances are only used during business hours (8 AM - 6 PM) Monday through Friday. Instances run 24/7 because developers forgot to stop them. The company wants to automatically stop instances after business hours and start them before business hours without developer intervention. Which solution implements this with the least operational overhead?
options: Write a Lambda function that calls ec2:StopInstances and ec2:StartInstances; schedule it using CloudWatch Events (EventBridge) cron expressions

Use AWS Instance Scheduler (available in AWS Solutions Library); deploy it in the account, tag EC2 instances with the schedule, and configure the scheduler to stop/start instances based on the tag; the scheduler runs as a Lambda function on EventBridge schedule
Use AWS Systems Manager Maintenance Windows to stop EC2 instances at 6 PM and start them at 8 AM on weekdays
Create two EventBridge scheduled rules: one at 8 AM Monday-Friday that runs an SSM Automation runbook to start tagged instances, and one at 6 PM to stop them
explain: AWS Instance Scheduler is a pre-built AWS solution that handles complex scheduling scenarios including multiple time zones, multiple accounts, and multiple regions. It deploys a Lambda function and DynamoDB configuration table via CloudFormation. Developers tag instances with a schedule name and the scheduler handles start/stop automatically. Running 24/7 vs 10 hours/day on weekdays reduces runtime from 168 to 50 hours/week — 70% cost reduction. EventBridge + SSM Automation works but requires building and maintaining the automation runbook. SSM Maintenance Windows are designed for patching, not instance scheduling. Custom Lambda with EventBridge also works but requires writing, testing, and maintaining code for edge cases (holidays, timezone changes).

q: A company runs an Amazon ECS cluster on EC2 launch type with 50 container instances. Container utilization averages 30% CPU and 25% memory across the cluster. The company is paying for heavily underutilized EC2 capacity. They want to right-size the compute without managing EC2 instances. Which migration achieves the greatest operational and cost improvement?
options: Right-size the EC2 instances from the current instance type to a smaller instance type based on the 30% CPU utilization metric
Enable EC2 Auto Scaling to reduce the cluster size when containers are underutilized and increase when utilization is high

Migrate ECS tasks from EC2 launch type to AWS Fargate launch type; Fargate charges per vCPU and memory per second per task rather than per EC2 instance; right-sizing task CPU/memory definitions eliminates EC2 cluster management and pays only for actual task resource consumption
Move to Amazon EKS on EC2 with Kubernetes node auto-provisioning to better pack containers onto fewer nodes
explain: Migrating to Fargate eliminates EC2 cluster management (patching, scaling, capacity planning) and charges only for the exact vCPU and memory allocated to each running task, billed per second. At 30% CPU utilization on EC2, Fargate's per-task billing eliminates the ~70% idle resource cost. No EC2 instances to manage, patch, or right-size. Fargate Spot provides additional savings for fault-tolerant tasks. Right-sizing EC2 instances reduces waste but maintains operational overhead of cluster management. EC2 Auto Scaling improves utilization but still requires managing EC2 instances. EKS adds Kubernetes complexity without necessarily improving cost or operations over well-configured ECS.

q: A company queries their Amazon Redshift data warehouse with hundreds of complex analytical queries daily. The cluster runs continuously but utilization drops to near zero between midnight and 6 AM. The company wants to eliminate the cost of the idle cluster overnight without losing data or requiring manual intervention. Which feature enables automated cluster suspension?
options: Migrate to Amazon Athena for all analytical queries; Athena is serverless and charges only per TB scanned with no cluster cost
Enable Redshift concurrency scaling; idle clusters automatically reduce their scale-out capacity during low usage periods

Enable Amazon Redshift Serverless; Redshift Serverless automatically pauses when there are no queries and resumes within seconds when queries are submitted; the company pays only for compute used during actual query execution

Use Redshift cluster pause and resume via a scheduled EventBridge rule; pause the cluster at midnight and resume at 6 AM; cluster data is preserved on storage during pause and billing stops for compute
explain: Redshift cluster pause/resume is the correct solution for existing Redshift clusters. When paused, Redshift billing stops for compute nodes while the cluster data remains on storage (storage costs continue). The cluster resumes within 2-5 minutes when needed. An EventBridge cron rule automates pause at midnight and resume at 6 AM. Redshift Serverless is an alternative but requires migrating from the provisioned cluster and may have different performance characteristics. Pausing the existing provisioned cluster preserves all schemas, data, users, and permissions without migration. Athena requires migrating data format and rewriting all queries. Redshift Concurrency Scaling adds temporary capacity during peaks — it doesn't reduce idle cluster costs.

q: A company has multiple AWS accounts in an AWS Organization. The security team needs to ensure that no IAM user in any account can disable AWS CloudTrail or delete CloudTrail logs. Account administrators must retain the ability to manage all other IAM permissions. Which control enforces this restriction across all accounts?
options: Create a Service Control Policy (SCP) that denies cloudtrail:StopLogging, cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, cloudtrail:UpdateTrail, and s3:DeleteObject on the CloudTrail log bucket; attach the SCP to the root of the organization or to all member OUs
Use AWS Organizations delegated administrator for CloudTrail; the delegated administrator manages CloudTrail and prevents member accounts from modifying it
Enable AWS Config with the cloudtrail-enabled managed rule in all accounts; Config automatically re-enables CloudTrail if it is disabled
Create an IAM policy in each account that denies CloudTrail management actions; attach the policy to all IAM users and roles in each account
explain: SCPs attached at the organization root or OU level apply to all principals in all member accounts — including account administrators. Even an IAM principal with full AdministratorAccess cannot perform actions explicitly denied by an SCP. Denying cloudtrail:StopLogging and cloudtrail:DeleteTrail prevents disabling logging. Denying s3:DeleteObject on the CloudTrail bucket preserves log integrity. Account administrators retain all other IAM management capabilities. IAM policies in each account can be modified by account administrators — no central enforcement. Config with cloudtrail-enabled detects and remediates after the fact but doesn't prevent disabling. CloudTrail delegated administrator centralizes management but doesn't enforce restrictions through SCPs.

q: A healthcare company stores patient records in Amazon S3. The records are encrypted with AWS KMS using a Customer Managed Key (CMK). A compliance audit requires that all encryption key usage be logged and that the company can prove no unauthorized entity accessed patient data. Which configuration provides this audit capability?
options: Configure KMS key policy to require MFA for all Decrypt operations; the MFA requirement creates an audit trail of authenticated key usage
Use Amazon Macie to scan the S3 bucket for sensitive data access patterns and generate findings when patient records are accessed by unusual principals
Enable S3 server access logging to record all requests to the bucket including the requester identity and the objects accessed

Enable AWS CloudTrail with data events for the S3 bucket and management events for AWS KMS; CloudTrail logs every S3 GetObject (including who accessed it and when) and every KMS Decrypt operation (including which key was used and which principal called it)
explain: Enabling CloudTrail with both S3 data events and KMS management events creates the most comprehensive audit trail. S3 data events log every GetObject, PutObject, and DeleteObject request with the requester's ARN, timestamp, source IP, and request ID. KMS management events log every Encrypt, Decrypt, and GenerateDataKey call including which CMK was used and which IAM principal made the request. Together these provide irrefutable evidence of who accessed patient records and whether the encryption key was used legitimately. S3 server access logging records requests but doesn't capture KMS key usage for encryption. Macie identifies sensitive data and unusual patterns but doesn't create a complete access audit log. KMS MFA requirement adds authentication but doesn't create an audit trail beyond what CloudTrail provides.

q: A company runs a web application on Amazon EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer. The application has been experiencing SQL injection and cross-site scripting attacks. The security team wants to block these attacks without modifying application code. Which service provides this protection?
options: Use AWS Shield Advanced on the ALB; Shield Advanced provides protection against SQL injection and application-layer attacks with DDoS mitigation
Enable Amazon GuardDuty on the account; GuardDuty detects SQL injection attempts in VPC Flow Logs and blocks malicious IPs

Associate AWS WAF with the Application Load Balancer; configure WAF with the AWS Managed Rules for Common Vulnerabilities rule group which includes rules detecting SQL injection and XSS patterns; WAF evaluates each HTTP request before it reaches the application
Configure Security Groups on the EC2 instances to block HTTP requests containing SQL keywords in the request body
explain: AWS WAF is the purpose-built web application firewall for HTTP/HTTPS traffic inspection. WAF rules inspect request components (headers, body, query strings, URIs) against patterns including SQL injection and XSS signatures. The AWS Managed Rules for Known Bad Inputs and Core Rule Set provide pre-built protection maintained by AWS security researchers. WAF integrates directly with ALB with no application code changes required. GuardDuty analyzes network flow logs and CloudTrail for threat detection — it doesn't inspect individual HTTP request payloads or block requests in real time. Shield Advanced provides DDoS protection, not application-layer attack blocking. Security Groups operate at network layer (IP/port) — they cannot inspect HTTP request bodies for SQL injection patterns.

q: A company's AWS Lambda functions need to access a third-party API using an API key. Currently the API key is stored as an environment variable in the Lambda configuration. A security audit flags this as a risk because the key is visible in plaintext in the Lambda console. The company wants to store the API key securely and rotate it every 90 days. Which solution addresses both security and rotation requirements?
options: Encrypt the API key using KMS before storing it in the Lambda environment variable; the Lambda function decrypts it at runtime; rotate the key by re-encrypting with a new value every 90 days
Use AWS Certificate Manager to store the API key as a certificate; Lambda retrieves it using the ACM API; ACM automatically rotates certificates on the configured schedule
Store the API key in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store as a SecureString (encrypted with KMS); schedule a Lambda function to update the parameter every 90 days with a new key

Store the API key in AWS Secrets Manager; configure a Lambda rotation function on a 90-day schedule; in the Lambda function code, call Secrets Manager GetSecretValue at runtime to retrieve the current API key; the function always uses the current rotated key
explain: AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built for storing and rotating API keys, credentials, and other secrets. Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation via Lambda rotation functions on configurable schedules. Lambda functions retrieve the current secret value at runtime — after rotation, all functions automatically use the new key without any deployment. The secret is encrypted at rest with KMS and never exposed in the Lambda configuration. SSM Parameter Store SecureString works for storage and KMS encryption but requires custom rotation logic (scheduled Lambda) and doesn't have built-in rotation orchestration. Encrypting the environment variable still stores the ciphertext in Lambda configuration and requires custom rotation and re-deployment. ACM is for TLS certificates, not API keys.

q: A company needs to grant a third-party auditing company temporary access to their AWS account to review specific S3 buckets and CloudTrail logs. The access should expire after 30 days, require MFA, and not require creating IAM users in the company's account. Which access method is most secure and appropriate?
options: Use AWS Resource Access Manager to share the S3 buckets with the auditing company's AWS account
Generate pre-signed S3 URLs for the specific S3 objects valid for 30 days; send the URLs to the auditing company
Create a temporary IAM user with the required permissions and an access key; delete the user after 30 days

Create an IAM role with the required permissions and a trust policy allowing the auditing company's AWS account to assume it; add a condition requiring MFA; share the role ARN with the auditing company; they assume the role from their own account using their own credentials; remove the trust policy after 30 days
explain: IAM cross-account role assumption is the correct and most secure method for third-party access. The trust policy allows the auditing company's AWS account ID to assume the role, and a Condition requiring aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent:true enforces MFA. The auditing company uses their own IAM users/roles to assume the cross-account role — no IAM users are created in the company's account. Access is revoked by removing the trust policy or deleting the role. Temporary IAM users require managing long-term credentials and creating accounts in the company's AWS account. Presigned S3 URLs provide object access but not CloudTrail log access and don't enforce MFA. RAM sharing is for sharing resources within organizations, not for third-party cross-account access with MFA.

q: A company uses Amazon VPC with private subnets for their application servers. The application servers need to download software packages from the internet for updates but should not be directly accessible from the internet. The company wants a managed solution that doesn't require maintaining EC2 instances. Which solution allows private instances to initiate outbound internet connections?
options: Deploy an AWS managed NAT Gateway in a public subnet; update the private subnet route table with a route to 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the NAT Gateway; NAT Gateway allows outbound internet traffic from private instances while blocking unsolicited inbound connections
Configure an Internet Gateway and attach it to the VPC; update the private subnet route tables to route traffic through the Internet Gateway
Deploy a NAT Instance (EC2) in the public subnet; configure the private subnet route table to route internet traffic through the NAT Instance
Use AWS PrivateLink to connect the private instances to internet services through VPC endpoints
explain: AWS Managed NAT Gateway is the correct solution for outbound internet access from private subnets without managing EC2 instances. NAT Gateway is a fully managed, highly available service that performs network address translation for outbound traffic. It allows instances in private subnets to initiate connections to the internet while preventing inbound connections from the internet to private IP addresses. NAT Instances (EC2) require managing OS patching, scaling, high availability, and security groups — operational overhead the question wants to avoid. An Internet Gateway attached with a route in private subnets would make private instances publicly routable — they would be directly accessible from the internet. AWS PrivateLink creates private connectivity to specific AWS services, not general internet access.

q: A company stores sensitive customer PII in Amazon DynamoDB. A compliance requirement mandates that PII fields (SSN, credit card numbers, date of birth) must be encrypted at the field level, not just at rest. The encryption must use customer-managed keys and the company must be able to revoke access to specific fields independently. Which approach implements field-level encryption in DynamoDB?
options: Use DynamoDB Streams to detect writes containing PII; trigger a Lambda function to re-encrypt PII fields with customer-managed keys and update the DynamoDB item
Enable AWS Macie on the DynamoDB table to identify and encrypt PII fields using customer-managed KMS keys automatically

Implement client-side encryption in the application layer using the AWS Encryption SDK; encrypt each sensitive field with a separate KMS CMK before writing to DynamoDB; the application decrypts fields using the corresponding CMK when reading; revoking a CMK immediately makes the corresponding field unreadable
Enable DynamoDB encryption at rest with a customer-managed KMS CMK; this encrypts all data including PII fields with the customer's key
explain: The AWS Encryption SDK provides client-side field-level encryption where each sensitive field can be encrypted with a different KMS CMK before being stored in DynamoDB. The encrypted ciphertext is stored in DynamoDB — even AWS cannot read the plaintext. Using separate CMKs per field type (one for SSN, one for credit card numbers, one for date of birth) allows independent revocation: revoking the SSN CMK makes all SSN fields unreadable without affecting other fields. DynamoDB encryption at rest encrypts the entire table with one key — you cannot independently revoke access to specific fields. DynamoDB Streams with Lambda adds latency and complexity; the data is briefly in DynamoDB unencrypted. Macie discovers PII but does not encrypt it.

q: A company's application generates presigned S3 URLs for users to download their documents. The security team discovers that some users are sharing presigned URLs with unauthorized parties who download the documents. The company wants to restrict presigned URL usage to only the original requesting user's IP address. Which solution implements this restriction?
options: Use S3 Object Lambda to validate the requester's identity before serving the document when a presigned URL is accessed

Generate presigned URLs with a bucket policy condition that restricts access to the requesting user's IP address using the aws:SourceIp condition key; include the user's IP in the presigned URL generation and enforce it via the bucket policy
Enable S3 Block Public Access on the bucket to prevent presigned URLs from being used by any external party
Reduce the presigned URL expiration time to 5 minutes so shared URLs expire before unauthorized users can use them
explain: S3 presigned URLs honor bucket policy conditions. A bucket policy with an Allow statement including a Condition for aws:SourceIp matching the user's IP address means the presigned URL only works from that IP. When generating the presigned URL, the application retrieves the user's IP from the HTTP request and creates a bucket policy condition scoped to that IP. This prevents IP-mismatched access even if the URL is shared. Short expiration reduces the window but doesn't prevent sharing within the expiration window. S3 Object Lambda intercepts requests but adding custom validation requires serverless infrastructure. Block Public Access prevents public access controls but presigned URLs use IAM authentication, not public access — Block Public Access doesn't restrict presigned URL usage.

q: A company runs a multi-tier application where the web tier communicates with the application tier and the application tier communicates with the database tier. All tiers run in separate subnets within the same VPC. The security team wants to enforce that only the web tier can communicate with the application tier and only the application tier can communicate with the database. Which security mechanism enforces this at the network level?
options: Configure Security Groups with least privilege rules: the application tier security group allows inbound only from the web tier's security group (not IP ranges); the database tier security group allows inbound only from the application tier's security group; this automatically updates as instances scale
Use Network ACLs for each subnet; create NACL rules that allow only specific CIDR blocks for each tier; NACLs provide stateless packet filtering at the subnet boundary
Create VPC route tables that only route traffic between adjacent tiers; block routing between non-adjacent tiers at the VPC routing level
Use AWS Network Firewall between subnets with rules allowing only specific tier-to-tier communication patterns
explain: Security Groups with source security group references are the correct mechanism for tier-to-tier isolation. Instead of allowing CIDR blocks (which can change as instances scale), the application tier's security group allows inbound from the web tier's security group ID. Any instance with the web tier security group can communicate — new web tier instances automatically have access without rule updates. The database security group only allows inbound from the application tier's security group. This is dynamic, scales automatically, and provides least-privilege access. NACLs with CIDR blocks require manual updates when IP ranges change and don't scale elegantly with Auto Scaling. Route tables control routing paths, not application-layer communication between subnets in the same VPC. Network Firewall adds cost and complexity beyond what security groups provide for this use case.

q: A company uses AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) to manage access to 50 AWS accounts. Each account has multiple permission sets (ReadOnly, Developer, Administrator). The company wants to ensure that a user who is absent for more than 90 days has their access automatically suspended without manual intervention. Which approach enforces this policy?
options: Set IAM Identity Center session duration to 90 days; sessions expire and users must re-authenticate; if they don't log in within 90 days they are effectively locked out
Use AWS Config with a custom rule that detects IAM Identity Center users inactive for 90 days and runs an SSM Automation to disable them
Create a Lambda function scheduled by EventBridge that calls IAM Identity Center APIs to check last login date and disable inactive users

Integrate IAM Identity Center with an external identity provider (Active Directory or Okta); configure the IdP to automatically disable accounts after 90 days of inactivity; IAM Identity Center synchronizes user status from the IdP — disabled IdP users cannot authenticate to any AWS account
explain: IAM Identity Center's most effective access lifecycle management comes from integrating with an enterprise IdP (Active Directory, Okta, Azure AD) using SCIM provisioning. When the IdP disables a user account (manually or through an automated inactivity policy), SCIM propagation updates IAM Identity Center within minutes — the user loses all AWS access across all 50 accounts simultaneously. The inactivity detection logic lives in the IdP which typically has mature user lifecycle management features. A custom Lambda function requires building and maintaining inactivity detection logic and IAM Identity Center API integration. Session duration expiration requires users to actively log in — it doesn't detect true inactivity. Config custom rules add complexity for what is fundamentally an identity lifecycle management problem.
